whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Boot is only treated as a dividend to the extent of earnings and profits and if it has the effect of a dividend distribution. It's not automatic.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The entire exchange does not become taxable. Gain is only recognized to the extent of the boot received, not the value of the stock.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Section 351(b) dictates that if a transferor receives boot (property other than stock) in an otherwise qualifying Section 351 exchange, gain is recognized, but not in excess of the amount of money and the fair market value of such other property received. The character of the gain (capital or ordinary) depends on the nature of the asset transferred.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Gain is recognized in a 351 exchange when boot is received. The basis is affected, but gain is recognized immediately, up to the boot amount.

questions.educational: Section 351 allows for the tax-free transfer of property to a corporation in exchange for stock, preventing double taxation on the same economic gain. However, the introduction of boot disrupts this tax-free nature, as the shareholder is receiving something of value beyond the corporation's stock. The gain recognized is limited to the realized gain to prevent taxing amounts exceeding the actual economic benefit received.
questions.examTip: Remember that the *lesser* of the realized gain or the amount of boot received is the amount of gain recognized in a Section 351 exchange. Pay close attention to the facts provided in the question to determine both the realized gain and the boot received.
questions.memoryAid: Boot = Gain (up to realized gain). Think of 'boot' as extra value received, triggering immediate tax consequences.
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is assuming that any receipt of boot in a Section 351 exchange automatically makes the entire exchange taxable. While boot triggers gain recognition, it's only to the *extent* of the boot received. Some test-takers may also incorrectly believe that boot is always treated as a dividend.

questions.question: Sarah owns 60% of the stock in Beta Corp. She is considering redeeming some of her shares. For the redemption to be treated as a sale of stock rather than a dividend, it must meet certain requirements. Which of the following conditions would qualify the redemption as a sale?
options: Is substantially disproportionate or completely terminates the shareholder's interest
Is pro-rata among all shareholders, maintaining their percentage ownership and control.
Exceeds $25,000 and is reported as a capital gain on the shareholder's individual income tax return.
Occurs within 2 years of incorporation, allowing for adjustments to initial capital contributions.
questions.explanation: A redemption receives sale treatment (capital gain) if it is substantially disproportionate, completely terminates the shareholder's interest, or is not essentially equivalent to a dividend. Attribution rules apply.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A stock redemption is treated as a sale (resulting in capital gain or loss) if it qualifies under specific exceptions to the general rule that redemptions are treated as dividends. Two key exceptions are a substantially disproportionate redemption under IRC Section 302(b)(2) or a complete termination of the shareholder's interest under IRC Section 302(b)(3).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Pro-rata redemptions are generally treated as dividends because the shareholder's ownership interest doesn't change. A sale requires a meaningful reduction in ownership.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The dollar amount of the redemption is not a determining factor in whether it's treated as a sale or a dividend. The key is the change in ownership percentage.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The timing of the redemption relative to incorporation is not a factor in determining whether it is treated as a sale or a dividend. The focus is on the shareholder's ownership before and after the redemption.

questions.educational: Corporate distributions can be treated as either dividends or capital gains, impacting the shareholder's tax liability. The distinction hinges on whether the distribution fundamentally alters the shareholder's ownership interest in the corporation. Rules like the substantially disproportionate redemption and complete termination of interest are designed to ensure that only redemptions that truly resemble a sale of stock qualify for capital gains treatment, preventing shareholders from disguising dividends as capital gains.
questions.examTip: Remember to consider the attribution rules when determining if a redemption qualifies as substantially disproportionate or a complete termination of interest; family members' stock ownership can be attributed to the redeeming shareholder, potentially disqualifying the redemption from sale treatment.
questions.memoryAid: STaR: Sale Treatment if redemption is Substantially disproportionate or Terminates interest.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the rules for stock redemptions and dividend distributions. The exam exploits the misconception that any redemption is treated as a sale of stock. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that a pro-rata redemption qualifies as a sale or that any redemption exceeding a certain dollar amount automatically qualifies.

questions.question: Green Valley Ventures, a partnership, has been operating for several years. Due to changes in ownership, there's a question about whether a technical termination occurred. Under current law, a partnership technical termination:
options: Occurs when 50% or more of the total interests in partnership capital and profits are sold or exchanged within a 12-month period, triggering a deemed liquidation.
Occurs when the partnership reports no gross income for two consecutive tax years and demonstrates no reasonable expectation of future profitability.
Requires a formal dissolution of the partnership under the specific provisions of the relevant state's Uniform Partnership Act, as well as federal tax law.
Was repealed by the TCJA; partnerships no longer terminate due to sales of interests
questions.explanation: The TCJA repealed the technical termination rule effective for tax years after 2017. Previously, a partnership terminated if 50% or more of interests were sold within 12 months. Now, such sales do not cause termination.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While this was the rule before TCJA, the TCJA repealed technical terminations. The sale of partnership interests no longer automatically triggers a termination.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Lack of income for two years is not a condition for technical termination, even before the TCJA. Technical termination was based on the sale or exchange of partnership interests.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Dissolution under state law and technical termination for tax purposes are separate concepts. A partnership can dissolve under state law without a technical termination, and vice versa.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) repealed the technical termination rule of IRC Section 708(b)(1)(B) for partnership tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. Therefore, sales or exchanges of 50% or more of the total interests in partnership capital and profits within a 12-month period no longer trigger a technical termination.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation aims to reflect the economic reality of the business. The technical termination rule, prior to its repeal, was intended to simplify partnership accounting after a major shift in ownership. However, it often created unnecessary administrative burdens and unintended tax consequences, leading to its repeal by the TCJA. Understanding partnership formation, operation, and termination is crucial for advising clients on the most tax-efficient business structure.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, always be mindful of the effective dates of tax law changes. The TCJA made significant alterations to partnership taxation, so knowing what rules are still in effect is essential.
questions.memoryAid: TCJA 'Terminated' Technical Terminations.
questions.commonMistake: The exam exploits the misconception that the technical termination rules for partnerships still apply. Many candidates are unaware that the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) repealed the technical termination rule based on the sale or exchange of 50% or more of the total interest in partnership capital and profits within a 12-month period. Test-takers often incorrectly recall the old rule or confuse it with state law dissolution requirements.

questions.question: John is a partner in ABC Partnership. He is trying to understand his tax basis related to the partnership. A partner's "outside basis" refers to:
options: The partnership's basis in assets held outside the U.S.
The partnership's basis in its assets
The FMV of the partnership interest
The partner's basis in their partnership interest
questions.explanation: Outside basis is the partner's tax basis in their partnership interest. Inside basis refers to the partnership's basis in its assets. These may differ due to contributions, distributions, or Section 754 elections.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The term 'outside basis' specifically relates to a partner's individual basis in their partnership interest, not the partnership's basis in assets, regardless of their location.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The partnership's basis in its assets is referred to as the 'inside basis.' The 'outside basis' is a partner-level concept, reflecting their individual investment and share of partnership activities, while 'inside basis' is a partnership-level concept.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While fair market value (FMV) is relevant in many tax contexts, the 'outside basis' is specifically the *tax basis*, not the FMV, of the partnership interest. The FMV might be used to determine the amount realized on a sale, but it's distinct from the basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - A partner's 'outside basis' represents their tax basis in their partnership interest. This basis is crucial for determining gain or loss upon the sale of the partnership interest and for calculating deductible losses. It is adjusted for contributions, distributions, and the partner's share of partnership income and losses, as per IRC Section 705.

questions.educational: A partner's outside basis is essential for determining the tax consequences of partnership operations and transactions. It reflects the partner's investment in the partnership and is adjusted annually to reflect their share of income, losses, contributions, and distributions. Understanding outside basis is crucial for accurately calculating gain or loss on the sale of a partnership interest and for determining the deductibility of partnership losses, which are limited to the partner's basis.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that a partner's outside basis is tracked separately from the partnership's inside basis and is crucial for determining the partner's individual tax liability related to the partnership.
questions.memoryAid: Outside = Owner's basis; Inside = Entity's basis
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse 'outside basis' with 'inside basis.' The exam tests whether you understand that outside basis represents a partner's basis *in their partnership interest*, while inside basis refers to the partnership's basis in its assets. Test-takers often incorrectly associate 'outside' with assets held outside the U.S. or the FMV of the partnership interest.

questions.question: Sunrise Cafe employs 20 full-time equivalent employees and pays average wages of $45,000 per employee. They provide health insurance coverage. Is Sunrise Cafe eligible for the Small Business Health Care Tax Credit? The Small Business Health Care Tax Credit is available to employers with:
options: Fewer than 25 full-time equivalent employees and average wages below threshold
Only self-employed individuals with no other employees are eligible for the credit.
Up to 500 employees, provided they are all covered under the same health insurance plan.
Any number of employees is eligible if they offer a qualified health insurance plan through the SHOP Marketplace.
questions.explanation: The Small Business Health Care Tax Credit is for small employers with fewer than 25 FTE employees earning less than $59,000 average wages (2024, indexed). Maximum credit is 50% of premiums (35% for tax-exempt employers).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The Small Business Health Care Tax Credit is specifically designed for small employers. To qualify, an employer must have fewer than 25 full-time equivalent (FTE) employees and pay average annual wages below a certain threshold, which is indexed annually. This option accurately reflects these key requirements.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The credit is for small businesses with employees, not solely for self-employed individuals.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The employee limit is significantly lower than 500 for the Small Business Health Care Tax Credit.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While offering insurance is a requirement, there are employee number and wage limitations that Sunrise Cafe exceeds.

questions.educational: The Small Business Health Care Tax Credit incentivizes small businesses to provide health insurance coverage to their employees. This credit helps offset the cost of premiums, making it more affordable for small businesses to offer this important benefit. Understanding business credits is crucial for the SEE exam, as you need to advise clients on available tax benefits to minimize their tax liability.
questions.examTip: Remember the specific FTE and average wage thresholds for the Small Business Health Care Tax Credit, as these are frequently tested on the EA exam. Pay attention to indexed amounts for the current tax year.
questions.memoryAid: 25 FTEs and Fair Wages = Health Care Credit
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the specific requirements for the Small Business Health Care Tax Credit. The exam exploits the misconception that any small business offering health insurance qualifies. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that the credit is available to businesses with more employees or higher average wages than the limits allow.

questions.question: Apex Manufacturing sold a machine used in its production process for a gain. The company had previously claimed depreciation deductions on the machine. Section 1245 recapture applies to:
options: Personal property where gain is taxed as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation taken
Goodwill and intangibles only, specifically when the amortization expense has been previously deducted for tax purposes.
Real property only, where the gain is treated as unrecaptured Section 1250 gain and taxed at a maximum rate of 25 percent.
All capital gains recognized on the sale of business assets, regardless of whether depreciation deductions were previously claimed.
questions.explanation: Section 1245 requires recapture of depreciation on personal property as ordinary income when the property is sold. Any gain in excess of depreciation recapture is Section 1231 gain (potentially capital gain).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Section 1245 dictates that any gain realized on the sale of personal property (or certain real property) is treated as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation previously taken. This prevents taxpayers from converting ordinary income (via depreciation deductions) into capital gains upon sale.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Section 1245 primarily applies to personal property, not goodwill or intangibles. While amortization can affect gain, it's not the sole trigger for 1245.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Section 1245 applies to personal property, not real property. Section 1250 addresses real property depreciation recapture.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Section 1245 recapture is limited to the amount of depreciation taken and does not apply to the entire capital gain if the gain exceeds accumulated depreciation.

questions.educational: Depreciation is an ordinary deduction that reduces taxable income. Section 1245 exists to prevent taxpayers from taking these ordinary deductions and then selling the asset at a capital gain, effectively converting ordinary income into capital gain. This recapture rule ensures that the tax benefit received through depreciation is 'paid back' when the asset is sold at a gain, up to the amount of depreciation taken.
questions.examTip: Remember that Section 1245 recapture always results in ordinary income, and it applies primarily to personal property. On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between Section 1245 (personal property) and Section 1250 (real property) recapture rules.
questions.memoryAid: 1245: 'P' for Personal Property. 1250: Think 'Real-ty' for Real Property.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse Section 1245 recapture with other types of capital gains or Section 1250 recapture (for real property). The exam tests whether you understand that Section 1245 applies specifically to *personal property* and that the gain is taxed as *ordinary income* to the extent of depreciation taken. Test-takers often incorrectly associate it with real property, goodwill, or all capital gains.

questions.question: David sold land to his wholly-owned corporation, Willow Creek Properties, at a loss. Under Section 267, losses on sales between related parties are:
options: Fully deductible
Deductible at 50%
Deferred until the property is sold to an unrelated party
Disallowed entirely
questions.explanation: Losses on sales between related parties (as defined in Section 267) are disallowed. However, the related buyer may reduce their gain on a subsequent sale to an unrelated party by the previously disallowed loss.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Section 267 specifically disallows losses on related party sales; they are not fully deductible. The purpose of this rule is to prevent artificial losses from being recognized within a related group.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Section 267 disallows the loss entirely, not just a portion of it. There is no provision for a 50% deduction of the loss in related party transactions.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the disallowed loss can provide a benefit to the related party buyer upon a subsequent sale to an unrelated party (by reducing the gain), the original loss is not 'deferred.' It's permanently disallowed to the seller, though it can impact the buyer's future gain.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 267 of the Internal Revenue Code disallows losses on sales or exchanges of property between related parties. This rule prevents taxpayers from artificially generating tax losses by transferring property within their controlled group.

questions.educational: Section 267 aims to prevent tax avoidance through artificial losses created by related party transactions. Understanding related party definitions (family members, controlled entities, etc.) is crucial. This rule connects to broader concepts of tax shelters and the IRS's efforts to ensure fair and accurate tax reporting.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully review the relationship between the buyer and seller when a loss is reported on a sale of property; look for keywords like 'sibling,' 'corporation owned by family,' or 'trust for children' which often indicate a related party transaction.
questions.memoryAid: RAL: Related party losses are Always Lost (to the seller).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the related party transaction rules under Section 267. The exam exploits the misconception that losses on sales are always deductible. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that the loss is deferred or deductible at a reduced rate, failing to recognize that losses between certain related parties are *entirely disallowed*.

questions.question: Partner A contributes property with a fair market value of $50,000 and an adjusted basis of $30,000 to a new partnership for a 50% interest. What is Partner A's basis in the partnership interest?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §722, a partner's basis in a partnership interest received for a property contribution equals the adjusted basis of the property contributed. Partner A's basis is $30,000 (carryover basis).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Under IRC §722, the contributing partner's basis in the partnership interest is generally equal to the adjusted basis of the property contributed. In this case, Partner A's adjusted basis in the contributed property is $30,000, so their basis in the partnership interest is also $30,000.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The fair market value of the contributed property ($50,000) is not used to determine the partner's basis in the partnership interest. The basis is determined by the adjusted basis of the property contributed, not its fair market value.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $40,000 is not a relevant calculation in determining the partner's basis. The partner's basis is directly tied to the adjusted basis of the contributed property, not an average or other calculation involving fair market value.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $25,000 is not a relevant calculation in determining the partner's basis. It seems to be an attempt to confuse test-takers by using 50% (the partner's ownership percentage) with the fair market value, but this is not the correct method.

questions.educational: The carryover basis rule for partnership contributions under IRC §722 reflects the principle that contributions are generally tax-free events. This prevents the recognition of gain or loss at the time of contribution, deferring it until the partnership disposes of the property or the partner disposes of their partnership interest. This aligns with the aggregate theory of partnerships, which views the partnership as a conduit for its partners.
questions.examTip: Remember that the basis of contributed property carries over to the partner's basis in the partnership interest, but the partnership's basis in the contributed property also carries over from the partner's basis. Be careful to distinguish between the partner's basis in their partnership interest and the partnership's basis in the assets it holds.
questions.memoryAid: Basis follows basis: The partner's basis in the partnership interest 'follows' the adjusted basis of the property they contributed.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the partner's basis in the partnership interest with the fair market value of the contributed property. The exam exploits the misconception that FMV always dictates basis. However, under §722, the contributing partner's basis in their partnership interest is generally equal to the adjusted basis of the property they contributed.

questions.question: Partner B contributes land with a FMV of $100,000 and basis of $40,000 to a partnership. If the partnership later sells the land for $120,000, how is the $80,000 gain allocated?
options: $60,000 to Partner B, $20,000 shared by all partners
$80,000 entirely to Partner B
$40,000 to Partner B, $40,000 shared by all partners
Shared equally among all partners
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(c), the $60,000 built-in gain ($100,000 FMV - $40,000 basis) at contribution must be allocated to Partner B. The $20,000 post-contribution appreciation is allocated per the partnership agreement.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Under IRC §704(c), when property is contributed to a partnership, any built-in gain (the difference between the property's fair market value and its adjusted basis at the time of contribution) must be allocated to the contributing partner. Here, the built-in gain is $60,000 ($100,000 FMV - $40,000 basis), allocated to Partner B. The remaining $20,000 gain ($120,000 selling price - $100,000 FMV at contribution) is allocated according to the partnership agreement, which we assume is shared by all partners since no other information is given.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While Partner B is allocated the built-in gain, the post-contribution appreciation is allocated according to the partnership agreement, not solely to the contributing partner.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The built-in gain is the difference between the FMV and basis at contribution, which is $60,000, not $40,000. The remaining gain is then allocated as per the partnership agreement.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - IRC §704(c) specifically requires the built-in gain to be allocated to the contributing partner before any other allocation methods are applied.

questions.educational: IRC §704(c) prevents shifting pre-contribution gains or losses to other partners. This ensures that the contributing partner recognizes the gain or loss that economically occurred before the partnership was formed. This rule is crucial for maintaining fairness and preventing tax avoidance within partnerships, a key area tested on the Business Taxation section of the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember to always identify and allocate built-in gains or losses to the contributing partner first before allocating any remaining gain or loss according to the partnership agreement. Pay close attention to the FMV and adjusted basis at the time of contribution.
questions.memoryAid: FMV minus Basis = Built-in Gain (allocate to contributing partner)
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is assuming all gains are split according to the partnership agreement. Section 704(c) specifically addresses contributed property and requires the pre-contribution gain (or loss) to be allocated to the contributing partner. Test-takers often forget this special allocation rule.

questions.question: A partner receives a 20% capital interest in a partnership worth $50,000 in exchange for services rendered. The tax consequence is:
options: The amount is excluded from income as compensation
Capital gain of $50,000
No income recognized until the interest is sold
Ordinary income of $50,000 in the year received
questions.explanation: When a partner receives a capital interest for services, the fair market value of the interest is ordinary income under IRC §83. The partner has a basis equal to the amount included in income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The receipt of a capital interest in a partnership in exchange for services is specifically included in income as compensation, not excluded. IRC §83 governs property transferred in connection with performance of services.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The income recognized is ordinary income, not capital gain. The partner is receiving compensation for services, which is always taxed at ordinary income rates. Capital gain only arises from the sale or exchange of a capital asset.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The partner recognizes income when the capital interest is *received*, not when it is sold. Deferring recognition until the sale would violate the principle that compensation for services is taxable when received.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - When a partner receives a capital interest in a partnership in exchange for services, the fair market value of that interest ($50,000 in this case) is treated as ordinary income to the partner. This is taxable in the year the interest is received, per IRC §83 and related regulations.

questions.educational: The rule that a capital interest received for services is ordinary income stems from the fundamental principle that compensation for services is taxable. This ensures that individuals cannot avoid income tax by receiving partnership equity instead of cash wages. This concept is tested throughout the SEE exam, particularly in partnership and individual taxation.
questions.examTip: Remember that the receipt of a capital interest for services creates ordinary income to the service provider, and the partnership can generally deduct the value of the interest as a business expense (subject to capitalization rules).
questions.memoryAid: Services for Shares = Ordinary Income (SOS = OI)
questions.commonMistake: The exam exploits the misconception that all partnership interests received are tax-free. Many candidates incorrectly believe that receiving a partnership interest is always a non-taxable event under Section 721. However, when a capital interest is received in exchange for services, it's considered compensation and is taxable as ordinary income.

questions.question: Partner C contributes property with a FMV of $100,000, basis of $70,000, subject to a $40,000 recourse liability (assumed by partnership). If Partner C has a 30% partnership interest, what is the basis in the partnership interest?
questions.explanation: Basis starts at $70,000 (property basis). Decrease by liability relief of $40,000 (treated as distribution). Increase by share of partnership liabilities ($40,000 × 30% = $12,000). Result: $70,000 - $40,000 + $12,000 = $42,000.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This answer only considers the partner's share of the liability as an increase to basis and fails to account for the initial basis of the contributed property and the liability relief.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This answer incorrectly calculates the net effect of the liability relief and the partner's share of the partnership liability. It likely subtracts the full liability from the initial basis and adds an incorrect amount for the share of partnership liabilities.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This answer only considers the basis of the contributed property and fails to account for the liability relief and the partner's share of the partnership liability. The assumption of the liability by the partnership is treated as a distribution to the partner, reducing their basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Partner C's basis in the partnership interest is calculated as follows: Beginning basis in contributed property ($70,000) less liability relief ($40,000) plus the partner's share of the partnership liability ($40,000 * 30% = $12,000). Therefore, $70,000 - $40,000 + $12,000 = $42,000. This reflects the net economic effect of the contribution and liability assumption.

questions.educational: When a partner contributes property to a partnership, the partner's basis in their partnership interest is generally the adjusted basis of the contributed property. However, if the partnership assumes a liability related to the property, the contributing partner is treated as receiving a distribution, which reduces their basis. Conversely, the partner's share of partnership liabilities increases their basis, reflecting their economic risk in the partnership. This is covered under IRC Section 722 and 752.
questions.examTip: Remember to always consider both the contribution of property and the assumption of liabilities when calculating a partner's basis in a partnership. The liability relief is treated as a distribution, while the partner's share of partnership liabilities increases their basis. This is a common area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: CBL: Contribution Basis, Liability Relief, Liability Share. Remember to adjust the contribution basis by subtracting liability relief and adding the partner's share of liabilities.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget to consider the impact of liabilities assumed by the partnership on the partner's basis. The exam exploits the misconception that basis is simply the adjusted basis of contributed property. The assumption of a partner's liability by the partnership is treated as a distribution of cash to the partner, which reduces their basis, but the partner also gets to increase their basis by their share of partnership liabilities.

questions.question: Two individuals are forming a partnership, Sky High Investments. One partner is contributing real estate. The tax-free contribution rules of Section 721 do NOT apply when:
options: The property contributed has a built-in loss exceeding the partner's basis, triggering a mandatory loss disallowance.
The partnership is a limited partnership formed primarily to avoid self-employment taxes on partnership income.
The contribution would result in the formation of an investment company
One partner contributes more than 80% of the total capital and receives guaranteed payments that are disproportionate to their capital contribution.
questions.explanation: Section 721 does not apply to contributions that result in diversification of interests and creation of an investment company. This prevents tax-free pooling of appreciated investments.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A built-in loss doesn't automatically disqualify a contribution from Section 721 treatment, though it does have implications for future loss recognition. The loss is preserved for the contributing partner.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The type of partnership (general or limited) does not affect the applicability of Section 721. Section 721 applies regardless of the partnership structure.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Section 721(b) specifically states that the general rule of nonrecognition of gain or loss does not apply to a contribution to a partnership which would be treated as an investment company (within the meaning of Section 351(e)). This prevents taxpayers from using partnerships to diversify their investment portfolios tax-free, which would otherwise be possible under Section 721.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The percentage of capital contributed by a partner does not, by itself, disqualify the contribution from Section 721 treatment. Guaranteed payments also do not impact 721 eligibility.

questions.educational: Section 721 allows for tax-free contributions of property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest. This rule facilitates the formation and growth of partnerships by deferring the recognition of gain or loss until the partnership interest is sold. However, exceptions exist, such as the investment company rule under Section 721(b), to prevent abuse of the nonrecognition provisions for diversifying investment portfolios.
questions.examTip: Remember that the investment company exception to Section 721 is triggered when the contribution results in diversification of the contributors' interests and the partnership would be considered an investment company under Section 351(e). Pay close attention to scenarios involving marketable securities.
questions.memoryAid: 721: Tax-free contribution, unless it's an 'Investment Company' (IC).
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that Section 721 always applies to contributions to a partnership. The exam tests whether you know the exceptions to the general rule of non-recognition. The formation of an investment company through the contribution of appreciated property can trigger gain recognition.

questions.question: Lisa contributes a building to the Maple Leaf Partnership. Lisa's adjusted basis in the building was $200,000, and its fair market value was $300,000. When a partner contributes property to a partnership, the partnership's basis in the property is:
options: Zero until the partnership recognizes gain upon disposition of the property in a taxable sale.
The fair market value of the property at the time of contribution, reflecting its current economic worth.
The partner's adjusted basis (carryover basis)
The lower of the fair market value or the adjusted basis, as a conservative measure of asset valuation.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §723, the partnership's basis in contributed property equals the contributing partner's adjusted basis in the property immediately before contribution (carryover basis).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The partnership takes a carryover basis, not a zero basis. The partnership's basis is determined at the time of contribution, not upon a later sale.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The partnership takes a carryover basis, not a fair market value basis. FMV is relevant for determining capital accounts, but not the partnership's basis in the contributed property.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Under IRC §723, the partnership's basis in property contributed by a partner is the adjusted basis of the property in the hands of the contributing partner at the time of the contribution. This is known as a carryover basis, meaning the partnership 'steps into the shoes' of the partner regarding basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The partnership takes a carryover basis, regardless of whether it is higher or lower than the fair market value. The lower of FMV or adjusted basis rule applies in other contexts, but not to contributions to a partnership.

questions.educational: The carryover basis rule for partnership contributions reflects the idea that the contribution is generally a non-taxable event. Because the partner isn't recognizing a gain or loss on the contribution, the partnership inherits the partner's basis to preserve the potential for future gain or loss recognition. This aligns with the broader concept of deferral in partnership taxation, a key area on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the mnemonic 'ABC' - Adjusted Basis Carryover - to quickly recall that the partnership takes the contributing partner's adjusted basis in contributed property. This is a frequently tested concept on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: ABC - Adjusted Basis Carryover
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is confusing the partnership's basis in contributed property with the partner's basis in their partnership interest. The exam exploits the misconception that the partnership gets a step-up in basis to fair market value. However, under Section 723, the partnership generally takes a carryover basis, meaning the same basis the contributing partner had.

questions.question: Jane contributed land (basis $20,000, held for 3 years) to the JKL Partnership in exchange for a 25% capital interest. Regarding Jane's partnership interest, the holding period:
options: Begins on the date of contribution, regardless of prior holding period
Is determined by the partnership agreement, allowing for flexibility in allocations
Includes the holding period of the contributed property
Is always long-term, provided the contribution is made for business purposes
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1223(1), when a partner receives a partnership interest in exchange for a capital asset or Section 1231 asset, the holding period includes the time the contributed property was held.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The holding period of the contributed property tacks on to the holding period of the partnership interest. The contribution date is irrelevant if the contributed property was held longer.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The partnership agreement dictates how profits, losses, and capital are allocated, but it does not determine the holding period of a partner's interest. Holding period is determined by tax law.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §1223(1) states that if property contributed to a partnership is a capital asset or a Section 1231 asset in the hands of the partner, the partner's holding period for the partnership interest includes the holding period of the contributed property. This 'tacking' rule prevents partners from immediately claiming long-term capital gains treatment on the sale of their partnership interest if the contributed asset was held long-term.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The holding period of the partnership interest depends on the holding period of the contributed asset. It is not automatically long-term.

questions.educational: The holding period of a partnership interest is crucial for determining whether any gain or loss on the sale of that interest is treated as short-term or long-term capital gain or loss. The 'tacking' rule in IRC §1223(1) prevents taxpayers from converting short-term gains into long-term gains simply by contributing property to a partnership. This concept is also relevant when considering the character of income and loss passed through from the partnership to the partners, a key aspect of partnership taxation.
questions.examTip: Remember the 'tacking' rule for holding periods when property is contributed to a partnership. Pay close attention to the type of asset contributed (capital or 1231) as this determines whether the holding period tacks.
questions.memoryAid: CAPITAL TACKS: If you contribute a CAPITAL asset, the holding period TACKS onto your partnership interest.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget the concept of tacked holding periods. The exam exploits the misconception that a new holding period always begins when property is contributed to a partnership. However, under Section 1223(2), the partner's holding period for the contributed property tacks on to the holding period of the partnership interest.

questions.question: A partnership incurs $7,000 in organizational expenses. How should these be treated for tax purposes?
options: Deduct the full $7,000 in the first year as an ordinary business expense, provided the partnership uses the cash method of accounting.
Capitalize and amortize the entire amount over 180 months
No deduction allowed in the current year; the $7,000 must be capitalized and amortized over a period of 15 years (180 months) starting with the partnership's formation.
Deduct $5,000 in the first year, amortize $2,000 over 180 months
questions.explanation: Partnerships may elect under IRC §709 to deduct up to $5,000 of organizational expenses in the first year (reduced if total exceeds $50,000), with the remainder amortized over 180 months.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While some expenses are deductible, organizational expenses have specific rules. The option incorrectly allows a full deduction in the first year without considering the $5,000 limit and amortization.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the amortization of organizational expenses over 180 months is correct for the amount exceeding $5,000, this option fails to account for the initial $5,000 deduction permitted under IRC §709.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While amortization is required, the option fails to acknowledge the immediate $5,000 deduction permitted for organizational expenses. It incorrectly states that no deduction is allowed in the current year.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §709 allows a partnership to deduct up to $5,000 of organizational expenses in the first year. Since the expenses here are $7,000, the partnership can deduct $5,000 immediately and amortize the remaining $2,000 ($7,000 - $5,000) over 180 months, starting with the month the partnership begins business.

questions.educational: The ability to deduct and amortize organizational expenses allows businesses, including partnerships, to recover the costs of setting up their operations over time. Without this provision, these costs would be capitalized indefinitely, creating a barrier to entry and potentially distorting financial reporting. Understanding the specific rules for organizational expenses is crucial for accurate tax planning and compliance, especially when dealing with different business entities.
questions.examTip: Remember the $5,000 immediate deduction limit for organizational expenses and start-up costs; this limit is reduced dollar-for-dollar when total expenses exceed $50,000. Pay close attention to the wording of the question to identify whether organizational expenses, start-up costs, or both are being tested.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '5 and 180' - $5,000 immediate deduction, and 180 months for amortization of the excess.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that all organizational expenses can be deducted immediately. The exam tests whether you know the specific rules for deducting and amortizing organizational expenses. While a portion can be deducted in the first year, the remainder must be amortized over 180 months.

questions.question: The law firm of Miller & Zois incurred $50,000 in costs related to syndicating the partnership (marketing interests to new partners). These syndication costs are:
options: Amortizable over 180 months like organizational costs
Deductible in the first year of operations
Deductible when the partnership terminates
Capitalized and never amortizable or deductible
questions.explanation: Unlike organizational costs, syndication costs must be capitalized and cannot be amortized or deducted. They include brokerage fees, registration fees, and costs of printing prospectuses for selling partnership interests.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Organizational costs are amortizable over 180 months (with a possible first-year deduction), but syndication costs are treated differently. Syndication costs are specifically disallowed from being treated like organizational costs under IRC Section 709.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Syndication costs are capital expenditures, not ordinary expenses. They do not meet the requirements for a current deduction under IRC Section 162 or any other provision.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While some capital expenditures can be deducted upon the liquidation of a business if they have no remaining value, syndication costs are permanently disallowed. They are neither amortizable nor deductible at any point, including termination.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Syndication costs, which are expenses related to the issuing and marketing of partnership interests, must be capitalized. IRC Section 709(a) specifically disallows any deduction or amortization of these costs, making them a permanent capital expenditure.

questions.educational: Partnership syndication costs are treated differently from organizational costs due to their nature. Syndication costs are incurred to raise capital, similar to stock issuance costs for corporations, and are therefore considered a permanent capital expenditure. Understanding the distinction between organizational and syndication costs is crucial for accurately calculating a partnership's taxable income and partners' basis.
questions.examTip: Remember that syndication costs are permanently capitalized and never deductible, unlike organizational costs which have a special amortization rule. Pay close attention to the wording of partnership expense questions on the SEE exam to differentiate between these two types of expenses.
questions.memoryAid: Syndication = Stuck (capitalized, never deducted)
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is confusing organizational costs with syndication costs. The exam exploits the misconception that both types of costs are treated the same for tax purposes. However, syndication costs, which are related to the marketing and selling of partnership interests, are never deductible or amortizable.

questions.question: A partner contributes property and simultaneously receives a guaranteed payment. The IRS may treat this as:
options: A tax-free contribution under Section 721
A loan from the partnership, if properly documented with a reasonable interest rate and repayment schedule.
A distribution of partnership earnings
A disguised sale under Section 707(a)(2)(B)
questions.explanation: When a partner contributes property and receives cash or other consideration, the IRS may recharacterize this as a disguised sale under IRC §707(a)(2)(B), making the transaction taxable.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While Section 721 generally provides for tax-free contributions of property to a partnership, this treatment does not apply if the contribution is effectively a sale. The guaranteed payment received simultaneously with the property contribution suggests a disguised sale, overriding the typical Section 721 nonrecognition.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While a loan is possible, the simultaneous nature of the contribution and payment, especially if the payment is tied to the property's value, points to a disguised sale, not a bona fide loan. The IRS will scrutinize the transaction to determine its true nature.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While guaranteed payments are related to partnership earnings, they are not simply a distribution of those earnings. Guaranteed payments are determined without regard to partnership income and are treated as if paid to a non-partner for services or the use of capital. The issue here is not the nature of the payment itself, but whether the entire transaction is a disguised sale.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §707(a)(2)(B) addresses disguised sales, where a contribution of property and a related distribution (like a guaranteed payment) are treated as a sale between the partner and the partnership. The IRS can recharacterize the transaction as a sale if the transfer of money or other consideration would not have occurred but for the transfer of property, and if, when the transfers are viewed together, it is properly characterized as a sale.

questions.educational: The disguised sale rules under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) prevent partners from circumventing the taxable sale rules by structuring transactions as contributions and distributions. These rules are crucial in partnership taxation because they ensure that economic realities are reflected in the tax treatment of transactions, preventing tax avoidance. Understanding these rules is essential for accurately determining a partner's basis and the partnership's basis in contributed property, which directly impacts future gains, losses, and depreciation deductions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the timing and relationship between property contributions and distributions to partners. If they occur close together, consider whether the disguised sale rules might apply, requiring you to treat the transaction as a taxable sale rather than a tax-free contribution.
questions.memoryAid: CONTRIBUTE & CASH = COULD BE A SALE (Disguised Sale)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates assume that contributions to a partnership are always tax-free under Section 721. The exam tests whether you are aware of the disguised sale rules. If a contribution and a related distribution (like a guaranteed payment) are essentially a sale, the transaction may be recharacterized as a sale between the partner and the partnership.

questions.question: The ABC Partnership is preparing its Form 1065. Which of the following items must be separately stated on the partnership return?
options: Cost of goods sold
Depreciation expense on business equipment
Utilities expense
Net section 1231 gains and losses
questions.explanation: Section 1231 gains/losses must be separately stated because their tax treatment may differ for each partner based on individual circumstances. COGS, depreciation, and utilities are included in ordinary income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Cost of goods sold (COGS) is directly related to the partnership's business operations and is used to calculate gross profit, which is part of the partnership's ordinary income. It is not separately stated but rather aggregated into the overall income calculation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Depreciation expense on business equipment is a common business expense that reduces the partnership's ordinary income. While depreciation methods can vary, the total depreciation expense is aggregated and reported as part of the partnership's overall expenses, not separately stated for each partner.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Utilities expense is a typical operating expense incurred by the partnership in the course of its business. Like other operating expenses, it is aggregated and reduces the partnership's ordinary income, and is not separately stated.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 1231 gains and losses must be separately stated because their character (capital or ordinary) depends on the partner's individual circumstances, such as whether they have other Section 1231 gains or losses from other sources. This separate statement ensures each partner can properly apply the Section 1231 netting rules on their individual tax return.

questions.educational: Partnerships operate under a pass-through taxation system, meaning the partnership itself doesn't pay income tax. Instead, income, deductions, gains, and losses are passed through to the partners, who report them on their individual income tax returns. Certain items, like Section 1231 gains/losses, charitable contributions, and investment interest expense, must be separately stated because their tax treatment can vary depending on the partner's individual tax situation. This ensures accurate tax reporting at the partner level.
questions.examTip: Remember that the K-1 form is the primary document used to report a partner's share of the partnership's income, deductions, credits, and other items. Pay close attention to the items that require separate reporting on Schedule K-1, as these are frequently tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: SILO: Separately stated items include Section 1231 gains/losses, Investment interest expense, Like-kind exchanges, and Other items (catch-all).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget which items must be separately stated. The exam exploits the misconception that only a few items need separate reporting. Cost of goods sold, depreciation, and utilities are generally aggregated, while items with special tax characteristics (like 1231 gains/losses) pass through separately to partners.

questions.question: Partners Able and Baker agree that Able will receive 80% of partnership depreciation. For this special allocation of partnership depreciation to be respected for tax purposes, it must:
options: Apply equally to all types of income
Be reported to the IRS in advance
Be approved by all partners unanimously
Have substantial economic effect
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(b), allocations must have substantial economic effect to be respected. This requires proper capital account maintenance, deficit restoration obligations, and QIO provisions or actual economic effect.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Special allocations do not need to apply equally to all types of income. They can be designed to allocate specific items of income or deduction disproportionately, as long as they have substantial economic effect under IRC §704(b).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - There is no requirement to report special allocations to the IRS in advance. The allocations are reported on Schedule K-1 of Form 1065, which is filed annually with the partnership's tax return. The IRS reviews these allocations to ensure they meet the substantial economic effect test.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While it's good practice to have all partners agree to special allocations, unanimous approval is not strictly required for the allocation to be respected for tax purposes. The key requirement is that the allocation has substantial economic effect under IRC §704(b), regardless of whether every partner formally approved it.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Under IRC §704(b), a special allocation of partnership income must have substantial economic effect to be respected for tax purposes. This means the allocation must actually affect the partners' economic positions, as reflected in their capital accounts, and there must be a reasonable possibility that the allocation will substantially affect the dollar amounts to be received by the partners from the partnership, independent of tax consequences.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation allows for flexibility in allocating income and losses among partners. However, this flexibility is limited by the 'substantial economic effect' requirement. This requirement, stemming from IRC §704(b), ensures that allocations reflect the true economic agreement between partners and are not solely designed to minimize taxes. Understanding this principle is crucial for accurately preparing partnership tax returns and advising clients on partnership agreements.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS scrutinizes partnership allocations, especially those that deviate from the standard profit and loss sharing ratios. Always evaluate whether a special allocation meets the substantial economic effect test before finalizing the partnership return.
questions.memoryAid: SEE it to BELIEVE it: Substantial Economic Effect is key for partnership allocations.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that any agreement among partners is acceptable for tax purposes. A common trap is overlooking the 'substantial economic effect' requirement. While unanimous approval might be needed for partnership agreements, it doesn't guarantee the allocation's validity for tax purposes.

questions.question: Partner Lee has a beginning basis of $45,000 in the AB Partnership. During the year, the partnership reported: $30,000 ordinary income, $5,000 tax-exempt interest, $8,000 charitable contributions, and $15,000 distributions to Lee. What is Lee's ending basis?
questions.explanation: Partner basis adjustments follow the ordering rules of IRC §705. Starting basis: $45,000. Add: ordinary income $30,000 + tax-exempt interest $5,000 = $80,000. Subtract: charitable contributions $8,000 + distributions $15,000 = $57,000. Tax-exempt income increases basis (prevents double taxation), and separately stated deductions decrease basis. Distributions reduce basis but are not taxable unless they exceed basis.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The correct answer is $57,000. Partner basis is adjusted annually to reflect the partner's share of partnership income, deductions, and distributions, following IRC §705. Tax-exempt income increases basis, while charitable contributions and distributions decrease it.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This answer incorrectly adds the ordinary income and tax-exempt interest to the beginning basis but fails to subtract the charitable contributions and distributions. Remember that both deductible expenses and distributions reduce a partner's basis.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This answer incorrectly adds all income items to the beginning basis and fails to subtract the distributions. Charitable contributions are a separately stated item that reduces basis, and distributions also reduce basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This answer represents the partner's beginning basis and fails to account for the adjustments necessary to determine the ending basis. Partnership income increases basis, while deductions and distributions decrease it.

questions.educational: A partner's basis in a partnership is crucial for determining the taxability of distributions and the deductibility of losses. Basis is increased by contributions, the partner's share of taxable income, and tax-exempt income. It is decreased by distributions, the partner's share of losses, and nondeductible expenses. Understanding basis adjustments is essential for accurately reporting partnership activities on Form 1040 Schedule E.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to whether an item increases or decreases basis. Tax-exempt income and capital contributions INCREASE basis, while distributions and deductible expenses DECREASE basis.
questions.memoryAid: CBD: Contributions and Book Income increase basis; Distributions decrease basis.
questions.commonMistake: A common mistake is forgetting the order of basis adjustments. Test-takers often incorrectly add distributions before accounting for income and deductions. Tax-exempt income increases basis, while non-deductible expenses (like charitable contributions) decrease it.

questions.question: A partner with a $20,000 basis in their partnership interest has a distributive share of $35,000 in partnership losses. How much can they deduct?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(d), a partner's share of partnership losses is limited to the partner's adjusted basis in the partnership interest. The $15,000 excess is suspended and carried forward indefinitely.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Under IRC §704(d), a partner's deductible share of partnership losses is limited to their adjusted basis in the partnership interest at the end of the partnership year. In this case, the partner's basis is $20,000, so that is the maximum loss they can deduct.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The partner has a basis of $20,000 in their partnership interest, meaning they can deduct losses up to that amount. Therefore, deducting $0 is incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the partner's distributive share of the loss is $35,000, IRC §704(d) limits the deductible loss to the partner's basis, which is $20,000. The excess $15,000 loss is suspended.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The partner can deduct up to their basis of $20,000. Deducting $15,000 would leave $5,000 of basis unused, which is not the correct application of IRC §704(d).

questions.educational: The limitation on deducting partnership losses to a partner's basis is in place to prevent partners from deducting losses exceeding their investment in the partnership. This rule ensures that partners don't use partnership losses to create or increase a negative capital account beyond their actual economic risk. Suspended losses are carried forward indefinitely and can be used when the partner's basis increases, such as through future contributions or partnership income.
questions.examTip: Remember to always check the partner's basis before determining the deductible loss. The basis limitation under IRC §704(d) is a common testing point on the EA exam, so be prepared to apply it in various scenarios.
questions.memoryAid: Basis Before Benefit: Deductible partnership losses are limited to the partner's basis.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the deductibility of losses with the partner's at-risk amount or capital account. The exam exploits the misconception that a partner can deduct losses exceeding their basis. Losses are limited to the partner's basis; excess losses are suspended and carried forward.

questions.question: John is a general partner in the Smith & Jones partnership. John's share of partnership ordinary income is generally:
options: Exempt from self-employment tax
Taxed at capital gains rates
Subject to self-employment tax
Subject to self-employment tax only if the partner works 500+ hours
questions.explanation: A general partner's distributive share of partnership ordinary income from a trade or business is considered self-employment income under IRC §1402(a). Limited partners generally are not subject to SE tax except for guaranteed payments.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A general partner's share of partnership ordinary income is specifically subject to self-employment tax under IRC §1402(a), not exempt. This reflects the partner's active participation in the business.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A general partner's share of ordinary income is taxed at ordinary income rates, not capital gains rates. Capital gains rates apply to the sale of capital assets, not the ongoing operational income of the partnership.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A general partner's distributive share of partnership ordinary income from a trade or business is considered self-employment income under IRC §1402(a). This is because general partners are actively involved in the business operations and are therefore treated as self-employed individuals for tax purposes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The requirement for self-employment tax is not contingent on the number of hours worked by the general partner. All ordinary income allocated to a general partner is subject to self-employment tax, regardless of their time commitment.

questions.educational: Partnerships are pass-through entities, meaning the income flows through to the partners who then pay tax at their individual rates. General partners are considered actively involved in the business, and their share of ordinary income is treated as self-employment income, subject to both income tax and self-employment tax (Social Security and Medicare). This contrasts with limited partners, whose income is generally not subject to self-employment tax unless it's a guaranteed payment for services.
questions.examTip: Remember that guaranteed payments to partners are always subject to self-employment tax, regardless of whether they are general or limited partners. This is a common area tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: GOSE: General partner Ordinary income = Self-Employment tax
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that partnership income is treated the same for all partners. A common trap is overlooking the self-employment tax implications for general partners. While limited partners may have different rules, a general partner's share of ordinary income is generally subject to self-employment tax, regardless of hours worked.

questions.question: Partner X receives a guaranteed payment of $50,000 for services. The partnership has a loss of $30,000 before considering the guaranteed payment. What is the partnership's ordinary income (loss) reported on Form 1065?
questions.explanation: Guaranteed payments are deducted by the partnership in computing ordinary income. The partnership loss is $30,000 + $50,000 guaranteed payment deduction = $80,000 total loss reported on Form 1065.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This answer only considers the initial partnership loss and fails to account for the guaranteed payment made to Partner X, which is deductible by the partnership.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This answer incorrectly subtracts the guaranteed payment from the initial partnership loss. Guaranteed payments increase the partnership's loss or reduce its income.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Guaranteed payments to partners for services are treated as deductible business expenses by the partnership. Therefore, the $50,000 guaranteed payment is added to the $30,000 loss, resulting in a total loss of $80,000 reported on Form 1065.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This answer only reflects the amount of the guaranteed payment and ignores the initial partnership loss of $30,000. The guaranteed payment must be considered in conjunction with the partnership's other income and expenses.

questions.educational: Guaranteed payments are payments made to a partner for services or the use of capital, determined without regard to partnership income. They are treated as if paid to a non-partner, creating a deductible expense for the partnership and taxable income to the partner. This treatment impacts both the partner's individual tax return and the partnership's overall taxable income, which flows through to the partners.
questions.examTip: Remember that guaranteed payments are deductible by the partnership when calculating ordinary income or loss on Form 1065, Schedule K, and are also reported separately on Schedule K-1 for each partner receiving the payment.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'G'uaranteed payments as 'G'oing against partnership income – they either reduce income or increase a loss.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse guaranteed payments with distributions or incorrectly net them against partnership income. The exam exploits the misconception that guaranteed payments are simply netted against partnership income. Guaranteed payments are deductible by the partnership, increasing the loss or decreasing the income.

questions.question: Partner Davis contributed $50,000 cash to the RST Partnership. He is also personally liable for a $20,000 recourse debt of the partnership. Which amount is included in Davis's at-risk amount for partnership activities?
options: Amounts borrowed from persons with an interest in the activity, even if the debt is recourse
Nonrecourse debt secured only by partnership property, allocated based on profit sharing ratios
All partnership liabilities regardless of recourse status, including those exceeding the partner's basis
Cash contributions plus recourse debt for which the partner is personally liable
questions.explanation: Under IRC §465, amounts at risk include cash contributions, adjusted basis of contributed property, and amounts borrowed for which the partner is personally liable or has pledged property not used in the activity.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Amounts borrowed from persons with an interest in the activity are specifically excluded from the at-risk amount, even if recourse.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Nonrecourse debt is generally not included in a partner's at-risk amount unless it's qualified nonrecourse financing.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The at-risk amount does not include all partnership liabilities; it focuses on amounts the partner could actually lose.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Cash contributions and recourse debt for which the partner is personally liable are specifically included in the at-risk amount under IRC §465. A partner is considered at risk for the money and the adjusted basis of other property they contribute to the activity, as well as amounts borrowed for use in the activity if the partner is personally liable for repayment or has pledged property as security.

questions.educational: The at-risk rules, found in IRC §465, limit a taxpayer's deductible losses from an activity to the amount they could actually lose. This prevents taxpayers from deducting losses exceeding their economic investment. Understanding the at-risk rules is crucial for the EA exam because it directly impacts the calculation of deductible partnership losses, a common area of inquiry.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of partnership debt questions. Determine if the debt is recourse or nonrecourse, and whether the partner is personally liable, to correctly calculate the at-risk amount.
questions.memoryAid: CAR: Cash + Assets + Recourse debt = At-Risk amount.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly include all partnership liabilities in a partner's at-risk amount. A common trap is overlooking the distinction between recourse and nonrecourse debt. Only recourse debt for which the partner is personally liable increases the at-risk amount, along with cash contributions.

questions.question: Bob gifts his daughter, Alice, a capital interest in his partnership. Capital is a material income-producing factor for the partnership. Alice:
options: Must be allocated income only to the extent of reasonable compensation to the donor
Cannot be recognized as a partner
Receives no allocation until the partnership terminates
Is allocated a capital share, but donor must receive reasonable compensation first
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(e), if capital is a material income-producing factor, a donee family member is recognized as a partner but their share of income may be limited. The donor partner must be allocated reasonable compensation for services first.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the donor must receive reasonable compensation, the donee partner is allocated a share of income based on their capital interest, not solely to the extent of the donor's compensation. The donee's share is determined after the donor receives reasonable compensation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - IRC §704(e) specifically allows for the recognition of family members as partners in partnerships where capital is a material income-producing factor, provided certain conditions are met.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A donee partner is entitled to a share of the partnership income during the partnership's operation, not just upon termination. The allocation is based on their capital interest, subject to the reasonable compensation rule for the donor.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §704(e) addresses family partnerships and states that if capital is a material income-producing factor, a donee is recognized as a partner. However, the donor must first receive reasonable compensation for services rendered to the partnership before the remaining income is allocated according to the partners' capital interests.

questions.educational: Family partnerships are scrutinized by the IRS to prevent income shifting to lower-taxed family members. IRC §704(e) ensures that if capital is a material income-producing factor, the donee partner is recognized, but the donor must be reasonably compensated for their services before income is allocated based on capital interests. This prevents artificial shifting of income without genuine economic substance.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 'reasonable compensation' rule for family partnerships under IRC §704(e) is a common testing point. Always consider whether the donor partner is being adequately compensated before allocating income to donee partners.
questions.memoryAid: Family Partnership: 'Reasonable first, then rest' – Donor gets reasonable compensation first, then the remaining income is allocated based on capital interests.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the rules for family partnerships. The exam exploits the misconception that a gift of a partnership interest is automatically disallowed. While scrutiny is applied, a donee is recognized as a partner if capital is a material income-producing factor, and they actually own and control the interest, but reasonable compensation to the donor must be considered.

questions.question: The XYZ Partnership has three partners: Alpha (40% interest, calendar year), Beta (30% interest, June 30 year-end), and Gamma (30% interest, March 31 year-end). The partnership generally must use the same tax year as:
options: The tax year chosen in the partnership agreement
A calendar year
The managing partner
The majority interest partner(s)
questions.explanation: Under IRC §706(b), a partnership must use the tax year of partners owning more than 50% of profits and capital. If no majority exists, it uses the principal partners' year, or the year with least deferral.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the partnership agreement outlines many operational aspects, it does not dictate the tax year. IRC §706(b) provides specific rules for determining the partnership's tax year, overriding any conflicting provisions in the partnership agreement.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A partnership is not automatically required to use a calendar year. While a calendar year might be used if other methods fail to establish a tax year, it is not the primary determinant under IRC §706(b).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The managing partner's tax year is not the primary factor in determining the partnership's tax year. The majority interest partner(s) take precedence. If there is no majority interest, then the principal partners' tax year is considered.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Under IRC §706(b)(1)(B), a partnership must adopt the same tax year as the partner(s) who own a majority interest (more than 50%) in partnership profits and capital. This rule aims to align the partnership's income reporting with its owners, preventing undue tax deferral.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation aims to prevent income deferral by aligning the partnership's tax year with its owners. IRC §706(b) establishes a hierarchy for determining the tax year: first, the majority interest partner(s); second, the principal partners' tax year; and finally, the least aggregate deferral method. Understanding these rules is crucial for accurately reporting partnership income and avoiding penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the percentages of ownership in partnership profits and capital when determining the partnership's required tax year. Remember the hierarchy: majority interest, principal partners, least aggregate deferral.
questions.memoryAid: Majority Rules: The majority interest partner(s) determine the partnership's tax year.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that the partnership can choose any tax year. A common trap is overlooking the priority rules for determining the partnership's tax year. The majority interest partner rule takes precedence over the principal partner rule and the least aggregate deferral rule.

questions.question: Partner Y sells property to a partnership in which Y has a 60% interest for $100,000. Y's basis in the property was $120,000. What is the tax treatment?
options: Recognize $20,000 loss as a capital loss, subject to Section 1231 netting rules.
Recognize $20,000 loss as an ordinary loss because Y controls over 50% of the partnership.
No loss allowed; treated as a contribution to the partnership, increasing Y's basis.
No loss recognized; deferred until partner sells partnership interest
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(b)(1), losses are disallowed on sales between a partner and partnership if the partner owns more than 50%. The partnership takes a carryover basis and can recognize the loss when selling to an unrelated party.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Losses are disallowed on sales to controlled partnerships. Section 1231 has no bearing here.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While Y controls the partnership, losses are disallowed, not converted to ordinary. The loss is deferred.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the loss is disallowed, it is not treated as a contribution. The loss is deferred, not added to basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §707(b)(1) states that no loss shall be allowed in the case of a sale or exchange of property (other than an interest in the partnership), directly or indirectly, between a partnership and a person who owns, directly or indirectly, more than 50% of the capital interest, or profits interest, in such partnership. The loss is disallowed at the time of the sale and effectively deferred. The partnership's basis in the property is a carryover basis from the partner.

questions.educational: IRC §707(b) aims to prevent related parties from artificially creating tax losses. By disallowing losses on sales between a partner and a controlled partnership, the tax code ensures that losses are only recognized when the property is ultimately sold to an unrelated party. This rule is crucial for understanding partnership taxation and preventing tax avoidance strategies.
questions.examTip: Remember the 50% threshold for disallowed losses in transactions between partners and partnerships. Also, be aware that the disallowed loss may be recognized later if the partnership sells the property at a gain.
questions.memoryAid: 50/707: 50% ownership triggers 707 loss disallowance.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget the related-party transaction rules for partnerships. The exam exploits the misconception that a loss is always deductible. When a partner controls (more than 50% interest) the partnership, losses on sales to the partnership are disallowed.

questions.question: Partner A has a basis of $40,000 in their partnership interest. The partnership distributes $25,000 cash to Partner A. What is the tax consequence?
options: No gain recognized; basis reduced to $15,000
$15,000 capital gain; distribution exceeds basis after liabilities
$25,000 ordinary income; guaranteed payment exceeding basis
$25,000 capital gain; full distribution taxed at capital rate
