whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - At least 25% business use is not sufficient to claim accelerated depreciation on listed property. If business use is 50% or less, the straight-line depreciation method must be used.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - At least 75% business use, while exceeding the 50% threshold, is not the minimum requirement for claiming accelerated depreciation on listed property. The requirement is more than 50%, not at least 75%.

questions.educational: The listed property rules are designed to prevent taxpayers from abusing accelerated depreciation methods on assets that are primarily used for personal purposes. By requiring more than 50% business use, the IRS aims to ensure that the depreciation deduction reflects the actual business-related wear and tear of the asset. This is a key concept within the broader topic of depreciation and asset expensing, which is heavily tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember to carefully analyze the business use percentage of listed property on the EA exam, as it directly impacts the allowable depreciation method and potential recapture implications. Pay close attention to whether the question asks about 'more than' or 'at least'.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '50/50' – if business use is 50% or less, it's a '50/50' chance you'll have to use straight-line depreciation (and potentially recapture prior deductions)!
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the business use percentage required for accelerated depreciation with other thresholds related to business expenses. The exam exploits the misconception that any business use allows accelerated depreciation or that a very high percentage is required.

questions.question: For a passenger automobile placed in service in 2024, the first-year depreciation limit (including bonus) is approximately:
questions.explanation: The 2024 luxury auto limits with bonus depreciation: Year 1: $20,400 (vs. $12,400 without bonus), Year 2: $19,800, Year 3: $11,900, Subsequent years: $7,160. These limits apply to passenger autos regardless of cost.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $12,200 is close to the first-year depreciation limit *without* bonus depreciation, but the question specifies 'including bonus'. Remember to consider bonus depreciation when applicable.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - For passenger automobiles placed in service in 2024, the first-year depreciation limit, including bonus depreciation, is $20,400. This limit applies regardless of the vehicle's cost, unless the vehicle is used less than 50% for business purposes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $10,200 is not a recognized depreciation limit for passenger vehicles placed in service in 2024, either with or without bonus depreciation. It's important to memorize the correct limits.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $19,200 is not the correct first-year depreciation limit for passenger vehicles placed in service in 2024. While close to the correct amount, accuracy is crucial on the EA exam.

questions.educational: Depreciation limits on passenger automobiles are in place to prevent taxpayers from deducting excessive amounts for luxury vehicles. These limits are adjusted annually and are particularly relevant when advising clients on business expenses. Understanding these limits is crucial for accurate tax planning and compliance, a key role for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the year the asset was placed in service, as depreciation limits change annually. Also, be mindful of the bonus depreciation rules in effect for that year.
questions.memoryAid: Remember '20-20-10-7' (roughly) for the first four years of depreciation with bonus (in thousands). This is a simplified way to recall the approximate amounts for the first few years.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly remember the first-year depreciation limit for passenger automobiles. The exam exploits the fact that the limit changes annually and includes bonus depreciation, making it higher than the base depreciation limit. Candidates may recall older limits or confuse it with the standard depreciation amount.

questions.question: Which of the following businesses MUST use the accrual method of accounting?
options: An S corporation with $10 million average gross receipts
A sole proprietorship with $20 million average gross receipts
A C corporation with $35 million average gross receipts
A partnership with $15 million average gross receipts
questions.explanation: Generally, businesses with average annual gross receipts over $30 million (3-year average) must use accrual accounting. C corporations below this threshold and all small businesses can use cash method. The TCJA increased this threshold from $5 million.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations, regardless of their average gross receipts, are generally not required to use the accrual method unless they are considered tax shelters. The $30 million gross receipts test applies to C corporations, not S corporations, unless they are tax shelters.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Sole proprietorships, like other small businesses, can generally use the cash method of accounting if their average annual gross receipts for the three preceding tax years do not exceed $30 million. The fact that this sole proprietorship has $20 million in average gross receipts means it is eligible to use the cash method.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - C corporations with average annual gross receipts exceeding $30 million for the three preceding tax years are generally required to use the accrual method of accounting under IRC Section 448. This requirement ensures a more accurate reflection of income for larger businesses.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Partnerships, like sole proprietorships and S corporations, are generally allowed to use the cash method of accounting if their average annual gross receipts for the three preceding tax years do not exceed $30 million. The $15 million average gross receipts puts this partnership under the threshold.

questions.educational: The accrual method recognizes revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when cash changes hands. This method provides a more accurate picture of a business's financial performance over time, which is why it's required for larger C corporations. The $30 million threshold (indexed for inflation) is a key factor in determining whether a business must use the accrual method.
questions.examTip: Remember the $30 million average annual gross receipts test for determining whether a C corporation must use the accrual method; this is a frequently tested concept on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: C over 30: C corporations over $30 million generally must use accrual accounting.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the gross receipts threshold for mandatory accrual accounting. The exam exploits the misconception that all corporations or businesses above a certain size must use the accrual method, regardless of their structure. The rules are different for C corporations and other entities.

questions.question: For tax years after 2022, the deduction for business meals is:
options: 100% deductible
Not deductible
80% deductible
50% deductible
questions.explanation: Business meals are generally 50% deductible. The temporary 100% deduction for restaurant meals (2021-2022) has expired. Entertainment expenses remain disallowed under TCJA.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 100% deduction for business meals was a temporary provision that applied primarily to restaurant meals in 2021 and 2022 to help the restaurant industry during the pandemic. This provision has expired, and the standard deduction rules now apply.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While some expenses are completely disallowed (like entertainment), business meals are generally deductible, albeit with limitations. Claiming no deduction at all would be an incorrect application of the tax law.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - An 80% deduction for business meals is not a provision in the current tax law. The standard deduction is 50%, and the temporary 100% deduction has expired. This percentage is not supported by the IRC.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Generally, business meals are 50% deductible under IRC Section 274(n). This means that only half of the cost of the meal can be deducted as a business expense, provided other requirements are met (e.g., the meal is ordinary and necessary, not lavish or extravagant, and the taxpayer or an employee is present).

questions.educational: The 50% limitation on business meal deductions is a long-standing rule designed to prevent abuse and to reflect the personal element inherent in many business meals. This limitation is a key aspect of expense deductions, and understanding it is crucial for accurately determining taxable income for businesses. It connects to broader exam topics such as Schedule C, Form 1065, and Form 1120, where business expenses are reported.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates provided in the question. The temporary 100% deduction for restaurant meals can be a distractor if you don't carefully note the tax year being asked about.
questions.memoryAid: 50/50: Business meals are generally 50% deductible, like flipping a coin.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly remember the deduction percentage for business meals. The exam exploits the misconception that the temporary 100% deduction from 2021 and 2022 is still in effect or that meals are not deductible at all. The standard deduction is 50%.

questions.question: A new business incurs $55,000 in qualifying start-up costs. How much can be deducted in the first year?
options: $5,000 (immediate deduction allowed, balance amortized)
$0 (must amortize over 180 months)
$3,333 (deduction if electing to expense under Section 179)
$55,000 (full deduction allowed as a business expense)
questions.explanation: The $5,000 immediate deduction is reduced dollar-for-dollar when start-up costs exceed $50,000. With $55,000 in costs, the immediate deduction is $0 ($5,000 - $5,000 excess = $0). The full $55,000 must be amortized over 180 months.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a $5,000 immediate deduction *was* allowed in the past, current law requires amortization of all start-up costs. The remaining balance is not relevant as no immediate deduction is allowed.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC Section 195 allows a deduction for start-up expenditures, but it phases out when costs exceed $50,000. Because the $55,000 in start-up costs exceeds this threshold, the immediate deduction is reduced to $0, and the entire amount must be amortized over 180 months (15 years).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Section 179 expensing applies to depreciable tangible personal property, not start-up costs. Start-up costs are amortized, not expensed under Section 179.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Start-up costs are specifically required to be amortized over 180 months. They cannot be fully deducted as a current business expense.

questions.educational: Start-up costs are expenses incurred before a business begins operating. The IRS allows businesses to deduct a portion of these costs immediately and amortize the rest to provide tax relief during the initial, often less profitable, stages of operation. This treatment balances the need to encourage business formation with the principle of matching expenses to the income they generate over time, a core concept in tax accounting.
questions.examTip: Remember the $5,000 and $50,000 thresholds for start-up and organizational costs. A common trick on the EA exam is to present costs slightly above $50,000 to test your understanding of the phase-out.
questions.memoryAid: 5 and 50: $5,000 immediate deduction, phased out above $50,000 in start-up costs.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the treatment of start-up costs with other types of expenses. The exam exploits the misconception that all start-up costs are fully deductible in the first year or that a specific dollar amount can be deducted immediately, without considering the amortization rules. The $5,000 immediate deduction is reduced if total start-up costs exceed $50,000.

questions.question: For 2024, the Social Security wage base is:
questions.explanation: The 2024 Social Security wage base is $168,600. The employer and employee each pay 6.2% OASDI tax on wages up to this limit. Medicare tax (1.45%) has no wage limit.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $160,200 was the Social Security wage base for 2023, not 2024. It's important to keep up-to-date with the annual changes to these figures.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $147,000 was the Social Security wage base for 2022, not 2024. Using outdated information will lead to incorrect tax calculations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $176,100 is not the Social Security wage base for 2024. This value is incorrect and not published by the IRS or SSA.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The Social Security wage base for 2024 is $168,600. This is the maximum amount of earnings subject to the Social Security tax (OASDI), which is 6.2% for both the employer and the employee.

questions.educational: The Social Security wage base is adjusted annually to reflect changes in average wages. This limit ensures that higher-income earners contribute a substantial amount to the Social Security system while also capping the amount of their earnings subject to the tax. Understanding these annual adjustments is crucial for accurate payroll tax calculations and compliance, a key responsibility for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the tax year specified in the question, as tax limits and thresholds change annually. A question may try to trick you by using outdated information.
questions.memoryAid: Remember '168' for 2024's Social Security wage base to help recall $168,600.
questions.authorityRef: Social Security Administration Publication, Contribution and Benefit Base
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly remember the Social Security wage base. The exam exploits the fact that the wage base changes annually and candidates may recall a prior year's amount or confuse it with other figures related to payroll taxes. It's crucial to use the correct year's amount.

questions.question: Sarah is a single employee at Tech Solutions Inc. In 2024, she earns $280,000 in wages. What wage threshold triggers the 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax for Sarah?
options: $125,000 for all taxpayers
$250,000 for all taxpayers
questions.explanation: The 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax applies to wages and self-employment income exceeding $200,000 (single), $250,000 (MFJ), or $125,000 (MFS). These thresholds are not indexed for inflation.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax is triggered when an individual's wages, compensation, and self-employment income exceed specific thresholds based on their filing status. These thresholds are $200,000 for single filers and $250,000 for those married filing jointly. This option accurately reflects these thresholds.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The $125,000 threshold applies only to those married filing separately. Using this threshold for all taxpayers is incorrect because the Additional Medicare Tax thresholds vary based on filing status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The thresholds provided in this option ($400,000 single, $450,000 MFJ) are significantly higher than the actual thresholds for the Additional Medicare Tax. These amounts are incorrect and would not trigger the tax when they should.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While $250,000 is the threshold for married filing jointly, it is not the threshold for all taxpayers. Single filers have a lower threshold of $200,000, making this option incorrect for single individuals.

questions.educational: The Additional Medicare Tax was introduced to help fund the Affordable Care Act (ACA). It's crucial to understand that this tax is *in addition* to the regular 1.45% Medicare tax, and it's only levied on the portion of income exceeding the specified thresholds. This tax is reported on Form 8959 and is a key component of employment tax compliance, a significant area for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: Remember that the Additional Medicare Tax thresholds are *not* indexed for inflation, so the amounts remain constant from year to year unless Congress changes the law. This is a common trick on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '200 Single, 250 Joint' to quickly recall the Additional Medicare Tax thresholds for the most common filing statuses.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the income thresholds for the Additional Medicare Tax with other income-based tax provisions. The exam exploits the misconception that the threshold is the same for all taxpayers or that it's a higher amount. The single threshold is $200,000.

questions.question: A qualified small business (under $5 million gross receipts, less than 5 years old) with an R&D credit can apply it against:
options: The business's federal income tax liability only.
Payroll tax liability (up to $250,000 per year)
Only the business's quarterly estimated tax payments.
The business owner's self-employment tax liability only.
questions.explanation: Qualified small businesses (gross receipts <$5M in current year, no receipts in prior 5+ years) can elect to apply up to $250,000 of R&D credit against employer FICA taxes instead of income tax. This benefits startups with no income tax liability.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The R&D credit for qualified small businesses can be applied against payroll tax liability, not just income tax.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A qualified small business, meeting the gross receipts and age requirements, can elect to apply up to $250,000 of its R&D credit against the employer portion of FICA taxes (payroll tax). This election is made on Form 6765 and is particularly beneficial for startups that may not yet have significant income tax liability.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The R&D credit for qualified small businesses can be applied against payroll tax liability, not just estimated tax payments.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The R&D credit for qualified small businesses can be applied against payroll tax liability, not just self-employment tax.

questions.educational: The qualified small business R&D credit election aims to incentivize innovation by reducing the immediate tax burden on startups. By allowing the credit to offset payroll taxes, it provides a more immediate benefit to companies that may not yet be profitable. This aligns with the broader goal of encouraging economic growth and technological advancement, a key area of focus for tax policy and the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the gross receipts and age requirements for a qualified small business to claim the R&D credit against payroll tax. Be ready to calculate the amount of the credit that can be applied and where to report it on Form 6765.
questions.memoryAid: R&D for QSBS: Research & Development for Qualified Small Businesses = Payroll Tax Credit.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume the R&D credit can only be applied against income tax. The exam exploits the misconception that payroll tax credits are uncommon or that the R&D credit is exclusively for larger companies. The payroll tax election is specifically for qualified small businesses.

questions.question: Davis Consulting Inc. is a calendar-year corporation. They want to elect S corporation status for the 2024 tax year. What is the deadline for Davis Consulting to file Form 2553 to be treated as an S corporation for the entire year?
options: April 15, 2024
March 15, 2024
January 1, 2024
December 31, 2024
questions.explanation: To be effective for the current tax year, Form 2553 must be filed within 2 months and 15 days of the year's start (March 15 for calendar-year corps). Late elections may qualify for relief under Rev. Proc. 2013-30.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - April 15th is the due date for the corporate income tax return (Form 1120) for a calendar-year C corporation, not the S corporation election form (Form 2553). The S election must be made earlier in the year to be effective for the full year.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - To be effective for the 2024 tax year, a calendar-year corporation must file Form 2553 electing S corporation status by March 15, 2024. This deadline is two months and fifteen days after the beginning of the tax year, as prescribed by IRC Section 1362(b).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - January 1st is the very first day of the tax year, and while a corporation *could* file Form 2553 on this date, it is not the deadline. The deadline allows for some time after the tax year begins to make the election.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - December 31, 2024, is the last day of the tax year, and filing Form 2553 on this date would be too late to elect S corporation status for 2024. The election must be made *before* the end of the tax year to be effective for that year.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a corporation to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders, avoiding double taxation at the corporate level. This election is made by filing Form 2553, and the timing of the filing is crucial for the election to be effective for the desired tax year. Understanding the deadlines for various elections is a key aspect of business taxation covered in the SEE2 exam blueprint.
questions.examTip: Remember that the S corporation election deadline is generally 2 months and 15 days after the beginning of the tax year. Be prepared to calculate this deadline for both calendar-year and fiscal-year corporations on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'March Madness' for the March 15th deadline for calendar-year S-corp elections.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume the deadline is the same as the individual tax return deadline (April 15th) or the corporate return deadline (March 15th of the *following* year). The key is understanding the 'prior year' rule and the 'two months and fifteen days' rule for timely S-election filing. Test-takers often forget that the election needs to be made *before* the tax year begins if they want it to be effective for the entire year.

questions.question: An S corporation net income is $200,000 and the shareholder-employee takes $40,000 salary. The IRS will likely:
options: Reclassify distributions as wages subject to employment taxes
Revoke the S election due to the shareholder-employee not taking a reasonable salary, thus violating S corporation requirements.
Accept this as reasonable compensation if the shareholder-employee can demonstrate that the distributions were not intended to avoid employment taxes.
Disallow all business deductions related to the shareholder-employee's compensation and distributions, treating them as personal expenses.
questions.explanation: The IRS scrutinizes S corp reasonable compensation. If salaries are too low relative to profits and work performed, distributions may be recharacterized as wages, resulting in back taxes, penalties, and interest on unpaid FICA.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The IRS is concerned about S corporation shareholder-employees taking distributions instead of reasonable compensation to avoid payroll taxes (Social Security and Medicare). In this case, a $40,000 salary for $200,000 of S corporation net income is highly suspect and likely to be challenged as insufficient, leading the IRS to reclassify a portion of the distributions as wages subject to employment taxes.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While an unreasonably low salary *could* lead to scrutiny, the IRS is more likely to reclassify distributions than immediately revoke the S election. Revocation is a more extreme measure.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The IRS is unlikely to accept this as reasonable compensation given the disparity between salary and net income. They will likely view it as an attempt to avoid employment taxes, regardless of intent.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The IRS would not disallow *all* business deductions. They would focus on reclassifying the distributions as wages subject to employment taxes, not disallowing legitimate business expenses.

questions.educational: The IRS requires S corporations to pay shareholder-employees 'reasonable compensation' for services rendered before any distributions are taken. This rule prevents S corporations from avoiding payroll taxes (Social Security and Medicare) by paying shareholder-employees primarily through distributions, which are not subject to these taxes. Understanding reasonable compensation is crucial for the SEE exam, as it directly impacts shareholder basis, taxable income, and potential penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, always consider the IRS's perspective: they want to ensure all applicable taxes are paid. When you see a large S corporation profit with a small shareholder-employee salary, flag it as a potential reasonable compensation issue.
questions.memoryAid: R.A.T. - Reasonable compensation Avoids Taxes.
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers incorrectly assume that any amount paid to a shareholder-employee is acceptable as long as the S corporation has sufficient income. The IRS scrutinizes situations where distributions are disproportionately high compared to salary, as this avoids employment taxes. Some might think the IRS would revoke the S election, but that's a more drastic measure usually reserved for more severe violations.

questions.question: A partner receives a guaranteed payment of $60,000 for services rendered to the partnership. The partnership agreement specifies this amount regardless of partnership income. How should this guaranteed payment be reported on the partner's individual tax return?
options: As self-employment income on Schedule SE and as ordinary income on Schedule E
As wage income on Form W-2, reported on Schedule C as a reduction to business income, and subject to payroll taxes.
As a distribution reducing the partner's basis with no current tax impact
As capital gain income reported on Schedule D, assuming the payment represents a distribution of partnership assets exceeding the partner's basis.
questions.explanation: Guaranteed payments are treated as ordinary income to the receiving partner and are subject to self-employment tax under IRC §707(c). They are reported on Schedule E as part of partnership income and on Schedule SE for self-employment tax purposes. Unlike distributions, guaranteed payments are deductible by the partnership and taxable to the partner regardless of partnership profitability.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Guaranteed payments to a partner for services are treated as if the partner were not a member of the partnership, per IRC §707(c). This means the payment is ordinary income to the partner, reported on Schedule E, and subject to self-employment tax, reported on Schedule SE.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are not wage income and are not reported on Form W-2. They are self-employment income.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Distributions reduce a partner's basis, but guaranteed payments are treated differently. Guaranteed payments are taxable income to the partner regardless of the partnership's profitability and do not simply reduce basis like a typical distribution of partnership profits or capital.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are for services, not a distribution of partnership assets, and are therefore not treated as capital gains.

questions.educational: Guaranteed payments ensure partners are compensated for their services or capital contributions, even if the partnership has a loss or insufficient income. This treatment aligns with the concept of 'separateness' in partnership taxation, where partners are sometimes treated as distinct from the partnership for specific transactions. Understanding guaranteed payments is crucial for accurately calculating a partner's taxable income and self-employment tax liability, a key area in partnership taxation.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of partnership questions. Distinguish between guaranteed payments, distributions, and capital contributions, as each has a different tax impact on the partner and the partnership.
questions.memoryAid: Guaranteed Payments = Ordinary Income + Self-Employment Tax (GOSE)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse guaranteed payments with distributions or wages. Guaranteed payments are treated as self-employment income, subject to self-employment tax, and also as ordinary income. A common mistake is to think it's just a distribution that reduces basis or wage income reported on a W-2.

questions.question: Apex Manufacturing is a C corporation. What is the applicable corporate income tax rate for Apex Manufacturing's taxable income?
options: Progressive rates from 15% to 35%
Flat 28%
Flat 21%
Flat 25%
questions.explanation: The TCJA established a flat 21% corporate tax rate for C corporations, replacing the graduated rate structure (15%-35%) that existed before 2018.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The progressive tax rates for C corporations (ranging from 15% to 35%) were eliminated by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017, effective for tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. This graduated rate structure is no longer in effect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A flat 28% corporate tax rate has never been the standard rate for C corporations. While certain specialized entities or situations might involve a 28% rate, it's not the general corporate income tax rate.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017 established a flat corporate income tax rate of 21% for C corporations. This flat rate applies to all taxable income of the corporation, simplifying tax calculations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A flat 25% corporate tax rate is not the current federal income tax rate for C corporations. The TCJA set the rate at 21%, not 25%.

questions.educational: The flat 21% corporate tax rate was implemented to encourage economic growth by reducing the tax burden on businesses. This change impacts how C corporations calculate their tax liability and affects their overall financial planning. Understanding this rate is crucial for accurately preparing corporate tax returns and advising clients on tax strategies.
questions.examTip: Remember that the flat 21% corporate tax rate simplifies tax planning for C corporations, but you must still be aware of other deductions and credits that can further reduce their tax liability, such as the dividends received deduction.
questions.memoryAid: C-Corps = 21% (Think: C-Corps can drink at 21!)
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers are unaware that the corporate tax rate is a flat 21%. They might recall older, graduated tax rate systems for corporations or confuse it with individual tax rates. The exam exploits the misconception that corporate tax rates are complex and progressive.

questions.question: Jane is a shareholder in Bright Future LLC, an S corporation. Which of the following items increases Jane's basis in her S corporation stock?
options: Guaranteed payments received and related expense deductions
Distributions received, regardless of retained earnings balance
Pass-through income and additional capital contributions
Company losses, even if exceeding Jane's at-risk amount
questions.explanation: S corp shareholder basis increases for: income (including tax-exempt), capital contributions. Basis decreases for: distributions, losses/deductions, non-deductible expenses. S corps don't have guaranteed payments.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are treated as compensation and do not affect stock basis; they are reported separately. The related expense deduction would decrease basis, not increase it.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Distributions reduce basis, but only to the extent of basis. Distributions exceeding basis are generally treated as capital gains, not a further reduction of basis.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An S corporation shareholder's basis is increased by their share of the corporation's pass-through income (both taxable and tax-exempt) and by any additional capital contributions they make to the corporation. These items represent an increase in the shareholder's investment and economic risk in the S corporation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Losses can only be deducted to the extent of a shareholder's basis and at-risk amount. Losses exceeding the at-risk amount are suspended, not used to reduce basis.

questions.educational: The basis of an S corporation shareholder's stock is crucial for determining the taxability of distributions and the deductibility of losses. Basis is increased by contributions and income and decreased by distributions and losses. This concept is similar to partnership basis rules, but it's important to distinguish between guaranteed payments (partnerships) and salary (S corps). Understanding basis is essential for accurately calculating taxable income and loss for S corporation shareholders, a key area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the mnemonic COLA-D to help you remember what affects basis: Contributions and Ordinary Income increase basis, Losses and Distributions decrease basis. This is a high-frequency topic on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: COLA-D: Contributions and Ordinary Income increase basis, Losses and Distributions decrease basis.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse items that increase basis with items that decrease basis. Pass-through income and capital contributions increase basis, while distributions and losses decrease it. Guaranteed payments are not directly related to stock basis; they are compensation. The exam exploits the misconception that all payments from the S corporation affect basis in the same way.

questions.question: John is a partner in ABC Partnership. In what order must John reduce his partnership basis for distributions, nondeductible expenses, and losses?
options: Distributions, nondeductible expenses, then losses in that order
Losses, then distributions, then nondeductible expenses
Distributions, then losses, then nondeductible expenses
Nondeductible expenses, then distributions, then losses
questions.explanation: Partner basis is reduced in this order: (1) distributions (to extent of basis), (2) nondeductible, non-capitalized expenditures, (3) deductible losses. This ordering matters when basis is limited.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A partner's basis is reduced in a specific order to ensure accurate tracking of their investment and prevent double-counting of losses. Distributions are taken first, then nondeductible expenses, and finally deductible losses, reflecting the priority of withdrawals and economic burdens.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Losses are not the first item to reduce basis. Distributions take precedence because they represent an actual withdrawal of capital from the partnership.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Nondeductible expenses reduce basis after distributions and losses. Nondeductible expenses, while not currently deductible, represent an economic cost to the partner and reduce their investment.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Nondeductible expenses are not the first item to reduce basis. Distributions take precedence as they represent a return of capital to the partner.

questions.educational: The ordering rules for basis reductions in a partnership are crucial for determining a partner's taxable gain or loss upon the sale of their partnership interest or upon distributions exceeding their basis. This ordering is designed to prevent partners from deducting losses in excess of their economic investment and to accurately reflect the tax consequences of partnership operations and distributions. Understanding these rules is essential for accurate tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: Remember the basis ordering rules! On the EA exam, pay close attention to scenarios involving distributions, nondeductible expenses, and losses to correctly calculate a partner's adjusted basis.
questions.memoryAid: D-N-L: Distributions, Nondeductible Expenses, Losses (reduces basis in that order).
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers struggle with the ordering rules for basis adjustments. Distributions are always taken into account *before* losses. Nondeductible expenses also reduce basis before losses. The exam exploits the misconception that losses are always the first item to reduce basis.

questions.question: Mark is an employee of a small business that sponsors a SIMPLE IRA plan. What is the maximum amount Mark can contribute to his SIMPLE IRA in 2024?
questions.explanation: The 2024 SIMPLE IRA contribution limit is $16,000 ($19,500 with catch-up for age 50+). This is lower than the 401(k) limit of $23,000 but higher than the Traditional/Roth IRA limit of $7,000.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $6,500 is significantly lower than the actual SIMPLE IRA contribution limit for 2024. This amount is closer to previous years' IRA contribution limits, but not for a SIMPLE IRA.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $15,500 is close to the correct answer, but it's not the exact maximum employee contribution amount for a SIMPLE IRA in 2024. Always double-check the specific dollar amounts for retirement plan contributions, as they change annually.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $23,000 is the maximum employee contribution limit for 401(k) plans in 2024, not for SIMPLE IRAs. It's crucial to differentiate between the contribution limits for various retirement plans.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The maximum employee contribution to a SIMPLE IRA for 2024 is $16,000. Employees age 50 and over can also make a catch-up contribution, increasing their maximum contribution to $19,500. SIMPLE IRAs are designed to be easy-to-administer retirement savings plans for small businesses.

questions.educational: SIMPLE IRAs are retirement plans specifically designed for small businesses and self-employed individuals. They offer a simplified approach to retirement savings compared to more complex plans like 401(k)s. Understanding the contribution limits for SIMPLE IRAs, SEP IRAs, and other retirement plans is crucial for advising small business clients on the most suitable retirement savings options and ensuring compliance with IRS regulations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the year specified in the question, as retirement plan contribution limits are adjusted annually by the IRS. Memorize the key limits for SIMPLE IRAs, 401(k)s, and traditional/Roth IRAs for the relevant tax year.
questions.memoryAid: SIMPLE: Sixteen (thousand) for standard contributions in 2024.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the contribution limits for different retirement plans. The SIMPLE IRA limit for 2024 is $16,000 (plus a catch-up contribution if eligible). The exam exploits the misconception that all IRA-type plans have similar contribution limits.

questions.question: Susan is self-employed and contributes to a SEP-IRA. What is the maximum amount Susan can contribute to her SEP-IRA for 2024?
options: $6,500 or 100% of compensation
$23,000 or 100% of compensation
$69,000 or 25% of compensation
$66,000 or 25% of compensation
questions.explanation: For 2024, SEP-IRA contributions are limited to the lesser of $69,000 or 25% of the employee's compensation. Self-employed individuals calculate this based on net SE income after the SE tax adjustment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $6,500 is the maximum contribution for a Roth or Traditional IRA for those under age 50 in 2023, not a SEP-IRA. The SEP-IRA has a much higher contribution limit designed for self-employed individuals and small business owners.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $23,000 is the maximum employee contribution for elective deferrals to 401(k), 403(b), and 457(b) plans for 2024, not a SEP-IRA. Confusing these limits is a common exam trick.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - For 2024, the maximum SEP-IRA contribution is indeed the lesser of $69,000 or 25% of the participant's compensation. This allows self-employed individuals and small businesses to contribute a significant portion of their income to retirement.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the 25% of compensation component is correct, the dollar limit is incorrect. The maximum SEP-IRA contribution for 2024 is $69,000, not $66,000. Using outdated figures is a common trap on the exam.

questions.educational: SEP-IRAs are designed to allow self-employed individuals and small business owners to save for retirement in a tax-advantaged way. The contribution limit is higher than traditional or Roth IRAs to compensate for the lack of employer-sponsored retirement plans. Understanding the nuances of self-employment tax and retirement plan options is crucial for advising clients on tax planning strategies.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the year specified in the question, as retirement plan contribution limits are adjusted annually for inflation. Make a quick reference sheet of key limits for the current and prior year to avoid easy mistakes.
questions.memoryAid: SEP = Substantial Earnings Potential (helps remember the higher contribution limits compared to regular IRAs)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the contribution limits for different retirement plans, especially SEP IRAs and 401(k)s. The SEP-IRA limit is 25% of compensation up to a maximum dollar amount ($69,000 for 2024). The exam exploits the misconception that all self-employment retirement plans have the same contribution rules.

questions.question: David owns a small business and wants to deduct losses. To meet the material participation test for his business, which single test, if met, is sufficient for David to deduct losses?
options: Participating more than 100 hours
Owning more than 10% of the activity
Participating in the activity for any amount of time
Participating more than 500 hours during the year
questions.explanation: One way to meet material participation is participating in the activity for more than 500 hours during the year. There are 7 tests total, including the 100-hour/no one else more test and 5 of last 10 years test.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Participating more than 100 hours is not, by itself, sufficient to meet the material participation test. While it is a component of one of the seven tests (the 'significant participation activity' test), it requires meeting other conditions, such as no other individual participating more.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Owning more than 10% of the activity is irrelevant to the material participation test. The test focuses solely on the number of hours spent actively involved in the business operations, not ownership percentage.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Participating in the activity for any amount of time is insufficient to meet the material participation test. The IRS requires a significant level of involvement, measured in hours, to qualify as material participation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Participating more than 500 hours during the year is one of the seven tests that, if met, automatically qualifies as material participation according to IRS regulations. This ensures the taxpayer is actively involved in the business operations.

questions.educational: The material participation tests are designed to distinguish between active business owners and passive investors. The IRS provides seven tests to determine material participation, ensuring that only those genuinely involved in the business's operations can deduct losses against non-passive income. Understanding these tests is crucial for accurately classifying income and losses on Form 1040 Schedule E and Form 8582.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the 500-hour rule is the most straightforward way to demonstrate material participation. If a client meets this test, you don't need to analyze the other six tests.
questions.memoryAid: 500 Hours = Material Power
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unfamiliar with the specific material participation tests. While there are several tests, participating more than 500 hours is a standalone test that, if met, automatically qualifies as material participation. The exam exploits the misconception that all tests require a combination of factors.

questions.question: The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC) is generally what percentage of first-year wages for employees working 400+ hours?
options: 40% of up to $6,000 wages
25% of up to $6,000 wages
100% of first $3,000 wages
50% of up to $10,000 wages
questions.explanation: WOTC is 40% of the first $6,000 of qualified first-year wages ($2,400 max) for employees working 400+ hours. For 120-399 hours, it's 25% of $6,000 ($1,500 max). Higher amounts apply for certain targeted groups.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC) incentivizes employers to hire individuals from specific target groups who face significant barriers to employment. For employees who work at least 400 hours, the employer can claim a credit equal to 40% of the first $6,000 in wages paid to that employee, resulting in a maximum credit of $2,400.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While 25% is a WOTC percentage, it applies to employees who work at least 120 hours but less than 400 hours. This option is incorrect because the question specifically asks about employees working 400+ hours.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The WOTC does not provide a credit of 100% of the first $3,000 in wages. This percentage and wage base are not associated with the general WOTC rules, although some targeted groups may have different wage bases.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The WOTC uses a wage base of $6,000 for most eligible employees, not $10,000. While certain targeted groups, such as qualified veterans, may have a higher wage base ($14,000 or $24,000), the general rule is $6,000.

questions.educational: The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC) is a federal tax credit designed to encourage employers to hire individuals from certain targeted groups, such as veterans, ex-felons, and individuals receiving government assistance. By providing a tax incentive, the government aims to reduce unemployment and promote economic opportunity for these individuals. Understanding business credits like WOTC is crucial for EA candidates as it directly impacts tax planning and compliance for business clients.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific requirements for each tax credit, including the eligible employee categories, wage limitations, and minimum work hour requirements, as these details are frequently tested.
questions.memoryAid: WOTC: 40% for 400+ hours (of $6,000 wages).
questions.commonMistake: Many test-takers are unaware of the specific percentage and wage base for the Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC). The WOTC is generally 40% of the first $6,000 in wages paid to eligible employees who work at least 400 hours. The exam exploits the misconception that the percentage or wage base is different.

questions.question: David Miller is deciding between forming his new consulting firm as a C corporation or an S corporation. He anticipates initial losses. Which of the following is an advantage of S corporation status over C corporation status for David?
options: Shareholders can be nonresident aliens
Ability to have multiple classes of stock
Unlimited number of shareholders
Avoidance of double taxation
questions.explanation: S corporations pass income through to shareholders, avoiding the double taxation of C corps (corporate tax + dividend tax). However, S corps are limited to 100 shareholders, one class of stock, and only U.S. citizens/residents.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are restricted in who can be shareholders. Specifically, nonresident aliens are not permitted to be shareholders in an S corporation. This restriction is in place to simplify tax administration and ensure proper reporting of income to the IRS.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - S corporations are limited to only one class of stock. This simplifies the allocation of income, losses, deductions, and credits among shareholders. C corporations, on the other hand, can have multiple classes of stock with varying rights and preferences.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - S corporations are limited to a maximum of 100 shareholders. C corporations do not have this restriction, making them more suitable for businesses anticipating a large number of investors.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The primary advantage of an S corporation is the avoidance of double taxation. Unlike C corporations, which are taxed at the corporate level and again when dividends are distributed to shareholders, S corporations pass their income, losses, deductions, and credits through to their shareholders' individual income tax returns. This pass-through taxation avoids the double taxation inherent in the C corporation structure.

questions.educational: The choice of entity is a critical decision with significant tax implications. S corporations offer pass-through taxation, avoiding double taxation, but come with restrictions on shareholder type, number, and stock classes. C corporations, while subject to double taxation, offer greater flexibility in these areas, making the optimal choice dependent on the specific circumstances and long-term goals of the business. Understanding these differences is crucial for advising clients on entity selection.
questions.examTip: When comparing S corps and C corps on the EA exam, always remember the 'double taxation' keyword for C corps and the shareholder restrictions for S corps. These are frequently tested points.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp = Single Taxation, Simple Structure (one class of stock).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly believe that S corporations can have nonresident alien shareholders. A common trap is overlooking the strict eligibility requirements for S corporations, particularly regarding shareholder status. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that the advantages of pass-through taxation outweigh the restrictions on ownership.

questions.question: Partner Adams contributes $50,000 cash and property with a basis of $20,000 (FMV $35,000) to a new partnership in exchange for a 40% interest. No liabilities are assumed. What is Adams' initial basis in the partnership interest?
questions.explanation: A partner's initial basis in a partnership interest equals the amount of cash contributed plus the adjusted basis of contributed property. Under IRC §722, basis = $50,000 cash + $20,000 property basis = $70,000. The FMV of the property ($35,000) is not used — the partner takes a carryover basis in contributed property. No gain or loss is recognized on the contribution under §721.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A partner's initial basis in a partnership interest is calculated by summing the cash contributed and the adjusted basis of any contributed property. In this case, Adams' basis is $50,000 (cash) + $20,000 (property basis) = $70,000.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This option incorrectly adds the fair market value of the contributed property to the cash contribution. The partner's basis in the partnership interest only includes the adjusted basis of the contributed property, not its fair market value.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This option only considers the cash contribution and ignores the basis of the contributed property. The adjusted basis of the property ($20,000) must be included when calculating the partner's initial basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option only considers the fair market value of the contributed property and ignores the cash contribution. Furthermore, it incorrectly uses the fair market value of the property instead of its adjusted basis.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation aims to reflect the economic reality of the partners' contributions and distributions. The carryover basis rule for contributed property ensures that any built-in gain or loss (difference between FMV and adjusted basis at contribution) is taxed to the contributing partner when the partnership disposes of the property. This aligns with the conduit nature of partnerships, where income and losses flow through to the partners.
questions.examTip: Remember that contributions of property to a partnership are generally non-taxable events under IRC §721, and the partner's basis in the partnership interest is determined by the adjusted basis of the contributed property, not its fair market value. This is a frequently tested concept on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: CASH + BASIS = PARTNERSHIP BASIS (Cash contributed plus the adjusted basis of contributed property equals the partner's initial basis in the partnership).
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is forgetting that a partner's initial basis includes both cash and the adjusted basis of property contributed. Many candidates incorrectly use the fair market value of the contributed property instead of its adjusted basis. Test-takers often incorrectly add the FMV of the property instead of the adjusted basis.

questions.question: Precision Manufacturing Inc. is a C corporation. What is the corporate income tax rate under current law (TCJA) for Precision Manufacturing?
questions.explanation: TCJA established a flat 21% corporate tax rate effective 2018, replacing the graduated rates that went up to 35%. This rate applies to all C corporation taxable income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017 established a flat corporate income tax rate of 21% for C corporations, effective for tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. This single rate applies to all levels of corporate taxable income, simplifying the tax calculation process.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 35% was the highest marginal corporate income tax rate before the TCJA of 2017. This rate applied to the highest levels of corporate income under the graduated rate system that was in place prior to 2018, but it is no longer the applicable rate.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 15% is not the current corporate income tax rate. While certain pass-through entities may have income taxed at individual rates, which could be lower than 21% due to deductions or credits, C corporations are subject to the flat 21% rate.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 28% was a previous corporate income tax rate that applied to certain income levels under the graduated rate system before the TCJA. This rate is not applicable under current law.

questions.educational: The corporate income tax is levied on the profits of C corporations, which are considered separate legal entities from their owners. Understanding the current flat 21% rate is crucial for calculating a corporation's tax liability and advising clients on tax planning strategies. This contrasts with pass-through entities like S corporations and partnerships, where income is taxed at the individual owner's level.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 21% corporate tax rate applies to C corporations only. Be prepared to differentiate between the tax treatment of C corporations and pass-through entities on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: C-Corps = 21% (Think: C the rate!)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates may recall previous corporate tax rates and incorrectly apply them. The exam exploits the misconception that corporate tax rates are complex and tiered. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that the corporate tax rate is progressive.

questions.question: Sunrise Tech Corp. converted from a C corporation to an S corporation on January 1, Year 1. In Year 3, it sells a building that had a fair market value of $750,000 and a basis of $500,000 on the conversion date. The built-in gains tax applies to an S corporation that:
options: Makes distributions to shareholders exceeding its Accumulated Adjustments Account and earnings and profits.
Has always been an S corporation and now recognizes a gain on the sale of property acquired many years ago.
Converted from C corp and sells appreciated assets within 5 years
Has passive investment income exceeding 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive tax years and prior C corp E&P.
questions.explanation: The built-in gains (BIG) tax applies when a C corp converts to S corp and sells assets with built-in gain within the recognition period (5 years). The tax is imposed at the corporate level at 21%.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Distributions exceeding AAA may have tax consequences for shareholders, but do not trigger the built-in gains tax.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The built-in gains tax only applies to S corporations that converted from C corporations, not those that were always S corporations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The built-in gains tax, under IRC Section 1374, is designed to prevent C corporations from avoiding corporate-level tax on appreciated assets by electing S corporation status and then selling those assets. If a C corporation converts to an S corporation and sells assets that had a built-in gain (the difference between the asset's fair market value and its adjusted basis on the date of conversion) within the five-year recognition period, the S corporation is subject to the built-in gains tax at the corporate tax rate (currently 21%).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Excessive passive investment income can trigger a corporate-level tax and potential S-corp termination, but not the built-in gains tax.

questions.educational: The built-in gains tax is a corporate-level tax imposed on S corporations that were formerly C corporations to prevent them from avoiding corporate tax on appreciated assets. The tax is calculated on the recognized built-in gain, which is the gain that existed at the time of conversion from C corp to S corp. This rule ensures that the appreciation that occurred while the entity was a C corporation is taxed at the corporate level, even after the S election.
questions.examTip: Remember that the built-in gains tax only applies to S corporations that converted from C corporations. Pay close attention to the dates of conversion and sale of assets when analyzing built-in gains tax questions on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: BIG Tax: C to S, Sell Soon (within 5 years).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the built-in gains tax with other S corporation rules. A common trap is overlooking the specific conditions that trigger the built-in gains tax. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that any sale of appreciated assets by a former C corporation is subject to the built-in gains tax, regardless of the timeframe.

questions.question: Jane is a partner in ABC Partnership. Which of the following increases Jane's basis in her partnership interest?
options: Partnership distributions of cash
Tax-exempt expenses of the partnership
Share of partnership liabilities assumed
Partnership losses allocated to the partner
questions.explanation: Partner basis is increased by contributions, share of income (including tax-exempt income), and share of partnership liabilities. Distributions, losses, and nondeductible expenses decrease basis.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Partnership distributions of cash represent a return of capital to the partner. This reduces the partner's basis in the partnership interest, reflecting the decreased investment.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While tax-exempt income increases a partner's basis, tax-exempt *expenses* are nondeductible and decrease a partner's basis. These expenses reduce the partner's economic investment without providing a corresponding tax benefit.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A partner's basis in a partnership interest includes their share of partnership liabilities. Under IRC Section 752, an increase in a partner's share of partnership liabilities, or an increase in a partner's individual liabilities by reason of the assumption by such partner of partnership liabilities, is treated as a contribution of money by such partner to the partnership, thereby increasing their basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Partnership losses allocated to a partner reduce the partner's basis in the partnership interest. These losses represent a decrease in the partner's investment in the partnership.

questions.educational: A partner's basis in their partnership interest is crucial for determining the tax consequences of distributions, sales of the partnership interest, and the deductibility of partnership losses. The basis is adjusted annually to reflect contributions, income, losses, and distributions. Understanding basis adjustments is essential for accurate tax reporting and planning related to partnerships, a key area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember to consider both increases and decreases to basis when calculating a partner's adjusted basis. Pay close attention to whether an item is income, expense, contribution, distribution, or liability-related, as each impacts basis differently.
questions.memoryAid: C.L.A.D. - Contributions and Liabilities Add; Distributions subtract.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse items that increase and decrease a partner's basis. A common trap is overlooking that partnership liabilities increase a partner's basis. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that only contributions increase basis, while distributions and losses decrease it.

questions.question: Global Widgets Inc. uses LIFO for tax purposes. Which of the following statements is true regarding Global Widgets' inventory accounting?
options: Also use LIFO for financial reporting
File Form 970 annually
Use only specific identification for sales
Maintain separate inventory for tax and book
questions.explanation: The LIFO conformity rule requires businesses using LIFO for tax purposes to also use LIFO for financial reporting (with limited exceptions). Form 970 is only filed in the year of LIFO election.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The LIFO conformity rule, as mandated by IRC Section 472(c), requires that if a taxpayer uses the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) inventory method for tax purposes, they must also use it for financial reporting to shareholders and creditors. This ensures consistency and prevents taxpayers from selectively using LIFO for tax benefits while presenting a different picture to investors.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form 970, Application to Use LIFO Inventory Method, is only filed once, in the year the taxpayer elects to use the LIFO inventory method; it is not an annual filing. After the initial election, the taxpayer continues using LIFO unless they obtain permission from the IRS to change methods.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The LIFO method is an alternative to specific identification, not a requirement to use it for sales. Specific identification involves tracking the actual cost of each item sold, while LIFO assumes the last items purchased are the first ones sold.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While detailed record-keeping is essential for LIFO, the requirement is not to maintain entirely separate inventories for tax and book purposes. The LIFO conformity rule necessitates that the same inventory valuation method (LIFO) is used for both, although supplementary records may be needed for tax calculations.

questions.educational: The LIFO conformity rule is a cornerstone of inventory accounting for tax purposes. It prevents companies from manipulating their reported income by using different inventory methods for financial reporting and tax returns. Understanding this rule is crucial for the SEE exam, as it directly impacts how businesses calculate their taxable income and comply with IRS regulations.
questions.examTip: Remember the LIFO conformity rule! If a question mentions LIFO for tax, immediately consider whether the financial reporting also uses LIFO. This is a common trap on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: LIFO = Like For Others (financial reporting)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the LIFO conformity rule. A common trap is overlooking the requirement to use LIFO for financial reporting if it's used for tax purposes. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that companies can use different inventory methods for tax and book purposes.

questions.question: Green Acres Landscaping had average annual gross receipts of $28 million for the past three years. Under the simplified small business exemption, which businesses can use the cash method of accounting?
options: Businesses with average annual gross receipts of $30 million or less
All businesses regardless of size
Only sole proprietorships
Businesses with average annual gross receipts of $25 million or less
questions.explanation: The TCJA raised the gross receipts threshold to $25M (indexed to ~$30M for 2024). Businesses meeting this test can use cash method, simplified inventory, and are exempt from UNICAP and percentage of completion.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The simplified small business exemption, as defined under IRC Section 448(c), allows businesses with average annual gross receipts of $30 million or less (indexed for inflation) for the three prior tax years to use the cash method of accounting. This simplifies their tax reporting and compliance burden.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the desire for simplicity is universal, the cash method is not available to all businesses regardless of size. IRC Section 448(a) generally prohibits C corporations and partnerships with C corporation partners from using the cash method unless they meet the gross receipts test.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While sole proprietorships can use the cash method if they meet the gross receipts test, the exemption is not limited to them. Other business entities, such as S corporations and partnerships (without C corporation partners), can also use the cash method if they meet the gross receipts test under IRC Section 448(c).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The gross receipts threshold for the simplified small business exemption is $30 million (indexed for inflation), not $25 million. Using the incorrect threshold would lead to an incorrect determination of eligibility for the cash method of accounting.

questions.educational: The cash method of accounting recognizes revenue when cash is received and expenses when cash is paid. This is simpler than the accrual method, which recognizes revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash flow. The simplified small business exemption aims to reduce the compliance burden for smaller businesses, allowing them to use a simpler accounting method and avoid complex rules like UNICAP and percentage of completion.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the gross receipts threshold for the simplified small business exemption, as it is indexed for inflation and may change annually. Always use the most current threshold provided in the exam materials or IRS guidance.
questions.memoryAid: 30 Million or Less = Cash Method Access
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the gross receipts test for using the cash method. A common trap is overlooking the specific threshold for average annual gross receipts. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that all small businesses can use the cash method, regardless of their revenue.

questions.question: During 2024, Smith & Jones Law Firm incurred expenses for business meals with clients. For 2024, business meals with clients are deductible at:
questions.explanation: Business meals are 50% deductible (the temporary 100% deduction for restaurant meals expired after 2022). Entertainment expenses remain 0% deductible under TCJA. Food at entertainment events is 50% if stated separately.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Business meals are generally deductible at 50% for 2024. This limitation is in place to prevent abuse and ensure that deductions are reasonable and directly related to business activities, per IRS guidelines.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 100% deduction for business meals at restaurants was a temporary provision that expired after 2022; it does not apply to 2024. Claiming a 100% deduction would be an overstatement of allowable expenses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While entertainment expenses are generally not deductible, business meals are still partially deductible. Completely disallowing business meal deductions would be incorrect under current tax law.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no provision in the tax code that allows for a 75% deduction for business meals. The standard deduction for business meals is 50%.

questions.educational: The 50% limitation on business meal deductions stems from the desire to balance allowing legitimate business expenses with preventing abuse and personal consumption disguised as business. This limitation impacts various business entities, including sole proprietorships, partnerships, and corporations, and is a common area tested on the SEE exam to assess your understanding of deductible business expenses.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the tax year specified in the question, as temporary provisions like the 100% restaurant meal deduction can significantly impact the correct answer. Always double-check the current year's rules for meal and entertainment expenses.
questions.memoryAid: 50/50: Business meals are deductible at 50%.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the current deduction limit for business meals. A common trap is overlooking the specific percentage allowed for business meal deductions. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that business meals are fully deductible.

questions.question: John started his new business, "John's Bakery," in 2024. A business can deduct up to how much in start-up costs in the year the business begins?
questions.explanation: Up to $5,000 of start-up costs (and separately up to $5,000 of organizational costs) can be deducted in the first year. The $5,000 is reduced by costs exceeding $50,000. Remaining costs are amortized over 180 months.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $25,000 is not the correct amount for the immediate deduction of start-up costs. While this number might seem familiar in the context of other deductions, it does not apply to start-up expenses.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $10,000 is not the correct amount for the immediate deduction of start-up costs. The immediate deduction is capped at $5,000, subject to certain limitations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC Section 195 allows a business to deduct up to $5,000 of start-up costs in the year the business begins operations. This deduction is reduced if total start-up costs exceed $50,000. Any remaining start-up costs are then amortized over 180 months (15 years).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $15,000 is not the correct amount for the immediate deduction of start-up costs. The maximum immediate deduction is $5,000, with the excess amortized.

questions.educational: Start-up costs are expenses incurred before a business begins operations. Congress allows for a current deduction of a portion of these costs to ease the financial burden on new businesses. The amortization of the remaining costs recognizes that these expenses provide a benefit over multiple years, aligning with the matching principle of accounting and tax law.
questions.examTip: Remember to distinguish between start-up costs and organizational costs, as both have a $5,000 immediate deduction limit and a $50,000 threshold for reducing the deduction; be prepared to calculate the deductible amount and the amortization expense.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '5 and 50' - $5,000 immediate deduction, reduced if costs exceed $50,000.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the specific dollar limitation for deducting start-up costs. A common trap is overlooking the phase-out rules for start-up costs exceeding a certain threshold. Test-takers often incorrectly assume a higher or lower deduction amount.

questions.question: Davis & Klein, a growing law firm, recently purchased new office furniture and equipment for $85,000. The firm's controller needs to determine the appropriate depreciation period for these assets. Office furniture and equipment is depreciated over:
options: 3 years
5 years
15 years
7 years
questions.explanation: Office furniture and fixtures are 7-year property under MACRS. Computers and certain manufacturing equipment are 5-year property. Land improvements (fencing, parking lots) are 15-year property.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 3-year property under MACRS generally applies to certain special tools and racehorses over 12 years old. Office furniture and equipment does not fall into this category.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 5-year property includes assets like computers, automobiles, and light-duty trucks. While computers are often found in an office, general office furniture and equipment are classified differently for depreciation purposes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 15-year property includes land improvements such as shrubbery, fences, and roads. Office furniture and equipment are tangible personal property, not land improvements.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Office furniture and equipment are classified as 7-year property under MACRS (Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System). This means the cost of these assets is generally recovered over a 7-year period using prescribed depreciation methods.

questions.educational: The Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) categorizes assets into different classes, each with a specific recovery period. This system is used to determine the annual depreciation expense for tax purposes. The classification of assets is crucial because it directly impacts the timing of deductions, affecting a business's taxable income and tax liability. Understanding MACRS is essential for accurately calculating depreciation and minimizing tax obligations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific asset being depreciated and its corresponding MACRS class life. A table of common asset classes and their recovery periods is a valuable resource to memorize or have readily accessible during the exam.
questions.memoryAid: OFFICE furniture = SEVEN years (think: office has SEVEN letters)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the correct MACRS recovery period for office furniture and equipment. A common trap is confusing the recovery periods for different types of assets. Test-takers often incorrectly assume a shorter or longer recovery period.

questions.question: The FUTA tax rate is 6.0% on the first $7,000 of wages per employee. After the FUTA credit for state unemployment taxes, the effective rate is typically:
questions.explanation: Employers receive a credit of up to 5.4% for state unemployment taxes paid timely, reducing the effective FUTA rate to 0.6%. Credit reduction applies in states that have outstanding Title XII loans.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This represents a partial credit against the FUTA tax, but not the typical effective rate after the maximum credit for state unemployment taxes is applied. The 5.4% credit is the key factor.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This is the amount of the FUTA credit that employers can receive for state unemployment taxes paid timely, not the resulting effective FUTA rate. It's the *credit*, not the final tax rate.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The FUTA tax rate is 6.0% on the first $7,000 of wages per employee. Employers can receive a credit of up to 5.4% for state unemployment taxes paid timely. Therefore, the effective FUTA rate is typically 0.6% (6.0% - 5.4%).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This percentage doesn't directly relate to either the initial FUTA tax rate, the maximum credit for state unemployment taxes, or the resulting effective FUTA rate after applying the credit. It's a distractor number.

questions.educational: The Federal Unemployment Tax Act (FUTA) provides unemployment benefits to workers who lose their jobs. Employers fund this system through FUTA taxes. To encourage states to establish their own unemployment programs, the federal government offers a credit against the FUTA tax for employers who pay state unemployment taxes. This credit incentivizes state-level participation and reduces the overall federal tax burden on employers in compliant states, aligning with the broader goal of shared federal-state responsibility for unemployment insurance.
questions.examTip: Remember the FUTA credit is generally 5.4%, making the effective FUTA rate 0.6%. Be aware that this credit can be reduced in states with outstanding Title XII advances from the federal government, a detail often tested on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: FUTA: 6.0% - 5.4% Credit = 0.6% Effective Rate. Think '6 minus 5.4 leaves point six'.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the gross FUTA rate with the net rate after the credit for state unemployment taxes. The exam exploits the misconception that the full 6% is always the effective rate. Test-takers often forget to account for the credit, which significantly reduces the employer's FUTA tax burden.

questions.question: The Work Opportunity Tax Credit provides a credit for hiring individuals from targeted groups equal to:
options: 40% of first-year wages up to $6,000 if worked 400+ hours
100% of first-year wages up to $2,400 if the employee is a qualified veteran.
25% of first-year wages up to $6,000, regardless of hours worked by the employee.
50% of first-year wages with no limit, but only for employees hired in designated empowerment zones.
questions.explanation: WOTC is 40% of up to $6,000 wages ($2,400 max) for most groups if the employee works 400+ hours. If 120-399 hours, credit is 25%. Higher limits apply for certain veterans and long-term TANF recipients.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC) generally provides a credit of 40% of the first $6,000 in wages paid to eligible employees from specific targeted groups, provided they work at least 400 hours. This results in a maximum credit of $2,400 per employee.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While there are increased WOTC benefits for hiring qualified veterans, the credit is not 100% of wages up to $2,400. The credit for qualified veterans can be higher, but it's not this specific amount or percentage.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 25% credit applies to employees working at least 120 hours but less than 400. The 40% credit requires 400+ hours.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is a wage limit for the WOTC, and the percentage is not 50%. While empowerment zones offer other tax incentives, this is not how the WOTC functions.
