questions.memoryAid: DIV-LIQ: Different dividend or liquidation rights = Likely a violation.
questions.commonMistake: The exam exploits the misconception that any difference in shareholder treatment violates the one-class-of-stock rule. Many candidates incorrectly assume that differences in compensation or ownership percentages automatically create a second class of stock, while the rule specifically addresses differences in dividend or liquidation rights.

questions.question: Johnson Enterprises wants to elect to be treated as an S corporation, starting January 1 of next year. The election to be treated as an S corporation is made on:
options: Form 2553
Form SS-4
Form 8832
Form 1120-S
questions.explanation: Form 2553, Election by a Small Business Corporation, is used to elect S corporation status. The form must be signed by all shareholders and filed by the 15th day of the 3rd month of the tax year for which the election is to be effective, or at any time during the preceding tax year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Form 2553, *Election by a Small Business Corporation*, is the specific IRS form used by eligible corporations to elect to be treated as an S corporation for federal tax purposes. This election allows the corporation's income and losses to be passed through to its shareholders.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form SS-4, *Application for Employer Identification Number (EIN)*, is used to apply for an EIN from the IRS. While an S corporation will need an EIN, this form is not used to elect S corporation status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 8832, *Entity Classification Election*, is used by an eligible entity to choose how it will be classified for federal tax purposes (e.g., as a corporation, partnership, or disregarded entity). While related to entity classification, it is not the specific form used to elect S corporation status; it's more broadly about choosing an entity's tax classification.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 1120-S, *U.S. Income Tax Return for an S Corporation*, is the form used to report the income, deductions, and credits of an S corporation *after* the S corporation election has been made. It is not the form used to make the initial election.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a qualifying corporation to avoid double taxation. Instead of the corporation paying income tax at the corporate level, the income and losses are passed through to the shareholders' individual income tax returns. This pass-through taxation is a key difference between S corporations and C corporations, and understanding the election process is crucial for the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the filing deadline for Form 2553: no later than the 15th day of the 3rd month of the tax year the election is to take effect, or at any time during the preceding tax year. Missing this deadline can delay the S corporation status.
questions.memoryAid: Think '2553 = S Corp Elect' to quickly recall the correct form for the S corporation election.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the various IRS forms used by businesses. Test-takers often incorrectly associate S corporation elections with forms used for EIN applications or entity classifications, rather than the specific form designated for S corporation election.

questions.question: GreenTech Solutions Inc. decided to elect S corporation status. They filed Form 2553 with the IRS on March 1, 2024. Assuming the form is accepted, the S election is effective:
options: The election is invalid and must be refiled
March 1, 2024
January 1, 2025
January 1, 2024
questions.explanation: For a calendar year corporation, Form 2553 must be filed by March 15 (2½ months into the year) for the election to be effective January 1 of that year. Filed on March 1, this election is timely for 2024 and effective January 1, 2024.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The election is valid because Form 2553 was filed before the 15th day of the third month of the tax year. Therefore, the election does not need to be refiled.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - An S election does not become effective on the date the form is filed. If the election is timely, it is effective as of the first day of the tax year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Since Form 2553 was filed by March 15th, the S election can be effective for the current tax year (2024), not the following year. The IRS allows for elections to be effective for the current year if the filing deadline is met.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Generally, a corporation can elect to be treated as an S corporation if all shareholders consent and Form 2553 is filed either (1) anytime during the preceding tax year or (2) on or before the 15th day of the 3rd month of the tax year the election is to take effect. Since the corporation filed Form 2553 on March 1, 2024, it meets the requirements for an S election to be effective January 1, 2024.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a qualifying corporation to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders, avoiding double taxation at the corporate level. Understanding the timing requirements for Form 2553 is crucial because a late or improperly filed election can delay S corporation status, impacting tax planning and compliance for both the corporation and its shareholders. This is a key concept tested in the Business Taxation section of the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the '2 months and 15 days' rule for S-corp elections. If you see a date question on Form 2553, immediately check if the filing date is before March 15th for a calendar-year corporation to determine the effective date.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'March 15th Deadline' for S-corp elections to start in the current year.
questions.commonMistake: A common trap is overlooking the timing requirements for S corporation elections. Many candidates incorrectly assume that filing Form 2553 at any point in the year automatically results in an S election for that year, failing to consider the specific deadlines for timely elections. They may also confuse the filing date with the effective date.

questions.question: A shareholder contributes property with a basis of $20,000 and FMV of $50,000 for 100% of S corporation stock. The shareholder's stock basis is:
questions.explanation: When property is contributed to an S corporation in exchange for stock in a §351 transaction, the shareholder's basis in the stock equals the adjusted basis of the property contributed ($20,000). No gain is recognized on the transfer if control requirements are met.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The fair market value ($50,000) of the property is not used to determine the shareholder's stock basis in a §351 transaction. The basis is generally the adjusted basis of the contributed property.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $35,000 is not the correct basis. There is no calculation that would lead to this number based on the information provided. The shareholder's stock basis is determined by the adjusted basis of the contributed property.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The shareholder's stock basis is not determined by the fair market value of the contributed property. The basis is generally the same as the adjusted basis of the property contributed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - In a §351 transaction where property is exchanged for stock, the shareholder's basis in the stock received is generally the same as the adjusted basis of the property contributed. Therefore, the shareholder's stock basis is $20,000, the adjusted basis of the property contributed.

questions.educational: Section 351 of the Internal Revenue Code allows for the tax-free transfer of property to a corporation in exchange for stock if, immediately after the exchange, the transferors are in control of the corporation. The shareholder's basis in the stock received is generally a substituted basis from the property transferred, preventing immediate recognition of gain and aligning with the principle of deferral in tax law, a key concept tested in the Business Taxation section of the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the basis rules for contributions to S corporations under §351 are similar to those for C corporations, but the subsequent treatment of losses and distributions differs significantly, so pay close attention to the entity type when calculating shareholder basis adjustments.
questions.memoryAid: BASIS: Basis of stock in an S-corp is generally the Adjusted basis of property contributed.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the basis rules for contributions to corporations and partnerships. The fair market value ($50,000) is tempting because it represents the value of the stock received, but the basis is determined by the basis of the contributed property. Some may incorrectly assume a zero basis if they don't recall the general rule.

questions.question: Maria is a shareholder in an S corporation, Willow Creek Farms. Which of the following items does NOT increase Maria's stock basis in Willow Creek Farms?
options: Separately stated tax-exempt income
Distributive share of losses
Additional capital contributions
Distributive share of income
questions.explanation: S corp stock basis increases for: income items (ordinary and separately stated), tax-exempt income, and capital contributions. Basis DECREASES for: distributions, losses and deductions, and nondeductible expenses. Losses reduce basis; they do not increase it.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Separately stated tax-exempt income increases a shareholder's basis in S corporation stock. This is because the income is not taxed at the corporate level but still increases the shareholder's economic benefit, thus increasing their basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A shareholder's distributive share of losses from an S corporation *decreases* their stock basis, not increases it. Losses represent a reduction in the shareholder's investment in the corporation. According to IRC Section 1367, losses reduce basis before distributions.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Additional capital contributions directly increase a shareholder's basis in S corporation stock. This is because the shareholder is investing more capital into the business, increasing their ownership stake and basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A shareholder's distributive share of income from an S corporation increases their stock basis. This reflects the shareholder's increased economic benefit from the corporation's profitability, even if the income is not immediately distributed.

questions.educational: S corporation shareholders use stock basis to determine the deductibility of losses and the taxability of distributions. Basis is increased by contributions and income items, and decreased by distributions and loss items. Understanding basis calculations is crucial for accurately reporting S corporation income and avoiding double taxation or disallowed losses, which are frequently tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the ordering rules for basis adjustments: Increases for income and contributions come first, followed by decreases for distributions, then losses. This order can affect the deductibility of losses in a given year, so pay close attention to dates in exam questions.
questions.memoryAid: C.I.D.L. - Contributions and Income increase basis; Distributions and Losses decrease basis.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that all items affecting taxable income also affect stock basis. A common mistake is to think that losses increase basis, when they actually decrease it. Tax-exempt income and capital contributions both increase basis, while losses decrease it.

questions.question: John is a shareholder in an S corporation. To determine the amount of deductible losses, what is the correct order for adjusting John's S corporation stock basis?
options: Income, then distributions, then losses
Losses, then income, then distributions
Income, then losses, then distributions
Distributions, then income, then losses
questions.explanation: Basis adjustments occur in this order: (1) increase for income items, (2) decrease for distributions (but not below zero), (3) decrease for nondeductible/non-capital expenses, (4) decrease for losses and deductions. This order maximizes tax-free distribution treatment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The correct order for adjusting S corporation stock basis is income items first, then distributions (but not below zero), and finally losses and deductions. This order is crucial because it allows shareholders to potentially receive distributions tax-free to the extent of their basis before losses reduce their basis, potentially disallowing loss deductions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Losses are applied *after* income and distributions. Applying losses first would prematurely reduce basis, potentially causing distributions to be taxable and limiting the deductibility of losses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While income does come first, losses are applied *before* distributions. Distributions are applied after income has increased the basis, and after losses have potentially decreased it.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Distributions are not the first adjustment to S corporation stock basis. Income items increase basis before distributions are considered, allowing for potentially tax-free distributions.

questions.educational: The ordering of S corporation basis adjustments is designed to maximize the shareholder's ability to receive tax-free distributions and deduct losses. By increasing basis for income first, shareholders can withdraw profits without immediate tax consequences, up to the amount of their basis. This aligns with the pass-through nature of S corporations, where income is taxed at the shareholder level, and distributions represent a return of that already-taxed income.
questions.examTip: Remember the ordering rules for S corporation basis adjustments are frequently tested on the SEE exam. Pay close attention to scenarios involving income, losses, and distributions, and how the order impacts taxable income and loss deductibility.
questions.memoryAid: I-D-L: Income, Distributions, Losses (for S-Corp basis adjustments).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the order in which basis adjustments are made, leading to incorrect calculations of deductible losses. Losses are only deductible to the extent of basis, so the basis must be adjusted for income and distributions *before* considering losses. Failing to follow the correct order can result in disallowed losses.

questions.question: Sarah is a shareholder in Oakwood Industries, an S corporation that has always been an S corporation. Sarah receives a $10,000 distribution. How is this distribution treated for tax purposes?
options: Always tax-free, as distributions from S corps are a return of capital.
Taxable as ordinary income, as S corporation distributions are considered wages.
Tax-free to the extent of stock basis, then capital gain
Always taxable as a dividend, regardless of earnings and profits or basis.
questions.explanation: For S corps without accumulated E&P (never a C corp), distributions are: (1) tax-free return of capital to the extent of stock basis, then (2) capital gain. There is no dividend treatment because no E&P exists.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Distributions are tax-free only to the extent of basis. Once basis is exhausted, the distribution is treated as capital gain.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - S corporation distributions are generally not considered wages unless the shareholder is also an employee receiving compensation for services.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - For an S corporation with no accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) from a prior C corporation history, distributions are treated as a tax-free return of capital to the extent of the shareholder's stock basis. Once the stock basis is reduced to zero, any further distributions are treated as a capital gain, as per Subchapter S rules.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corps do not have earnings and profits unless they were previously a C corp or acquired a C corp. Distributions are first a return of capital, then capital gain.

questions.educational: S corporations, unlike C corporations, are pass-through entities. This means the corporation's income and losses are passed through to the shareholders and reported on their individual income tax returns. Distributions from an S corporation are generally tax-free to the extent of the shareholder's basis because the shareholder has already paid tax on the income that generated those distributions. Understanding the difference between S corp and C corp distributions is crucial for the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to whether the S corporation has accumulated E&P from a prior C corporation history, as this significantly impacts the tax treatment of distributions.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corps: 'Basis Before Booty' – Distributions are tax-free up to basis, then capital gain 'booty'.
questions.commonMistake: The exam exploits the misconception that all distributions from S corporations are taxable. Many candidates incorrectly assume distributions are always taxable as ordinary income or dividends, failing to consider the tax-free treatment to the extent of basis. Others may forget the capital gain treatment for distributions exceeding basis.

questions.question: An S corporation with accumulated E&P from C corp years distributes cash. The distribution is first treated as:
options: A return of capital, reducing the shareholder's stock basis, until basis is zero.
A distribution taxed as a capital gain to the extent it exceeds the shareholder's basis.
Tax-free from AAA (Accumulated Adjustments Account)
A taxable dividend from accumulated E&P, regardless of the AAA balance.
questions.explanation: For S corps with C corp E&P, distributions come from: (1) AAA (tax-free until exhausted), (2) accumulated E&P (dividend), (3) remaining stock basis (tax-free), then (4) capital gain. AAA is depleted first to preserve the pass-through character.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Distributions from an S-Corp with C-Corp E&P are first sourced from the AAA, not return of capital. Return of capital only occurs after AAA and E&P are exhausted.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Distributions are not capital gains unless they exceed both the AAA and accumulated E&P, and also the shareholder's stock basis. This option skips the proper ordering rules.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An S corporation with accumulated E&P from C corporation years must first distribute from its Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA). This distribution is tax-free to the extent of the shareholder's stock basis, preserving the pass-through nature of S corporation income.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The distribution is first sourced from the AAA and is tax-free to the extent of the AAA. Only after the AAA is exhausted is the distribution considered a dividend from E&P.

questions.educational: The distribution rules for S corporations with accumulated E&P are designed to ensure that previously taxed S corporation income is distributed tax-free before any distributions are treated as dividends from prior C corporation earnings. This ordering is crucial for maintaining the integrity of the S corporation's pass-through taxation and preventing double taxation. Understanding these rules is essential for accurately calculating shareholder basis and determining the taxability of distributions.
questions.examTip: Remember the distribution ordering: AAA first, then accumulated E&P, then return of capital (basis), then capital gain. This sequence is frequently tested on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: AAA before E&P, then Basis, then Gain (AEBG)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the distribution rules for S corporations with and without accumulated E&P. Distributions are not treated as return of capital until after AAA is exhausted. Many incorrectly assume distributions are always dividends or return of capital, overlooking the priority of the AAA.

questions.question: Sunrise Medical Inc. is an S corporation. The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) is adjusted for all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Nondeductible expenses related to tax-exempt income
Distributions to shareholders
Tax-exempt income
Ordinary business income
questions.explanation: AAA is NOT increased by tax-exempt income (that goes to OAA - Other Adjustments Account). AAA IS increased by: taxable income items. AAA IS decreased by: distributions, losses/deductions, and nondeductible expenses (other than those related to tax-exempt income).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Nondeductible expenses related to tax-exempt income *do* decrease the AAA. These expenses, while not deductible for tax purposes, still represent an economic outflow from the S corporation and reduce the amount available for distribution to shareholders.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Distributions to shareholders *do* decrease the AAA. Distributions are tracked through the AAA to determine the taxability of distributions to shareholders. Distributions reduce the AAA before reducing other equity accounts.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Tax-exempt income increases the Other Adjustments Account (OAA), *not* the AAA. The AAA tracks the cumulative taxable income of the S corporation that has not yet been distributed to shareholders. Tax-exempt income, by definition, is not taxable and therefore does not affect the AAA.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Ordinary business income *does* increase the AAA. The AAA is increased by separately stated income items and the ordinary business income of the S corporation. This reflects the accumulated taxable earnings that have not yet been distributed.

questions.educational: The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) is a critical calculation for S corporations. It tracks the cumulative taxable income and deductible losses of the S corporation that have not been distributed to shareholders. This account determines the taxability of distributions, ensuring that previously taxed income is distributed tax-free before distributions are considered dividends from accumulated earnings and profits (AE&P). Understanding the AAA is essential for proper S corporation tax compliance and shareholder tax planning.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of S corporation distribution questions. Determine if the S corporation has AE&P, as this impacts the taxability of distributions *after* the AAA is exhausted.
questions.memoryAid: AAA: 'Adjusted After All (taxable items)'. OAA: 'Only Other Adjustments (tax-exempt)'.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that all items affecting shareholder basis also affect the AAA. Tax-exempt income increases shareholder basis but does *not* increase the AAA. The AAA is a corporate-level account tracking earnings that have already been taxed to the shareholders, and tax-exempt income, by definition, has not been.

questions.question: David is a shareholder in an S corporation, TechStart LLC. What is the limit on the amount of S corporation loss David can deduct on his individual income tax return?
options: The shareholder's at-risk amount, without considering any debt basis or passive loss limitations.
Stock basis plus debt basis, subject to at-risk and passive loss rules
Stock basis plus debt basis, without regard to the at-risk rules but considering passive loss limitations.
Stock basis only, without considering any direct loans made by the shareholder to the S corporation.
questions.explanation: S corp loss deductions face three hurdles: (1) stock and debt basis, (2) at-risk limitations, (3) passive activity rules. Losses exceeding any limitation are suspended and carried forward. All three must be satisfied to deduct losses currently.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The at-risk amount is a limitation, but the initial limit is stock basis plus debt basis. This option ignores the initial basis limit and debt basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An S corporation shareholder's deductible loss is limited to their stock basis plus any direct loans they've made to the S corporation (debt basis). However, even if sufficient basis exists, the loss is further limited by the at-risk rules and the passive activity loss rules. Losses exceeding these limitations are suspended and carried forward.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The at-risk rules must be considered in addition to the passive loss limitations. This option incorrectly omits the at-risk limitation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Direct loans from the shareholder to the S corporation increase the shareholder's basis for loss deduction purposes. This option incorrectly limits the basis to stock only.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning their income and losses are passed through to the shareholders. However, shareholders can only deduct losses to the extent of their investment in the S corporation. This limitation ensures that shareholders don't deduct losses exceeding their economic risk in the entity, aligning with the principles of tax fairness and preventing abusive tax shelters. The at-risk rules further limit losses to the amount the shareholder is personally liable for, or has pledged as security for the debt.
questions.examTip: Remember to apply the loss limitations in the correct order: first basis (stock and debt), then at-risk, and finally passive activity loss rules. A common trick on the EA exam is to provide information to calculate all three limitations, but only one is the *most* limiting factor.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'BAD' for Basis, At-risk, Debt (basis). This helps remember the order in which you consider these limitations on S-corp losses.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates focus solely on the basis limitation and forget the at-risk rules. While stock and debt basis are the starting point, the deductible loss is further limited by the at-risk rules and passive activity loss rules. The exam exploits the misconception that basis is the *only* limitation.

questions.question: Lisa is a shareholder in an S corporation, Riverbend Consulting. Under what circumstances does Lisa have debt basis in Riverbend Consulting?
options: Another shareholder loans money to the S corporation
The shareholder personally guarantees an S corporation loan
The shareholder loans money directly to the S corporation
The S corporation borrows money from a bank
questions.explanation: S corp shareholders only get debt basis for DIRECT loans from the shareholder to the corporation. Personal guarantees of corporate debt do NOT create debt basis. This differs from partnerships, where guaranteed debt can create basis. Only actual economic outlay by the shareholder creates debt basis.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Another shareholder's loan to the S corporation does not create debt basis for the shareholder in question. Debt basis is personal to the shareholder who makes the loan.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A shareholder's personal guarantee of an S corporation loan does not create debt basis unless the shareholder actually makes payments on the guarantee. The shareholder must have an economic outlay to increase basis. Mere guarantees are insufficient per case law such as Selfe v. United States.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A shareholder who directly loans money to their S corporation establishes debt basis. This direct loan represents an economic outlay by the shareholder, increasing their ability to deduct losses that may flow through from the S corporation. This is a fundamental principle in S corporation taxation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - When an S corporation borrows money from a bank, it creates debt basis for the corporation, not the shareholders. The shareholders do not have an economic outlay in this scenario, so their individual basis is unaffected.

questions.educational: S corporation shareholders can deduct losses up to their basis, which consists of stock basis and debt basis. Stock basis is the shareholder's investment in the S corporation's stock. Debt basis arises when a shareholder directly loans money to the S corporation. Understanding basis is crucial for determining the deductibility of losses and the taxability of distributions from S corporations, a key area on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that unlike partnerships, guarantees of S corporation debt do NOT create basis for loss deduction purposes on the EA exam; there must be an actual economic outlay by the shareholder.
questions.memoryAid: Direct Loan = Debt Basis (DL=DB)
questions.authorityRef: Estate of Leavitt v. Commissioner, 90 T.C. 206 (1988); Rev. Rul. 75-144
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates believe that a shareholder's guarantee of an S corporation loan creates debt basis. However, direct lending is required. A guarantee is generally not enough to create debt basis unless the shareholder actually makes payments on the guarantee, at which point they are considered to have made a loan to the corporation.

questions.question: Mark is a shareholder in an S corporation. During the year, Mark's share of the S corporation's losses exceeds his stock basis. How are these excess losses treated?
options: Convert to capital losses after a 5-year carryforward
First reduce debt basis, then are suspended
Are deductible against other income, but only up to $3,000
Are permanently disallowed unless the S-corp elects liquidation
questions.explanation: Losses in excess of stock basis reduce any debt basis the shareholder has from direct loans to the corporation. If losses still exceed combined stock and debt basis, the excess is suspended and carried forward indefinitely until sufficient basis is restored.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Excess losses are not converted to capital losses. They are suspended and carried forward indefinitely until the shareholder has sufficient basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - S corporation losses first reduce a shareholder's basis in their stock. Once stock basis is exhausted, losses then reduce the shareholder's basis in any direct loans they've made to the S corporation. Any remaining losses exceeding both stock and debt basis are suspended under IRC Section 1366(d) and carried forward indefinitely until the shareholder restores basis.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Excess losses are not deductible against other income in the current year. They are suspended until the shareholder has sufficient basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Excess losses are not permanently disallowed simply because the S-corp doesn't liquidate. They are suspended and carried forward indefinitely until the shareholder has sufficient basis.

questions.educational: The basis limitation rules for S corporation losses exist to prevent shareholders from deducting losses exceeding their economic investment in the company. This ensures that shareholders don't receive tax benefits beyond their actual at-risk amount. Understanding basis is crucial for the SEE exam because it directly impacts the deductibility of losses and the taxability of distributions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of S corporation loss questions. Determine if the shareholder has debt basis in addition to stock basis, as this significantly impacts the amount of deductible losses.
questions.memoryAid: SILO: Stock basis, Income, Loan basis, Offset losses (order of basis adjustments).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget the order in which losses exceeding basis are treated. Losses exceeding stock basis first reduce debt basis (but not below zero). Any losses exceeding both stock and debt basis are suspended and carried forward indefinitely to future years when basis is restored. The exam exploits the misconception that losses exceeding basis are simply disallowed.

questions.question: Jane is a shareholder-employee of Willow Creek Consulting, an S corporation. She performs substantial services for the company. Which of the following statements accurately describes her compensation requirements?
options: Receive all income as salary, subject to standard payroll tax withholdings and reporting requirements.
Receive reasonable compensation as salary before distributions
Choose between salary or distributions each year, based on anticipated tax liabilities and personal financial planning needs.
Receive all income as distributions, provided that the S corporation maintains sufficient retained earnings to cover these payments.
questions.explanation: S corporation shareholder-employees who perform services must receive reasonable compensation as salary before taking distributions. The IRS closely scrutinizes S corps that pay minimal or no salary to avoid employment taxes. Noncompliance can result in reclassification and penalties.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S-corp shareholder-employees performing substantial services must receive reasonable compensation as salary. They cannot avoid payroll taxes by taking all income as salary.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - S corporation shareholder-employees who perform services must receive reasonable compensation as salary before taking distributions. This is to ensure that the S corporation and the shareholder-employee are paying their fair share of employment taxes (Social Security and Medicare). The IRS scrutinizes situations where shareholder-employees take minimal or no salary and instead take large distributions to avoid these taxes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Shareholder-employees cannot arbitrarily choose between salary and distributions to minimize taxes. Reasonable compensation is required before distributions.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Shareholder-employees performing substantial services cannot receive all income as distributions. This is an attempt to avoid payroll taxes on the portion that should be salary.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning income and losses are passed through to the shareholders' individual income tax returns. However, shareholder-employees are subject to employment taxes (Social Security and Medicare) on their wages. The IRS requires 'reasonable compensation' to prevent shareholders from avoiding these taxes by taking distributions instead of salary, which would only be subject to income tax, not employment taxes. This concept is crucial for understanding the tax implications of S corporations and is frequently tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, be alert for questions involving S corporation shareholder-employees and their compensation. If the question mentions minimal or no salary with large distributions, it's likely testing your understanding of the 'reasonable compensation' rule and potential IRS reclassification.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corp: Salary first, then Share (distributions).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that S corporation shareholders can avoid employment taxes by taking distributions instead of salary. However, if a shareholder-employee performs substantial services, they must receive reasonable compensation as salary, subject to employment taxes, before taking distributions. The IRS scrutinizes this area to prevent tax avoidance.

questions.question: Mark is a shareholder-employee of GreenTech Solutions, an S corporation. He received a $50,000 distribution this year. Regarding the employment tax treatment of this distribution, it is:
options: Subject to self-employment tax
Not subject to employment taxes
Subject to FUTA tax only
Subject to Social Security and Medicare tax
questions.explanation: S corporation distributions to shareholders are NOT subject to employment taxes (Social Security, Medicare, or self-employment tax). This differs from partnerships/sole proprietorships and is a benefit of S corp status. However, salary/wages ARE subject to FICA.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Distributions from an S corporation to shareholder-employees are considered a return on investment, not earned income. Therefore, they are not subject to self-employment tax, which applies to income earned through a trade or business.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Distributions from an S corporation to shareholder-employees are treated as a return on their investment in the company. These distributions are not considered wages or self-employment income and are therefore not subject to employment taxes (Social Security, Medicare, or self-employment tax). However, reasonable compensation paid as salary is subject to employment taxes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - FUTA (Federal Unemployment Tax Act) tax applies to wages paid to employees. While S corporations are employers and pay FUTA tax on employee wages, distributions to shareholders are not considered wages and are therefore not subject to FUTA tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Social Security and Medicare taxes (FICA taxes) apply to wages paid to employees. Distributions to shareholders are not considered wages but rather a return on investment, and are therefore not subject to Social Security and Medicare tax. Only the shareholder-employee's salary is subject to these taxes.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning the corporation's income and losses are passed through to the shareholders' individual income tax returns. To prevent shareholders from avoiding employment taxes, the IRS requires S corporations to pay reasonable compensation to shareholder-employees for services rendered. This compensation is subject to employment taxes, while distributions exceeding reasonable compensation are not.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS scrutinizes S corporation shareholder-employee compensation to ensure reasonable salaries are paid before distributions are taken, as this directly impacts employment tax revenue. Be prepared to advise clients on establishing and documenting reasonable compensation.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp Distributions: No FICA! (But salary still is.)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse distributions with salary. Distributions to shareholders are generally *not* subject to employment taxes (Social Security, Medicare, FUTA, SUTA). However, this assumes the shareholder-employee has already received reasonable compensation as salary for services performed.

questions.question: Precision Manufacturing, an S corporation, is preparing its Schedule K-1 (Form 1120-S) for its shareholders. Which of the following items should be separately stated?
options: Net rental real estate income
Officer compensation, including benefits
Depreciation expense on manufacturing equipment
Cost of goods sold, including direct labor
questions.explanation: Separately stated items include those that could affect individual shareholder tax liability differently: capital gains/losses, §1231 gains/losses, charitable contributions, rental income, interest income, dividends, §179 deduction, and foreign taxes. Net rental real estate income is separately stated.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Net rental real estate income is a separately stated item because it's subject to passive activity loss rules at the shareholder level under IRC §469. This means a shareholder's ability to deduct rental losses may be limited based on their individual involvement and other passive income, necessitating separate reporting on Schedule K-1.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Officer compensation is a deductible expense that is aggregated on Form 1120-S, not separately stated on Schedule K-1.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Depreciation expense is used to calculate ordinary business income and is not separately stated on Schedule K-1.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Cost of goods sold is used to calculate gross profit and is not separately stated on Schedule K-1; it flows into ordinary business income.

questions.educational: The requirement to separately state certain items on Schedule K-1 stems from the pass-through nature of S corporations. Because the shareholders, not the corporation, are ultimately liable for the taxes on the business's income, items that could have a unique impact on each shareholder's individual tax situation must be reported separately. This ensures accurate tax reporting and allows shareholders to properly apply relevant tax rules and limitations at their individual level.
questions.examTip: When preparing Schedule K-1s, remember to consider items that are subject to individual shareholder limitations or have special tax treatments at the individual level. These are prime candidates for separate reporting.
questions.memoryAid: SILO: Separately stated Items Limit Owners. This reminds you that separately stated items are often subject to limitations at the owner level.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates do not fully understand which items must be separately stated on Schedule K-1. Items that could affect a shareholder's tax liability differently depending on their individual circumstances must be separately stated. Officer compensation is an ordinary business expense that flows through as part of the overall business income or loss.

questions.question: Sunrise Ventures, an S corporation, recognized a $10,000 long-term capital gain this year. How is this capital gain treated when passed through to the shareholders?
options: Are retained by the S corporation and used to offset future operating losses, without direct shareholder impact.
Retain their character as capital gains to shareholders
Convert to ordinary income when passed to shareholders
Are taxed at the corporate level at a reduced rate of 21%, with no further tax implications for the shareholders.
questions.explanation: S corporation income items retain their character when passed through to shareholders. Capital gains remain capital gains, interest remains interest, etc. This allows shareholders to benefit from preferential capital gains rates and to properly calculate their own investment income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are pass-through entities. All income, including capital gains, must be passed through to the shareholders, even if the corporation has operating losses.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - S corporations operate under the conduit principle, meaning that the character of income items is preserved as they flow through to the shareholders. Capital gains recognized by the S corporation retain their character as capital gains in the hands of the shareholders, allowing them to be taxed at the applicable capital gains rates.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The character of capital gains is maintained when passed through to shareholders. Capital gains do not convert to ordinary income. This preservation of character is a key feature of pass-through taxation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations are generally not subject to federal income tax at the corporate level. The income passes through to the shareholders who report it on their individual returns.

questions.educational: S corporations are designed to avoid double taxation, a common issue with C corporations. The pass-through taxation model ensures that income is taxed only once, at the shareholder level. Understanding the conduit principle and the preservation of income character is crucial for accurately reporting S corporation income on individual tax returns and advising clients on the tax implications of operating as an S corporation.
questions.examTip: When calculating a shareholder's basis in an S corporation, remember to include their share of capital gains, as this increases their basis and can impact the deductibility of losses.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp = See-Through (Pass-Through)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget that S corporations are pass-through entities. Capital gains and losses retain their character when passed through to the shareholders. This allows shareholders to net these gains and losses with their other capital gains and losses on their individual income tax returns.

questions.question: Evergreen Corp. has been an S corporation for several years. For the past three years, its passive investment income has exceeded 30% of gross receipts, and it has C corporation earnings and profits. What is the impact?
options: If any shareholder dies and their stock passes to an ineligible shareholder, such as a C corporation or nonresident alien, the S election is terminated.
The corporation operates in more than one state and fails to properly apportion income, potentially jeopardizing its S corporation status in those states.
The corporation has passive investment income exceeding 25% of gross receipts for 3 consecutive years AND has C corp E&P
Net income exceeds $10 million, which subjects the corporation to a higher state tax rate, potentially impacting shareholder distributions and overall profitability.
questions.explanation: The S election automatically terminates if: (1) the corporation has passive investment income exceeding 25% of gross receipts for 3 consecutive years AND (2) has accumulated E&P from C corp years. The election also terminates for eligibility violations.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the death of a shareholder *could* lead to termination if the stock is transferred to an ineligible shareholder, the death itself is not the terminating event. The question implies a guaranteed termination.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Operating in multiple states doesn't automatically jeopardize S corporation status. Proper apportionment is important for tax compliance, but doesn't directly terminate the S election.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An S corporation election terminates automatically if the corporation has (1) accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) from a C corporation year AND (2) passive investment income exceeding 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive tax years. This rule, found in IRC Section 1362(d)(3), is designed to prevent corporations from using the S election to shelter excessive passive income while retaining the benefits of prior C corporation operations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The amount of net income does not directly impact the S corporation election. While high income may have other tax consequences, it doesn't cause termination.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a business to be taxed like a partnership while retaining the legal structure of a corporation. However, certain restrictions exist to prevent abuse of this structure, especially regarding passive income and prior C corporation earnings. The termination rules are crucial because they can lead to unexpected C corporation taxation, which can significantly impact the shareholders' tax liabilities. Understanding these rules is essential for advising clients on maintaining their S corporation status.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the 'and' versus 'or' conjunctions in S corporation termination rules. The passive income test requires BOTH accumulated C corp E&P AND excessive passive income for three years to trigger termination. A common trick on the EA exam is to present scenarios where only one condition is met.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'P.A.C.' for Passive, Accumulated, and Consecutive to remember the three key elements of the passive income termination rule: Passive income over 25%, Accumulated C-corp E&P, for 3 Consecutive years.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the passive income termination rules for S corporations with C corporation earnings and profits. The S election can be terminated if the corporation has C corporation E&P *and* passive investment income exceeding 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive years (not 30%). The exam exploits the misconception of the exact percentage and the requirement for prior C corp E&P.

questions.question: The shareholders of Redwood Industries, an S corporation, are considering revoking their S election. What percentage of shareholders must consent to the revocation?
options: Unanimous consent (100%)
Three-fourths (75%) of shareholders, including all those holding preferred stock.
Two-thirds (66.7%) of shareholders, provided they represent at least 75% of the issued shares.
A majority (more than 50%)
questions.explanation: To voluntarily revoke an S election, shareholders holding more than 50% of the corporation's shares (both voting and nonvoting) must consent. This is less stringent than the original election, which requires consent of all shareholders.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Unanimous consent (100%) is required for the initial S corporation election, but not for revocation. The revocation process is designed to be less burdensome than the initial election.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The revocation of an S election only requires a majority consent, regardless of preferred stock holdings.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While share ownership can be relevant in other corporate decisions, S election revocation is based solely on the number of shareholders consenting, not the percentage of shares they own.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - To voluntarily revoke an S corporation election, shareholders holding more than 50% of the corporation's issued and outstanding shares (both voting and nonvoting) must consent. This allows a majority of shareholders to decide the entity's tax status, reflecting their economic interest in the corporation.

questions.educational: The ability to revoke an S corporation election provides flexibility for businesses as their circumstances change. An S corporation might choose to revoke its election if it anticipates losses that would be better utilized at the corporate level or if it plans to attract investors who prefer C corporation status. Understanding the revocation rules is crucial for advising clients on the optimal tax structure for their business.
questions.examTip: Remember that the revocation of an S election is effective on a specific date chosen by the shareholders, as long as it's on or after the date the revocation is filed. Pay attention to the effective date when calculating income and deductions for the short S year and the short C year.
questions.memoryAid: Revocation Requires a 'Majority' - Think 'RM' for Revocation Majority.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates overestimate the level of shareholder consent required to revoke an S election. Only a *majority* (more than 50%) of the shareholders must consent to the revocation. The exam exploits the misconception that a higher level of agreement is needed.

questions.question: Apex Corporation elected S status on January 1, Year 1, after being a C corporation for many years. In Year 3, it sells a building it owned on December 31, Year 0, recognizing a $500,000 gain. Apex is subject to built-in gains tax if it:
options: Derives more than 25% of its gross receipts from passive investment income for three consecutive years.
Pays distributions to shareholders that exceed the corporation's accumulated adjustments account (AAA) balance.
Recognizes gain on assets held when it converted to S status within 5 years
Has accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) from its prior C corporation years at the end of the tax year.
questions.explanation: The Built-In Gains (BIG) tax is imposed at the highest corporate rate (21%) on net recognized built-in gain during the 5-year recognition period after converting from C to S status. The tax applies to appreciation on assets held at conversion that is recognized within 5 years.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While excessive passive income can terminate an S election, it does not trigger the built-in gains tax. The built-in gains tax relates to appreciation of assets before the S election.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Distributions exceeding the AAA can result in taxable dividends to shareholders, but do not trigger the built-in gains tax. The built-in gains tax is related to pre-S election asset appreciation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The built-in gains (BIG) tax, as defined in IRC Section 1374, targets the appreciation of assets that occurred while the corporation was a C corporation. If this appreciation is recognized (realized) through a sale or disposition within the 5-year recognition period (formerly 10 years for elections before 2009), the gain is subject to tax at the highest corporate rate (21%).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The existence of accumulated E&P can affect the taxability of distributions, but it does not, by itself, trigger the built-in gains tax. The built-in gains tax is related to pre-S election asset appreciation.

questions.educational: The built-in gains tax exists to prevent C corporations from avoiding corporate-level tax on appreciated assets by electing S corporation status and then selling those assets shortly thereafter. Without this rule, a C corporation could avoid double taxation. Understanding the interaction between C corporation rules (like E&P) and S corporation rules (like the AAA) is crucial for the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the built-in gains tax applies only to gains recognized within the 5-year recognition period following the S election and is calculated using the highest corporate tax rate. Pay close attention to the dates of conversion and asset sales in exam questions.
questions.memoryAid: BIG tax = Built-In Gains tax. Think: BIG appreciation before S election = BIG tax potential.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse built-in gains tax with passive income restrictions for S corporations. While both can affect S corporations, built-in gains tax specifically targets gains on assets held at the time of conversion. Test-takers often incorrectly associate passive income with all S corporation restrictions.

questions.question: Shareholder Thompson owns 40% of an S corporation and is also an employee. Thompson receives a W-2 salary of $80,000 and health insurance premiums of $12,000 paid by the corporation. How should Thompson report the health insurance for tax purposes?
options: The $12,000 is included in Thompson's W-2 wages and may be deducted as a self-employed health insurance deduction on Form 1040

The $12,000 is a tax-free fringe benefit excluded from Thompson's gross income, similar to benefits available to C-corporation employees, and is not reported anywhere on Thompson's individual income tax return.

The $12,000 is not deductible at all because S corporation shareholders owning more than 2% of the company's stock are treated as partners for fringe benefit purposes and are generally ineligible for health insurance deductions.

The $12,000 is deducted on Schedule A as a medical expense, subject to the 7.5% adjusted gross income (AGI) threshold, along with any other unreimbursed medical expenses Thompson incurred during the tax year.
questions.explanation: S corporation shareholders owning more than 2% are treated as self-employed for fringe benefit purposes. Health insurance premiums paid by the S corporation must be included in the shareholder-employee's W-2 wages (Box 1, but not Boxes 3 or 5). The shareholder can then claim the self-employed health insurance deduction as an above-the-line deduction on Form 1040, resulting in a net deduction benefit.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - S corporation shareholders owning more than 2% of the stock are treated as partners for fringe benefit purposes, per IRC Section 1372. Therefore, health insurance premiums paid by the S corporation for a more-than-2% shareholder-employee are included in their W-2 wages (Box 1) but are not subject to Social Security or Medicare taxes. The shareholder can then deduct the premiums as a self-employed health insurance deduction on Form 1040, Schedule 1.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Health insurance premiums paid by an S corporation for a more than 2% shareholder-employee are not treated as a tax-free fringe benefit. They are included in income and then potentially deductible.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While it's true that more-than-2% shareholders are treated as partners, they *can* deduct health insurance premiums to the extent of their compensation, if certain conditions are met.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Self-employed health insurance premiums are deducted above-the-line on Form 1040, not as an itemized deduction on Schedule A. They are not subject to the AGI threshold.

questions.educational: The treatment of fringe benefits for S corporation shareholders is a common area of confusion. Congress treats more-than-2% shareholders as partners for fringe benefit purposes to prevent them from receiving preferential treatment compared to sole proprietors or partners. This ensures a level playing field in taxation and prevents potential abuse of the S corporation structure for personal expenses.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 2% shareholder rule is a key threshold for S corporation fringe benefits; if a shareholder owns more than 2%, they are generally treated as self-employed for benefits like health insurance. Be prepared to calculate the deductible amount, considering any limitations based on net earnings from self-employment.
questions.memoryAid: 2% Rule: S-corps treat >2% shareholders like partners for fringe benefits (Health Insurance).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly believe that health insurance premiums paid by an S corporation for a more than 2% shareholder-employee are treated the same as for regular employees. A common trap is overlooking the specific rules for shareholder-employees, leading to the incorrect assumption that it's either a tax-free fringe benefit or not deductible at all.

questions.question: Harmony Designs, an S corporation, is choosing its tax year. Which of the following tax years must it generally use?
options: A calendar year
A 52-53 week year
The same year as its majority shareholder
Any fiscal year it chooses
questions.explanation: S corporations are required to use a "permitted year" - generally a calendar year or the natural business year if it qualifies. Like partnerships, this prevents deferral of shareholder income. A fiscal year may be elected under §444, but a deposit is required.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - S corporations are generally required to use a calendar year as their tax year under IRC Section 1378(a)(1). This requirement is in place to prevent shareholders from deferring income, as the shareholder's tax year is typically a calendar year.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While a 52-53 week year is a permissible tax year under IRC Section 441(f), it must end with reference to the required year end. For an S corporation, the required year end is generally a calendar year, so this is not the primary or most common choice.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While an S corporation can elect a tax year that matches its majority shareholder's year under IRC Section 444, this is not the general rule. A Section 444 election requires making a required payment to the IRS to offset the tax deferral benefit.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations do not have complete freedom to choose any fiscal year they desire. They are restricted to using a permitted year, which is generally a calendar year, unless they can establish a business purpose for a different year-end or make a Section 444 election.

questions.educational: The requirement for S corporations to use a calendar year (or demonstrate a business purpose for a different year-end) is rooted in the desire to prevent income deferral. Without this rule, shareholders could manipulate the timing of income recognition by choosing a fiscal year for the S corporation that differs from their own, potentially delaying tax payments. This aligns with the broader tax principle of matching income and expenses within the same tax year.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that S corporations are generally calendar year taxpayers, and any deviation from this rule requires a valid business purpose or a Section 444 election with its associated required payment.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp = Same as Santa (Calendar Year)
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume S corporations have flexibility in choosing their tax year. A common mistake is thinking they can align with the majority shareholder's year or choose any fiscal year, overlooking the general requirement for a calendar year unless a valid business purpose exists and is approved by the IRS.

questions.question: Sunrise Industries, an S corporation with a calendar year end, needs to file its Form 1120-S. When is Form 1120-S for Sunrise Industries due, assuming no extensions are filed?
options: The 15th day of the 4th month after year end
The 15th day of the 4th month after year end, unless an extension is filed
The 15th day of the 3rd month after year end
The 15th day of the 3rd month after year end, but only if a valid extension is in place
questions.explanation: Form 1120-S is due by the 15th day of the 3rd month after the close of the tax year. For calendar year S corps, this is March 15. An automatic 6-month extension is available using Form 7004. This is earlier than Form 1040 to allow shareholders to receive their K-1s.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 15th day of the 4th month after year-end (April 15th for a calendar year) is the due date for partnerships filing Form 1065, not S corporations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The due date for Form 1120-S is not the 15th day of the 4th month after the year end, even without an extension. This is the due date for C corporations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - S corporations, using Form 1120-S, must file their tax returns by the 15th day of the 3rd month following the close of their tax year. For a calendar-year S corporation, this date is March 15th, allowing shareholders sufficient time to prepare their individual returns with the K-1 information.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the 15th day of the 3rd month after year end is the correct due date, it is not contingent on an extension being in place. The base due date is March 15th.

questions.educational: The earlier filing deadline for S corporations (March 15th for calendar year filers) compared to individuals (April 15th) is intentional. This allows S corporation shareholders to receive Schedule K-1s from the S corporation in time to accurately prepare and file their individual income tax returns (Form 1040) by the individual filing deadline. Understanding these deadlines and the flow of information between entities and individuals is crucial for accurate tax compliance.
questions.examTip: Remember that the S corporation deadline is earlier than the individual deadline. On the exam, pay close attention to the entity type and the corresponding filing deadline to avoid common mistakes.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corps Submit Sooner: Think 'S' for 'Sooner' to remember that S corporations have an earlier filing deadline than individuals.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the filing deadline for S corporations (Form 1120-S) with that of C corporations or individual income tax returns. The exam exploits the misconception that all business returns are due on April 15th, or the 15th day of the 4th month after year-end, leading to the selection of incorrect options.

questions.question: Jane is a shareholder in an S corporation, Willow Creek Farms. Willow Creek generates qualified business income (QBI). For purposes of calculating Jane's Section 199A qualified business income deduction, S corporation shareholders:
options: Calculate QBI based on their distributive share of qualified items
Calculate QBI at the corporate level, and then allocate the deduction pro-rata to shareholders based on ownership percentage.
Must elect to claim the deduction on Form 1120-S, which then flows through as a single item to the shareholder's Schedule K-1.
May not claim the deduction directly; instead, the S corporation must reduce taxable income by the amount of QBI before passing through income.
questions.explanation: S corporation shareholders calculate QBI based on their pro-rata share of qualified items from the S corp. The deduction is claimed on the shareholder's personal return. W-2 wages and UBIA of qualified property also flow through for limitation calculations.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Section 199A allows eligible self-employed individuals and small business owners to deduct up to 20% of their qualified business income (QBI). For S corporations, QBI, W-2 wages, and UBIA (unadjusted basis immediately after acquisition) are calculated at the entity level and then passed through to the shareholders based on their pro-rata share, allowing shareholders to calculate their individual 199A deduction.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The QBI deduction is calculated at the shareholder level, not the corporate level. While the QBI itself is determined at the S-corp level, the deduction is based on the shareholder's specific circumstances.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The QBI deduction is not elected on Form 1120-S. The S corporation reports QBI items on Schedule K-1, but the shareholder calculates the deduction on their individual return.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporation shareholders are explicitly allowed to claim the QBI deduction, subject to certain limitations. The corporation does not reduce taxable income by QBI.

questions.educational: The Section 199A deduction was created to provide a tax benefit to small business owners and self-employed individuals, similar to the corporate tax rate reduction under the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. Understanding pass-through taxation is crucial, as items retain their character when passed to shareholders, allowing them to claim deductions and credits on their individual returns based on the S corporation's activities. This contrasts with C corporations, where the corporation itself pays taxes on its income.
questions.examTip: Remember that for the Section 199A deduction, the S corporation acts as a conduit, passing through QBI, W-2 wages, and UBIA to its shareholders. Focus on understanding how these items are calculated at the corporate level and then allocated to the shareholders for their individual deduction calculations.
questions.memoryAid: QBI flows 'S'traight through to the shareholder's 1040.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly believe that the QBI deduction is calculated and claimed at the S corporation level. A common trap is overlooking the pass-through nature of S corporations, leading to the incorrect assumption that the corporation itself claims the deduction rather than the shareholders based on their distributive share.

questions.question: A shareholder has $10,000 stock basis and $5,000 debt basis. The S corp passes through a $12,000 loss. The shareholder can deduct:
options: $15,000 (stock plus debt basis)
$12,000 (full loss)
$10,000 now, $2,000 reduces debt basis and is deductible
$10,000 (stock basis only)
questions.explanation: The shareholder can deduct the full $12,000 loss because combined basis is $15,000 ($10,000 stock + $5,000 debt). First, stock basis is reduced to zero ($10,000 absorbed). Then, debt basis is reduced by $2,000 (from $5,000 to $3,000). No loss is suspended.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the shareholder's total basis is $15,000 ($10,000 stock + $5,000 debt), the question asks how much of the loss the shareholder can *deduct*. The full loss can be deducted, but the question is asking about the deduction amount, not just the total available basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An S corporation shareholder can deduct losses to the extent of their stock basis and direct debt basis in the corporation. Here, the shareholder's combined basis is $15,000 ($10,000 stock + $5,000 debt), which is sufficient to absorb the entire $12,000 loss. Therefore, the shareholder can deduct the full $12,000 loss.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While it's true that the loss first reduces stock basis and then debt basis, this option incorrectly implies that only $10,000 is deductible now, with the remaining $2,000 deductible later. The full $12,000 is deductible in the current year because the shareholder has sufficient basis to absorb the loss.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An S corporation shareholder's deductible loss is not limited to their stock basis alone. They can also deduct losses to the extent of their direct debt basis in the corporation. This option ignores the $5,000 debt basis, leading to an incorrect deduction amount.

questions.educational: S corporation shareholders can deduct losses passed through to them, but the deduction is limited to their basis in the S corporation stock and any direct loans they've made to the S corporation. This limitation prevents shareholders from deducting losses exceeding their investment, reflecting the general tax principle that losses cannot exceed at-risk amounts. Understanding basis is critical for S corporation taxation, as it affects not only loss deductibility but also the taxability of distributions.
questions.examTip: Remember the basis ordering rules for S corporations: losses first reduce stock basis, then debt basis. Any losses exceeding the combined basis are suspended and carried forward indefinitely until the shareholder regains sufficient basis.
questions.memoryAid: SILO: Stock basis, Income increases basis, Losses decrease basis, Outflows (distributions) decrease basis. Think of losses flowing into the SILO and reducing the stock and debt.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the order in which losses are applied against basis. The exam exploits the misconception that debt basis is used before stock basis, or that losses can be deducted without regard to basis limitations. Test-takers often incorrectly add stock and debt basis together without considering the proper application of losses.

questions.question: Mark, an S corporation shareholder, has previously reduced his debt basis due to losses exceeding his stock basis. Now, the S corporation generates $50,000 of income. When an S corporation generates income after debt basis has been reduced by prior losses:
options: Income has no effect on previously reduced debt basis
The shareholder must contribute new capital
Income first restores debt basis, then stock basis
Income first restores stock basis, then debt basis
questions.explanation: Net income items first restore stock basis (from zero up). Only after stock basis is restored to the amount before any debt-basis-reducing losses does income restore debt basis. This ordering ensures proper treatment of subsequent distributions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Income does affect previously reduced debt basis. S corporation rules specifically outline how income restores basis after losses have reduced both stock and debt basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A shareholder is not required to contribute new capital when an S corporation generates income after losses have reduced debt basis. The income itself is used to restore basis according to the prescribed ordering rules.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Income does not first restore debt basis. The correct ordering, as per Subchapter S rules, prioritizes the restoration of stock basis before any debt basis is restored.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - According to IRC Section 1367, when an S corporation generates income after losses have reduced both stock and debt basis, the income first restores the shareholder's stock basis up to its original level. Only after the stock basis is fully restored does the income then restore the debt basis.

questions.educational: The basis rules for S corporations are crucial for determining the taxability of distributions and the deductibility of losses. The ordering rules for restoring basis (stock first, then debt) prevent shareholders from inappropriately recognizing gains on distributions or claiming losses beyond their economic investment. Understanding these rules is essential for accurate tax planning and compliance in the S corporation context, a common entity choice for small businesses.
questions.examTip: Remember the mnemonic 'BSD' - Basis, Stock, Debt - to recall the order in which losses reduce basis (stock first, then debt) and 'S to D' - Stock to Debt - to remember the order in which income restores basis. This is a frequently tested area on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'Stocking up before Debts': Stock basis needs to be 'stocked up' (restored) before debt basis can be addressed.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the order in which income restores basis. The exam exploits the misconception that income first restores debt basis, then stock basis. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that debt basis is restored before stock basis, leading to the selection of the wrong option.

questions.question: A shareholder sells S corporation stock for $75,000. The shareholder's adjusted basis is $50,000. The gain recognized is:
options: $75,000 ordinary income
$25,000 ordinary income
$25,000 capital gain
$0 - S corp sales are tax-free
questions.explanation: The sale of S corporation stock results in capital gain or loss (amount realized minus adjusted basis). Here: $75,000 - $50,000 = $25,000 gain. The character is capital (long or short-term depending on holding period), not ordinary income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The sale of S corporation stock generally results in capital gain or loss, not ordinary income. Ordinary income typically arises from the operation of the business itself, not the sale of ownership interests.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the calculation of $25,000 is correct ($75,000 - $50,000), the character of the gain is capital, not ordinary. Ordinary income would be incorrect unless there was a specific recharacterization rule that applied, which is not indicated in the question.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The sale of S corporation stock is treated as the sale of a capital asset. The gain is calculated as the amount realized ($75,000) less the adjusted basis ($50,000), resulting in a $25,000 capital gain. The holding period determines whether it's a short-term or long-term capital gain.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The sale of S corporation stock is a taxable event. The shareholder is selling an asset (the stock), and any gain realized is subject to capital gains tax. S corporations themselves are pass-through entities, but the sale of their stock is not tax-free.

questions.educational: The sale of S corporation stock is treated similarly to the sale of other corporate stock for tax purposes. The gain or loss is generally capital in nature. Understanding the difference between capital gains/losses and ordinary income is crucial for the EA exam, as it impacts tax rates and deduction limitations. This also connects to partnership interest sales, which have similar rules but can have ordinary income components under Section 751.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between the tax treatment of S corporation operations (pass-through of income/loss) and the sale of S corporation stock (capital gain/loss). Pay attention to holding periods to determine short-term vs. long-term capital gain rates.
questions.memoryAid: SOS: Sale of Stock = Ordinary... NOT! It's Capital Gain/Loss.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that the sale of S corporation stock is treated differently than the sale of other capital assets. A common trap is overlooking the general rule that the sale of stock results in capital gain or loss, leading to the selection of incorrect options suggesting ordinary income or a tax-free transaction.

questions.question: GreenTech Solutions, an S corporation, made a $5,000 cash donation to a local homeless shelter. Charitable contributions made by an S corporation:
options: Are limited to 10% of the S corporation's taxable income before the charitable contribution deduction, similar to C corporations.
Are not deductible by the S corporation or its shareholders, as they are considered personal expenses of the business owners.
Pass through to shareholders who apply their own AGI limitations
Are deducted at the corporate level, reducing the S corporation's taxable income and therefore the amount of income passed through to shareholders.
questions.explanation: S corporation charitable contributions are separately stated items that pass through to shareholders on Schedule K-1. Each shareholder deducts their share subject to their own AGI limitations (60%, 30%, or 20% depending on the type of contribution).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations pass through charitable contributions to shareholders; the 10% limitation applies to C corporations, not S corporations or their shareholders.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Charitable contributions are deductible, but the deduction flows through to the shareholders, not disallowed entirely.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Charitable contributions made by an S corporation are treated as separately stated items that pass through to the shareholders on Schedule K-1. Each shareholder then includes their share of the contribution on their individual return, subject to their own adjusted gross income (AGI) limitations under IRC Section 170(b). These limitations vary depending on the type of property contributed and the type of organization receiving the contribution.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations are pass-through entities; deductions generally flow through to the shareholders' individual income tax returns, not deducted at the corporate level.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities, designed to avoid double taxation. Instead of the corporation paying taxes on its income and then the shareholders paying taxes on dividends, the income (and deductions) flow directly to the shareholders. This treatment extends to charitable contributions, allowing shareholders to utilize these deductions subject to their individual AGI limitations, mirroring the treatment of charitable contributions made directly by individuals.
questions.examTip: Remember that S corporations are pass-through entities. On the EA exam, always consider whether an item is reported at the corporate level or passed through to the shareholders. Pay close attention to separately stated items on Schedule K-1.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corps 'Share' Charitable Contributions: The charitable contribution deduction is 'shared' with the shareholders.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly believe that S corporations can directly deduct charitable contributions. A common trap is overlooking the pass-through nature of S corporations, leading to the incorrect assumption that the corporation itself claims the deduction rather than the shareholders on their individual returns.

questions.question: Sarah is a shareholder-employee of Oakwood Consulting, an S corporation. Sarah receives a salary of $80,000 per year. S corporation shareholder-employee wages are subject to:
