Any shareholder sells their stock to an individual who is not a U.S. citizen or permanent resident, thereby violating the restrictions on eligible S corporation shareholders.
The corporation fails to file Form 1120-S by the due date, even with extensions, resulting in an automatic revocation of the S corporation election due to non-compliance with IRS filing requirements.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(d)(2), an S election is automatically terminated effective on the day the corporation ceases to be a small business corporation. Events that trigger termination include: exceeding 100 shareholders, having an ineligible shareholder (nonresident alien, corporation, most trusts, partnership), issuing a second class of stock, or having more than 25% of gross receipts from passive investment income for 3 consecutive years while having accumulated E&P. Inadvertent terminations may be corrected under IRC §1362(f).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Revenue levels do not automatically terminate an S election. While high revenue might make a C corp more advantageous, it's not a terminating event.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1362(d)(2) states that an S corporation election terminates automatically if the corporation ceases to be a small business corporation. This includes exceeding the shareholder limit (currently 100), having an ineligible shareholder (e.g., a C corporation or most trusts), or having more than one class of stock. The termination is effective on the date the corporation fails to meet the requirements.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Stock sales only terminate the S election if the new shareholder is an ineligible type of shareholder (e.g., a corporation or a non-resident alien). A sale to an eligible shareholder is fine.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Failure to file Form 1120-S can result in penalties, but it does not automatically terminate the S corporation election. The IRS may revoke the election, but it's not automatic.

questions.educational: S corporation status allows a business to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders for federal tax purposes, avoiding double taxation at the corporate level. Maintaining S corporation eligibility is crucial. Understanding the specific requirements for S corporation status and the events that can trigger termination is essential for advising clients and ensuring compliance.
questions.examTip: Remember the key eligibility requirements for S corporations: no more than 100 shareholders, only one class of stock, and eligible shareholders. These are frequently tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: 100-1-E: 100 shareholders, 1 class of stock, Eligible shareholders (to remember S-corp requirements).
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly focus on financial performance or administrative errors as automatic termination events. The exam exploits the misconception that exceeding revenue thresholds or missing filing deadlines automatically terminates the S election, while the actual trigger is failing to meet the eligibility requirements outlined in the IRC.

questions.question: Global Manufacturing, a C corporation, incurred a net operating loss (NOL) in 2023. Under current law, how can Global Manufacturing utilize this NOL?
options: Be carried back 2 years to offset income, then carried forward 20 years, fully offsetting income in those years.
Be carried forward indefinitely but limited to 80% of taxable income in the carryforward year
Be carried back 5 years to offset taxable income, with no limitation on the amount of the NOL that can be used in those prior years.
Only offset 50% of taxable income in the year the NOL is incurred, with the remaining loss permanently disallowed.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §172 (as amended by the TCJA), NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2017 may only be carried forward indefinitely — no carryback is allowed (with limited exceptions for farming losses and certain insurance companies). The NOL deduction is limited to 80% of taxable income (computed without the NOL deduction) in the carryforward year. Pre-2018 NOLs continue to follow the prior 2-year carryback/20-year carryforward rules without the 80% limitation.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - NOLs cannot be carried back under current law. Also, carryforwards are limited to 80% of taxable income.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §172, as amended by the TCJA, allows NOLs generated in tax years beginning after December 31, 2017, to be carried forward indefinitely. However, the deduction is limited to 80% of taxable income (calculated without regard to the NOL deduction) in the carryforward year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - NOLs cannot be carried back under current law. Carryforwards are limited to 80% of taxable income.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While there was a 50% limitation in the past, current law allows for an 80% offset and indefinite carryforward.

questions.educational: The NOL deduction is designed to smooth out taxable income over time, recognizing that businesses can have fluctuating profits and losses. The TCJA's changes to NOL rules reflect a shift in tax policy, aiming to generate revenue and simplify tax administration. Understanding the pre- and post-TCJA rules is crucial for accurately calculating a corporation's taxable income and tax liability, a core competency tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the year the NOL was generated, as pre-2018 NOLs are subject to different rules (carryback 2 years, carryforward 20 years, no 80% limitation). The exam will often test your ability to distinguish between these rules.
questions.memoryAid: TCJA = The Carryback Just Abandoned (for most NOLs after 2017).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the current NOL rules with those from prior years. A common trap is remembering the pre-TCJA rules regarding NOL carrybacks, which are no longer generally applicable. The exam tests whether you are up-to-date on the current NOL carryforward rules and the 80% limitation.

questions.question: John is a partner in ABC Partnership. He receives a $10,000 cash distribution and property with a $5,000 FMV. Before the distribution, his outside basis was $12,000. In this nonliquidating distribution, John generally:
options: Recognizes gain equal to the amount distributed, calculated as the sum of cash and the fair market value of property received, regardless of the partner's outside basis or the partnership's inside basis.
Recognizes gain only to the extent cash received exceeds the partner's outside basis; noncash property takes a carryover basis limited to the partner's remaining basis

Always recognizes gain or loss based on the difference between the fair market value of distributed assets and the partner's outside basis, with losses recognized only in liquidating distributions under specific circumstances.

Defers all gain for 5 years, provided the distributed property is held for at least that period and is used in a qualified trade or business, with gain recognized in the year of disposition if sold before the 5-year mark.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §731(a), a partner generally does not recognize gain on a current distribution unless the cash received exceeds the partner's outside basis. No loss is recognized on a current distribution. Under IRC §732(a), distributed property (other than cash) takes the partnership's basis (carryover basis), but limited to the partner's remaining outside basis after reducing for cash received. The partner's outside basis is reduced by the amount of cash and the basis of property received.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Gain is not automatically recognized equal to the entire distribution. Gain is only recognized to the extent that cash distributed exceeds the partner's basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §731(a) dictates that a partner recognizes gain in a current distribution only to the extent that cash received exceeds their outside basis in the partnership. IRC §732(a) states that noncash property takes a carryover basis, but this basis cannot exceed the partner's remaining outside basis after accounting for any cash received.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Gain or loss is not *always* recognized. Gain is recognized only when cash exceeds basis, and losses are generally not recognized in nonliquidating distributions.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no general rule to defer gain from partnership distributions for 5 years. Gain is recognized in the current year if cash exceeds basis.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation aims to prevent double taxation while allowing flexibility in allocating income and losses. The rules regarding current distributions are designed to allow partners to receive distributions without triggering immediate tax consequences, as long as they don't receive more cash than their investment (outside basis) in the partnership. This aligns with the broader concept of basis tracking, which is crucial for accurately determining gain or loss upon the eventual sale of the partnership interest.
questions.examTip: Remember the ordering rules for basis adjustments in partnership distributions: first reduce basis by cash received, then by the basis of distributed property. This is a common area for errors on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: CASH first, then PROPERTY. (Cash reduces basis first, then property.)
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly assume that any distribution from a partnership triggers immediate gain recognition. The exam exploits the misconception that cash and property distributions are treated the same way for gain recognition purposes. It's crucial to remember that gain is only recognized to the extent cash exceeds basis, and property takes a carryover basis (limited by the partner's remaining basis).

questions.question: An S corporation shareholder's loan basis (from loans made directly by the shareholder to the corporation) is:
options: Combined with stock basis for determining deductible losses, but treated as a single basis that cannot be reduced below zero, with any excess loss permanently disallowed and forfeited.
A separate basis that may be reduced by losses only after stock basis reaches zero; any reduction must be restored by subsequent income before the shareholder may increase stock basis

Not relevant for tax purposes except when determining the character of distributions exceeding stock basis, where it is treated as a capital contribution increasing the amount considered a return of capital.

Only relevant for gain calculations on sale, where it increases the shareholder's amount realized, but it does not affect loss deductibility or the taxability of distributions during the S corporation's operation.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1367 and Treas. Reg. §1.1367-2, a shareholder may deduct S corporation losses against loan basis after stock basis is reduced to zero. However, loan basis must be restored (by subsequent net income) before stock basis can be increased. Repayment of a loan that has been reduced results in capital gain to the shareholder. Importantly, only direct shareholder loans create debt basis — guaranteed loans or loans from related parties do not.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Shareholder loan basis is a separate basis, tracked independently of stock basis. Losses reduce loan basis only after stock basis is exhausted, and any reductions must be restored before stock basis can be increased.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1367(b)(2) and Treas. Reg. §1.1367-2 dictate that losses exceeding a shareholder's stock basis can reduce their loan basis (direct loans only). Subsequent income first restores any loan basis reductions before increasing stock basis. This ensures that shareholders don't avoid tax on loan repayments after taking losses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Shareholder loan basis is relevant for determining the deductibility of losses. Losses can be deducted to the extent of both stock and loan basis, and distributions exceeding stock basis are generally treated as capital gains, not returns of capital.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Shareholder loan basis is relevant for determining the deductibility of losses during the S corporation's operation, not just at the time of sale. It also affects the taxability of distributions.

questions.educational: The concept of loan basis in S corporations exists to prevent shareholders from using S corporation losses to offset other income without bearing the economic risk. By requiring shareholders to reduce loan basis after stock basis is exhausted, the tax law ensures that they are truly at risk for the losses they deduct. This aligns with the broader tax principle of matching deductions with economic substance, a key concept in business taxation.
questions.examTip: Remember that only direct loans from the shareholder to the S corporation create loan basis. Guarantees of corporate debt or loans from related parties do NOT create loan basis for loss deduction purposes on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: SILO: Stock basis first, then Income restores Loan basis, then Offset losses against loan basis.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the treatment of stock basis and loan basis in S corporations. A common trap is assuming that they are combined or that loan basis is irrelevant. The exam tests whether you understand that loan basis is a separate basis that can be reduced by losses only after stock basis is exhausted and must be restored before stock basis can be increased.

questions.question: Sunrise Industries is an S corporation with several shareholders. They wish to use a fiscal year ending September 30 for business reasons. An S corporation must generally use which tax year?
options: Any fiscal year chosen by the shareholders, provided all shareholders unanimously agree and the IRS approves the selection based on administrative convenience.
A calendar year ending December 31, unless a valid business purpose is established or a Section 444 election is made
A fiscal year ending June 30, as this aligns with the natural business cycle for many agricultural and seasonal businesses, regardless of the S corporation's actual activities.
The same tax year as its largest shareholder, determined by percentage of ownership, unless the shareholder is also an S corporation or a partnership with a different permitted year-end.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1378, an S corporation must use the calendar year (ending December 31) as its tax year, unless it can establish a business purpose for a different year to the satisfaction of the IRS, or it makes a Section 444 election (which allows a fiscal year ending September, October, or November with a required payment). The required year restriction prevents deferral of income to shareholders.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While shareholder agreement might be relevant in some corporate decisions, the IRS primarily focuses on business purpose or a Section 444 election, not shareholder preference, for S corp tax year determination.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1378 mandates a calendar year for S corporations unless a valid business purpose is established and approved by the IRS, or a Section 444 election is made. The Section 444 election allows for a fiscal year with a deferral period of no more than three months, subject to required payments under IRC §7519.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a June 30 year-end *can* be justified, it's not automatically permitted for all S corporations simply because it's common in certain industries. A valid business purpose specific to the *company* must be demonstrated.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations are generally required to use a calendar year, not the tax year of their largest shareholder. The shareholder's tax year is irrelevant to the S corporation's filing requirements.

questions.educational: The requirement for S corporations to primarily use a calendar year is rooted in preventing shareholders from deferring income taxes. If an S corporation could freely choose a fiscal year, shareholders could potentially delay reporting their share of the S corporation's income, thereby delaying tax payments. Understanding the rules around permissible tax years is crucial for advising clients on S corporation formation and compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to keywords like 'generally,' 'unless,' and 'exceptions' when dealing with S corporation tax year requirements. Remember the calendar year default and the two exceptions: business purpose and Section 444 election.
questions.memoryAid: S Corps: 'See' the Calendar (year) unless '444' or 'Business Purpose' saves the day.
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly believe that S corporations have complete flexibility in choosing their tax year. The exam exploits the misconception that shareholder preference is sufficient justification for a fiscal year. It's crucial to remember that S corporations generally must use a calendar year unless a valid business purpose is established or a Section 444 election is made.

questions.question: XYZ Corp. distributes $50,000 to its shareholders. XYZ's current E&P is $30,000, and accumulated E&P is $10,000. A distribution from a C corporation to its shareholders is treated as a dividend to the extent of:
options: The corporation's cash balance at the end of the tax year, without considering liabilities.
The corporation's current and accumulated earnings and profits (E&P)
The shareholder's adjusted basis in the stock, representing their capital investment.
The corporation's gross revenue less cost of goods sold, reflecting operational income.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §316, a dividend is any distribution of property from a corporation to its shareholders out of either current earnings and profits (current year E&P) or accumulated earnings and profits (E&P from prior years). Distributions are sourced first from current E&P, then from accumulated E&P. Distributions exceeding total E&P are treated as return of capital (reducing stock basis), and amounts exceeding basis are treated as capital gain.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Distributions are taxable as dividends to the extent of E&P, not simply the cash balance. Cash balance doesn't reflect profitability or prior distributions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §316, a distribution from a C corporation to its shareholders is treated as a dividend to the extent it is made from the corporation's current and accumulated earnings and profits (E&P). E&P represents the corporation's economic capacity to pay dividends.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Distributions exceeding E&P reduce basis, but are not dividends to the extent of basis. Basis only becomes relevant after E&P is exhausted.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Gross revenue less cost of goods sold represents gross profit, not E&P. E&P is a more comprehensive measure of a corporation's ability to pay dividends.

questions.educational: The concept of Earnings and Profits (E&P) is crucial in corporate taxation because it determines the extent to which distributions to shareholders are taxed as dividends. E&P is not the same as taxable income or retained earnings; it's a unique calculation designed to reflect the corporation's economic capacity to pay dividends. Understanding E&P is essential for accurately determining the tax consequences of corporate distributions, a frequent topic on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that distributions are taxed as dividends to the extent of E&P, then as a return of capital (reducing basis), and finally as capital gain. Focus on mastering the E&P calculation, as it's a common area for exam questions.
questions.memoryAid: E&P before ROC (Return of Capital) and CG (Capital Gain): Earnings and Profits determine the dividend portion first!
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the source of distributions with the determination of dividend income. A common trap is focusing on the corporation's cash balance or shareholder's stock basis. The exam tests whether you understand that distributions are treated as dividends to the extent of current and accumulated earnings and profits (E&P).

questions.question: Smith & Jones Partnership has been operating for 20 years. Due to irreconcilable differences, all partners except Smith decide to leave the partnership and cease conducting any partnership business. Under current law (post-TCJA), a partnership terminates for tax purposes when:
options: 50% or more of the total interests in partnership capital and profits are transferred within a 12-month period, triggering a technical termination.
No part of any business, financial operation, or venture of the partnership continues to be carried on by any of its partners
The managing partner resigns and provides written notice to all remaining partners, thereby dissolving the partnership according to standard partnership agreements.
Two or more partners disagree on fundamental operational decisions, leading to a formal vote to dissolve the partnership as outlined in the partnership agreement.
questions.explanation: Under the TCJA amendments to IRC §708(b), the "technical termination" rule (when 50% or more of interests were transferred in 12 months) was repealed for partnership tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. Now, a partnership terminates for tax purposes only when no part of any business, financial operation, or venture of the partnership continues to be carried on by any partner in a partnership. This simplification eliminated many unintended termination consequences.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a transfer of 50% or more of partnership interests within 12 months *can* trigger a technical termination, the question describes an actual termination where the business ceases to operate. The transfer rule is about ownership changes, not cessation of business.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §708(b)(1)(A) states that a partnership terminates when no part of any business, financial operation, or venture of the partnership continues to be carried on by any of its partners. This means that if the partnership's activities cease and no partners continue the business, the partnership is considered terminated for tax purposes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The resignation of a managing partner, while potentially disruptive, does not automatically terminate a partnership for tax purposes. The partnership can continue to operate with the remaining partners.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Disagreements among partners, even significant ones, do not automatically terminate a partnership for tax purposes. The partnership can continue if the business continues to operate, even with internal conflict.

questions.educational: The termination of a partnership has significant tax consequences, including the potential for gain or loss recognition by the partners and the closing of the partnership's tax year. Understanding the rules for partnership termination is crucial for proper tax planning and compliance. The TCJA simplified the termination rules to reduce unintended consequences and administrative burdens.
questions.examTip: Remember that the repeal of the technical termination rule under TCJA significantly simplified partnership tax. Focus on the 'cessation of business' rule as the primary trigger for partnership termination on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Partnership Termination: No Business = No Partnership (for tax purposes).
questions.commonMistake: Test-takers often incorrectly focus on changes in ownership or disagreements among partners as termination events. The exam exploits the misconception that a transfer of partnership interests or internal disputes automatically terminates the partnership. It's crucial to remember that a partnership terminates when no part of its business continues to be carried on by any of its partners.

questions.question: An S corporation with accumulated E&P from C corporation years faces what consequence if its passive investment income exceeds 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive years?
options: Nothing – S corporations, once properly elected, are permanently shielded from any restrictions related to passive investment income, regardless of its proportion to gross receipts.
The S corporation election is automatically terminated, and the corporation reverts to C corporation status
The corporation must pay a special penalty tax, calculated as 21% of the excess passive investment income above 25% of gross receipts, but only if it does not distribute all earnings.
The shareholders must pay an additional 10% surtax on their pro rata share of the S corporation's passive investment income exceeding 25% of gross receipts, reported on Schedule K-1.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(d)(3), if an S corporation has accumulated E&P (from prior C corporation years) and passive investment income (rents, royalties, dividends, interest, annuities, gains from sales of securities) exceeding 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive tax years, the S election is automatically terminated at the beginning of the fourth year. Additionally, under IRC §1375, an excess net passive income tax (at the 21% corporate rate) is imposed for each year that the passive income exceeds 25% of gross receipts while there is accumulated E&P.
questions.subtopic: Passive Income Effects on S Corps

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations with C corporation E&P *are* affected by excessive passive income. The election can be terminated.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §1362(d)(3), if an S corporation has accumulated E&P from C corporation years and its passive investment income exceeds 25% of its gross receipts for three consecutive tax years, its S corporation election is terminated, effective on the first day of the fourth tax year. This means the corporation will revert to C corporation status and be taxed under Subchapter C of the Internal Revenue Code.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a penalty tax *can* apply to S corporations with C corporation E&P and excessive passive income, the consequence after three years is termination of the S election, not just a tax. The tax rate is also incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The shareholders do not pay a surtax directly on the passive income. The corporation's S election is terminated after three years of exceeding the passive income threshold.

questions.educational: The rule regarding passive income limitations for S corporations with prior C corporation E&P is designed to prevent companies from using the S corporation structure primarily as a tax shelter for investment income. Without this rule, a C corporation could accumulate earnings, elect S status, and then distribute those earnings as dividends taxed at the shareholder level, potentially avoiding corporate-level tax. This rule is closely related to the concept of 'built-in gains' tax, which also aims to prevent tax avoidance when a C corporation converts to an S corporation.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the specific wording of questions regarding S corporations and passive income. Distinguish between the excess net passive income tax (IRC §1375) and the termination of the S election (IRC §1362(d)(3)). The former is an annual tax, while the latter is a more severe consequence after three consecutive years.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'PET' - Passive income Exceeding 25% for Three years = S election Termination.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that passive income is never a concern for S corporations or that the consequence is merely a penalty tax. The exam exploits the misconception that S corporations are immune to passive income restrictions. It's crucial to remember that excessive passive income for three consecutive years can lead to automatic termination of the S election.

questions.question: David gifts his daughter, Emily, a 20% interest in his family partnership, which operates a retail business where capital is a material income-producing factor. Under IRC §704(e), a family member who receives a partnership interest by gift:
options: Is treated the same as any other partner, with no special rules regarding allocation of income or losses, provided the partnership agreement explicitly states that all partners share profits and losses in proportion to their ownership interests, regardless of the source of the capital.
Is recognized as a partner only if capital is a material income-producing factor; the donor must be allocated reasonable compensation for services before income is allocated to the donee

Cannot be a partner unless they actively participate in the day-to-day management of the partnership's business and demonstrate significant expertise in the retail industry, thereby contributing more than just capital to the partnership's success.

Must contribute cash equal to the gifted interest's value to the partnership within 30 days of receiving the gift, ensuring that the partnership's capital account remains balanced and that the donee has a financial stake equivalent to their ownership percentage.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(e), family partnerships are subject to special rules. A donee family member is recognized as a partner if capital is a material income-producing factor and the donee has genuine ownership and control. The donor-partner must be allocated reasonable compensation for services rendered to the partnership before allocating income between the partners. The donee's share of income cannot exceed the proportionate share based on the donated capital interest.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This is incorrect because IRC §704(e) does have special rules for family partnerships, especially regarding reasonable compensation for services rendered by the donor. The partnership agreement alone does not override these rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §704(e) dictates that a family member receiving a partnership interest by gift is recognized as a partner only if capital is a material income-producing factor for the partnership. Furthermore, the donor must be reasonably compensated for their services to the partnership before any income is allocated to the donee to prevent artificial shifting of income.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This is incorrect. While active participation can strengthen the case for recognition, it's not a strict requirement under IRC §704(e). The primary focus is on whether capital is a material income-producing factor and reasonable compensation is allocated.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This is incorrect. There is no requirement under IRC §704(e) for the donee to contribute cash equal to the gifted interest's value. The interest is a gift, not a purchase.

questions.educational: IRC §704(e) exists to prevent taxpayers from shifting income to family members in lower tax brackets through the use of partnerships. By requiring capital to be a material income-producing factor and ensuring reasonable compensation for services, the IRS aims to ensure that partnership allocations reflect economic reality and prevent tax avoidance. This ties into the broader theme of partnership taxation, where allocations must have substantial economic effect.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to questions involving family partnerships. Remember the two key requirements of IRC §704(e): capital as a material income-producing factor and reasonable compensation for the donor's services. These are frequently tested.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'Family Partnership Rules' as 'Capital & Compensation' - Capital must be material, and the donor deserves Compensation.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that family members are treated the same as any other partner or that they cannot be partners at all. The exam exploits the misconception that gifting a partnership interest has no special rules. It's crucial to remember that if capital is a material income-producing factor, the donor must be allocated reasonable compensation for services before income is allocated to the donee.

questions.question: Alpha Corp. merges with Beta Corp., forming a new entity called Gamma Corp. This merger is completed under applicable state law. Under IRC §368(a), which of the following is a Type A reorganization?
options: A stock redemption
A statutory merger or consolidation under state law
A transfer of assets for stock in a newly formed corporation
A simple purchase of a corporation's assets for cash
questions.explanation: Under IRC §368(a)(1)(A), a Type A reorganization is a statutory merger or consolidation effected under applicable state law (or federal law or the law of a foreign jurisdiction). In a merger, one corporation absorbs another; in a consolidation, two corporations combine to form a new entity. Key requirements include continuity of interest (significant portion of consideration must be stock) and continuity of business enterprise. Type A reorganizations offer the most flexibility in consideration types.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A stock redemption is a corporation's repurchase of its own shares from its shareholders, which is governed by IRC §302 and does not qualify as a tax-free reorganization under IRC §368.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A Type A reorganization, as defined in IRC §368(a)(1)(A), is a statutory merger or consolidation. This involves one corporation absorbing another (merger) or two corporations combining to form a new entity (consolidation), both carried out under state law. The key is that the transaction must qualify as a merger or consolidation under the relevant state or federal statute.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A transfer of assets for stock in a newly formed corporation describes a Type D reorganization under IRC §368(a)(1)(D), specifically involving a divisive reorganization where assets are transferred to a controlled corporation, not a Type A reorganization.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A simple purchase of a corporation's assets for cash is a taxable transaction, not a tax-free reorganization under IRC §368. This is generally treated as a sale of assets, and the selling corporation recognizes gain or loss on the sale.

questions.educational: Corporate reorganizations are complex transactions that allow companies to restructure without triggering immediate tax consequences. These reorganizations, governed by IRC §368, aim to facilitate legitimate business adjustments. The 'continuity of interest' and 'continuity of business enterprise' doctrines are crucial in determining whether a transaction qualifies as a tax-free reorganization, ensuring that the shareholders retain a substantial equity interest and the business continues in some modified form.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific requirements of each reorganization type (A, B, C, D, E, F, G). Memorizing the basic definitions and key differences will help you quickly eliminate incorrect answer choices.
questions.memoryAid: A-Merger: A is for 'Absorb' - one company absorbs another in a merger.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates focus on the general concept of mergers but fail to recall the specific definitions under IRC §368. A common trap is assuming that any combination of companies qualifies as a Type A reorganization, overlooking the requirement for a statutory merger or consolidation under state law. Test-takers often incorrectly associate stock redemptions or asset transfers with Type A reorganizations.

questions.question: Tech Solutions Inc. is completely liquidating. Sarah, a shareholder, receives $100,000 in the liquidation. Her basis in Tech Solutions stock is $40,000. When a C corporation completely liquidates under IRC §331, shareholders generally:
options: Report the distribution as ordinary income to the extent of the corporation's accumulated earnings and profits, as if it were a dividend, and treat any excess as a return of capital, reducing the shareholder's basis.
Treat the liquidating distribution as full payment in exchange for their stock, recognizing capital gain or loss equal to the difference between the distribution amount and their stock basis

Do not recognize any gain or loss until the shareholder disposes of other assets received in the liquidation, at which point the basis of the stock is allocated among the assets received based on their relative fair market values.

Report the distribution as a dividend, taxable at the preferential dividend tax rates, but only if the corporation has sufficient current or accumulated earnings and profits to cover the distribution; otherwise, it's a return of capital.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §331, amounts received by a shareholder in a complete liquidation of a corporation are treated as full payment in exchange for the stock. The shareholder recognizes capital gain or loss equal to the difference between the amount received (FMV of property plus cash) and the shareholder's adjusted basis in the stock. Under IRC §336, the liquidating corporation recognizes gain or loss on the distribution of property as if it sold the property at FMV.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Liquidating distributions are treated as payments for stock, not as dividends taxed as ordinary income. While dividends are ordinary income, this scenario involves a complete liquidation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §331 explicitly states that amounts received by a shareholder in a complete liquidation are treated as full payment in exchange for their stock. The shareholder recognizes capital gain or loss to the extent the distribution exceeds or is less than their adjusted basis in the stock. This treatment aligns with the general principle that shareholders are essentially selling their ownership interest back to the corporation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Shareholders must recognize gain or loss in a corporate liquidation. The gain or loss is calculated at the time of the distribution, not deferred until later asset disposal.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Liquidating distributions are treated as payments for stock, not as dividends. While dividends are possible from a C-corp, a liquidating distribution is treated differently under §331.

questions.educational: Corporate liquidations represent the final winding down of a business entity. Understanding the tax implications for both the corporation (IRC §336) and the shareholders (IRC §331) is crucial. The general rule is that shareholders treat the liquidation as a sale of their stock, while the corporation recognizes gain or loss as if it sold its assets at fair market value. This ensures that all appreciation or depreciation is taxed before the corporation ceases to exist.
questions.examTip: Remember that the shareholder's basis in their stock is the key determinant of the gain or loss recognized in a corporate liquidation. Pay close attention to whether the question provides the shareholder's basis or if you need to calculate it.
questions.memoryAid: §331: Sale, Sale, Sale! (Liquidation treated as a sale of stock)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse liquidating distributions with ordinary dividends or other forms of income. A common trap is assuming that the distribution is taxed at ordinary income rates. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that no gain or loss is recognized, especially if they are thinking of tax-free reorganizations, or that the distribution is taxed as a dividend.

questions.question: Parent Co. owns 85% of Subsidiary Co. Parent Co. decides to liquidate Subsidiary Co., receiving all of its assets. Under IRC §332, a parent corporation that liquidates an 80%-or-more owned subsidiary:
options: Recognizes gain on the received assets, calculated using fair market value at the time of liquidation, less the parent's basis in the subsidiary's stock.
Recognizes no gain or loss; the subsidiary's asset bases carry over to the parent
Must pay a special liquidation tax, calculated as a percentage of the subsidiary's accumulated earnings and profits distributed to the parent company.

Recognizes loss on the received assets if the fair market value of the assets is less than the parent's adjusted basis in the subsidiary's stock, but only to the extent of previously recognized gains.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §332, when a parent corporation (owning 80% or more of the subsidiary's stock) liquidates the subsidiary, the parent recognizes no gain or loss. Under IRC §334(b)(1), the parent takes a carryover basis in the subsidiary's assets. The subsidiary also recognizes no gain or loss on distributions to the 80% parent under IRC §337. However, the subsidiary does recognize gain (not loss) on distributions to minority shareholders. The parent also inherits the subsidiary's tax attributes (NOLs, E&P, etc.) under IRC §381.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Under IRC §332, liquidations of 80%-or-more owned subsidiaries are generally tax-free to the parent. The parent does not recognize gain.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §332 dictates that no gain or loss is recognized by a parent corporation that owns at least 80% of a subsidiary when the subsidiary is liquidated. Furthermore, IRC §334(b)(1) states that the parent corporation takes a carryover basis in the assets received from the subsidiary, meaning the parent's basis is the same as the subsidiary's basis was.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - There is no special liquidation tax under IRC §332 for qualifying liquidations. The purpose of §332 is to allow tax-free liquidations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Under IRC §332, the parent corporation does not recognize a loss on the liquidation of its subsidiary. Loss recognition is disallowed.

questions.educational: IRC §332 is designed to facilitate corporate restructuring without triggering immediate taxation. This provision allows a parent company to absorb a subsidiary's assets and operations seamlessly, promoting efficiency within the corporate group. Understanding consolidated returns and intercompany transactions is crucial for mastering this area, as these concepts often intertwine with liquidation scenarios.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 80% ownership threshold is critical for §332 to apply. On the EA exam, carefully analyze ownership percentages to determine if the liquidation qualifies for nonrecognition treatment.
questions.memoryAid: 80% or more? No tax to explore! (on the parent, that is)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates forget the specific exception for liquidations of 80%-or-more owned subsidiaries. A common trap is assuming that gain or loss is always recognized on the transfer of assets. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that a special liquidation tax applies or that a loss is recognized, overlooking the carryover basis rule.

questions.question: When a C corporation elects S corporation status, which of the following tax consequences should be considered?
options: All accumulated E&P is immediately distributed and taxed to the shareholders as qualified dividends, and the corporation's basis in its assets is adjusted to fair market value.
Built-in gains tax on appreciated assets, LIFO recapture tax, passive investment income tax on accumulated E&P, and accounting method changes
No immediate tax consequences occur upon conversion; the election is seamless, with all prior C corporation tax attributes, such as NOL carryforwards, remaining fully available to the S corporation.

The corporation must liquidate and reform as a new entity, triggering recognition of all gains and losses at the corporate level and requiring a complete re-establishment of all business operations and legal standings.
questions.explanation: Converting from C to S status triggers several considerations: (1) the built-in gains (BIG) tax under IRC §1374 on net unrealized appreciation within 5 years; (2) LIFO recapture — if the corporation used LIFO inventory, it must include the LIFO reserve in income in the last C corporation year (IRC §1363(d)); (3) excess passive income tax under IRC §1375 if passive income exceeds 25% of gross receipts while accumulated E&P exists; and (4) potential accounting method changes. Planning these issues is critical.
questions.subtopic: Converting C Corp to S Corp

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - E&P is not automatically distributed upon S election. While distributions can be taxable dividends if E&P exists, there's no mandatory distribution or basis adjustment.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Converting from a C corporation to an S corporation triggers several potential tax consequences. The built-in gains tax under IRC §1374 applies to appreciated assets held at the time of conversion if sold within 5 years. LIFO recapture under IRC §1363(d) requires including the LIFO reserve in income in the last C corporation year. The passive investment income tax under IRC §1375 can apply if passive income exceeds 25% of gross receipts and the corporation has accumulated E&P. Finally, accounting method changes may be required to comply with S corporation tax rules.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The conversion is not seamless. Certain C corporation tax attributes, like NOL carryforwards, are restricted, and built-in gains are subject to tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An S election does not require liquidation and reformation. It's an election to change the tax status, not the legal entity.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a business to be taxed similarly to a partnership, avoiding double taxation. However, Congress included provisions like the built-in gains tax to prevent C corporations from avoiding corporate-level tax on appreciated assets by electing S status shortly before selling them. Understanding these transition rules is crucial for tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the timing of asset sales after an S election, as the built-in gains tax only applies to assets held at the time of conversion and sold within the subsequent 5-year recognition period. Remember to check for LIFO recapture as well.
questions.memoryAid: BIG LIPS: Built-In Gains, LIFO Recapture, Passive Income, S election (considerations).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates underestimate the tax implications of converting from a C corporation to an S corporation. A common trap is thinking the conversion is seamless. Test-takers often incorrectly assume that all accumulated E&P is immediately distributed or that the corporation must liquidate and reform, overlooking the potential for built-in gains tax, LIFO recapture, and passive investment income tax.

questions.question: Jane is a partner in the ABC Partnership. She is leaving the partnership this year and receiving a liquidating distribution. In which of the following scenarios can Jane recognize a loss from this distribution? A loss can only be recognized if:
options: The partnership's overall loss for the year exceeds Jane's share of partnership liabilities, and the liquidating distribution includes only cash.
The only property distributed is cash, unrealized receivables, and/or inventory, and the total is less than the partner's outside basis
The partner has held the interest for more than 5 years and has consistently reported losses from the partnership on their individual tax return.
The partnership files a special election under Section 754 to adjust the basis of partnership property, and the distribution includes substantially appreciated inventory.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §731(a)(2), a partner may recognize a loss on a liquidating distribution only if the distribution consists solely of cash, unrealized receivables, and/or inventory items, and the total amount of cash plus the basis of the receivables and inventory is less than the partner's outside basis. The loss is a capital loss. If any other property is distributed, no loss may be recognized — instead, the partner takes a substituted basis in the property.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A partnership loss does not automatically allow a partner to recognize a loss on liquidation. Loss recognition depends on the assets received and the partner's basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §731(a)(2) dictates that a partner can recognize a loss in a liquidating distribution only when the distribution consists solely of cash, unrealized receivables, and inventory, and the total adjusted basis of these assets is less than the partner's adjusted basis in their partnership interest (outside basis). This ensures that the partner's economic loss is recognized when they receive only these types of easily valued assets.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The holding period of the partnership interest is irrelevant for determining loss recognition on a liquidating distribution. Loss recognition depends on the assets received and the partner's basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A Section 754 election affects the basis of partnership property but does not, by itself, allow a partner to recognize a loss on a liquidating distribution. Loss recognition depends on the assets received and the partner's basis.

questions.educational: The rules surrounding liquidating distributions are designed to ensure that gains and losses are recognized appropriately as a partner exits the partnership. The limitation on loss recognition when other property is distributed is due to the difficulty in immediately valuing such property, deferring the loss until the distributed property is sold or disposed of. This aligns with the principle of matching income and expenses and preventing premature loss recognition.
questions.examTip: Remember the mnemonic 'CUI' (Cash, Unrealized Receivables, Inventory) when thinking about loss recognition in partnership liquidations. Only distributions consisting solely of these assets can trigger a loss for the departing partner on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: CUI or CRY (Cash, Receivables, Inventory) - you might CRY if you only get these in a liquidation and your basis is higher!
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates struggle with the limited circumstances under which a partner can recognize a loss on a liquidating distribution. A common trap is assuming that any loss by the partnership allows the partner to recognize a loss. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that the holding period of the partnership interest or a special election affects the ability to recognize a loss.

questions.question: For a stock redemption to qualify for sale or exchange treatment (capital gain) rather than dividend treatment, it must meet one of the tests under IRC §302(b). Which of the following qualifies?
options: Any repurchase of stock by the corporation, provided the shareholder has held the shares for at least one year and the corporation has sufficient earnings and profits to cover the distribution.
A substantially disproportionate redemption (shareholder's ownership drops below 80% of pre-redemption percentage and below 50% total voting power)

A redemption that increases the shareholder's ownership percentage due to other shareholders selling their shares back to the corporation at the same time, resulting in a higher proportional ownership.
A redemption using borrowed funds, as long as the interest rate on the borrowed funds is commercially reasonable and the redemption does not violate any debt covenants of the corporation.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §302(b)(2), a substantially disproportionate redemption qualifies for sale/exchange treatment if, after the redemption: (1) the shareholder owns less than 50% of the total combined voting power, and (2) the shareholder's percentage of voting stock is less than 80% of their pre-redemption percentage. Other qualifying tests include: a complete termination of interest (§302(b)(3)), a redemption not essentially equivalent to a dividend (§302(b)(1)), or a partial liquidation (§302(b)(4)).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The mere repurchase of stock doesn't guarantee sale or exchange treatment. It must meet one of the specific tests under IRC §302(b).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A substantially disproportionate redemption, as defined in IRC §302(b)(2), allows a shareholder to treat the redemption as a sale or exchange (capital gain/loss) rather than a dividend. The shareholder must own less than 50% of the total combined voting power after the redemption, and their percentage ownership of voting stock after the redemption must be less than 80% of their percentage ownership before the redemption.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A redemption that increases a shareholder's ownership is essentially the opposite of what's required for sale or exchange treatment under the disproportionate redemption or partial liquidation rules.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The source of funds used for the redemption (borrowed or otherwise) is irrelevant in determining whether the redemption qualifies for sale or exchange treatment. The redemption must still meet one of the tests under IRC §302(b).

questions.educational: IRC §302 aims to distinguish between genuine sales of stock back to the corporation (capital gain/loss treatment) and disguised dividends (ordinary income). Without these rules, corporations could easily distribute earnings as capital gains by simply repurchasing stock pro-rata from all shareholders. Understanding these rules is crucial for tax planning and advising clients on the most tax-efficient way to structure stock redemptions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific percentage thresholds in the §302(b)(2) substantially disproportionate redemption test (80% and 50%). Misreading these numbers is a common mistake.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'DISS' for Disproportionate, Interest Termination, Substantially Disproportionate, Sale (or partial liquidation). These are the key tests for sale or exchange treatment.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates misunderstand the requirements for a stock redemption to qualify for sale or exchange treatment. A common trap is assuming that any repurchase of stock qualifies. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that a redemption that increases ownership or uses borrowed funds qualifies, overlooking the specific tests under IRC §302(b).

questions.question: Dr. Smith is the sole shareholder of Smith Medical, an S corporation. He takes a small salary of $60,000 but receives distributions of $200,000. The IRS may recharacterize the S corporation distributions as wages if:
options: The S corporation has a history of paying dividends to shareholders in addition to distributions, indicating a potential attempt to avoid payroll taxes.
The shareholder-employee receives distributions but little or no salary, and the salary does not reflect reasonable compensation for services performed
The S corporation's revenue exceeds $500,000, suggesting the shareholder-employee should be taking a larger salary commensurate with the company's financial performance.
The shareholder owns less than 50% of the S corporation's stock, implying that their distributions are subject to different rules than those of a majority shareholder.
questions.explanation: The IRS scrutinizes S corporations where shareholder-employees receive minimal or no salary while taking significant distributions. Under IRC §7436, the IRS may recharacterize distributions as wages subject to FICA and income tax withholding if the compensation is not reasonable for the services provided. Factors considered include: training and experience, duties and responsibilities, comparable salaries, time devoted, dividend history, and compensation agreements.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations do not pay dividends; they make distributions. The IRS focuses on reasonable compensation, not the mere existence of distributions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The IRS recharacterizes S corporation distributions as wages under IRC §7436 when a shareholder-employee receives distributions but little or no salary, and the salary doesn't reflect reasonable compensation for the services they perform. This is to prevent S corporations from avoiding payroll taxes (FICA and income tax withholding) by disguising wages as distributions. The IRS looks at factors like training, experience, duties, responsibilities, comparable salaries, and time devoted to determine reasonable compensation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The IRS focuses on reasonable compensation for services performed, not solely on the company's revenue. High revenue doesn't automatically mean the salary is unreasonable.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The percentage of stock owned by the shareholder is not directly relevant to the IRS's determination of reasonable compensation and potential recharacterization of distributions as wages.

questions.educational: The IRS closely monitors S corporations to ensure shareholder-employees are paid reasonable compensation for their services. This is because S corporations are pass-through entities, and distributions are not subject to payroll taxes (Social Security and Medicare). By underpaying wages and taking distributions, shareholder-employees attempt to avoid these taxes, which is why the IRS can recharacterize distributions as wages subject to FICA and income tax withholding. This concept is crucial for understanding S corporation taxation and compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS's main concern with S corporation distributions is the avoidance of payroll taxes. If a shareholder-employee is performing significant services for the S corporation, they must receive a reasonable salary before taking distributions.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp: Salary Should Suffice (SSS). Make sure the salary is sufficient before taking distributions.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates focus on the distributions themselves, rather than the reasonableness of the salary paid to the shareholder-employee. A common trap is assuming that any distributions are automatically suspect. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that the existence of any dividends, the corporation's revenue, or the shareholder's ownership percentage are the determining factors, overlooking the reasonable compensation requirement.

questions.question: Under IRC §707(a)(2)(B), a transaction between a partner and a partnership may be recharacterized as a disguised sale if:
options: The partner contributes property and receives an equal value distribution within 2 years, and the distribution would not have been made but for the contribution

The partner receives any guaranteed payment from the partnership, regardless of whether it is for services rendered or the use of capital, and the payment is properly characterized as such under §707(c).

The partner contributes services to the partnership in exchange for a partnership interest, and the value of that interest is proportionate to the services provided, without any associated transfer of property.

The partner borrows money from the partnership at a commercially reasonable interest rate, and the loan is properly documented with a fixed repayment schedule and adequate security, reflecting an arm's-length transaction.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) and Treas. Reg. §1.707-3, a contribution of property by a partner followed by a related distribution from the partnership (or vice versa) may be treated as a disguised sale if, viewed together, the transfer and distribution are properly characterized as a sale. If a distribution occurs within 2 years of a contribution, there is a presumption of a disguised sale. Factors include: the timing and amount of the distribution, the entrepreneurial risks borne, and whether the distribution would have occurred regardless.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §707(a)(2)(B) addresses disguised sales, where a contribution of property by a partner is followed by a distribution that is, in substance, a sale. The key is whether the distribution would have occurred regardless of the contribution. If the distribution is tied to the contribution and occurs within two years, it's presumed to be a disguised sale, making this the correct answer.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are addressed under §707(c) and are treated as payments to a non-partner only for purposes of §61(a) and §162(a); they do not automatically trigger disguised sale treatment under §707(a)(2)(B).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A contribution of services for a partnership interest is generally a taxable event but does not constitute a disguised sale of property under §707(a)(2)(B), which specifically addresses contributions of property.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A legitimate loan from a partnership to a partner, with standard terms and documentation, is treated as a loan and does not trigger disguised sale rules, as there is an expectation of repayment and no transfer of ownership.

questions.educational: The disguised sale rules under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) prevent partners from disguising sales of property to a partnership as contributions followed by distributions to avoid recognizing gain. These rules are crucial in partnership taxation because contributions are generally tax-free, while sales are taxable. Understanding these rules is essential for accurately determining a partner's basis and the partnership's tax liabilities, a key area of the SEE2 exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the timing of contributions and distributions when analyzing partnership transactions. A distribution within two years of a contribution is a red flag for a potential disguised sale, requiring further analysis of the facts and circumstances.
questions.memoryAid: 2-Year Rule: Contribution then Distribution? Could be a Disguised Sale Solution!
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse disguised sales with other types of transactions between partners and partnerships. A common trap is assuming that any payment to a partner is a disguised sale. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that guaranteed payments, contributions of services, or loans from the partnership trigger disguised sale treatment, overlooking the specific requirements of IRC §707(a)(2)(B).

questions.question: Under IRC §1202, a noncorporate taxpayer may exclude from gross income what percentage of gain on the sale of Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) acquired after September 27, 2010 and held for more than 5 years?
options: 50%, with the remaining gain taxed at a maximum rate of 28% after considering AMT preferences.
75%, provided the qualified small business had less than $25 million in assets when the stock was issued.
100%, up to the greater of $10 million or 10 times the adjusted basis of the stock
90%, but only if the stock was acquired before February 18, 2009, and held for at least six years.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1202, a noncorporate taxpayer may exclude 100% of the gain from the sale of QSBS acquired after September 27, 2010 (75% for stock acquired between February 18, 2009 and September 27, 2010; 50% for stock acquired before that). The exclusion is limited to the greater of $10 million or 10 times the taxpayer's adjusted basis in the stock. QSBS requirements include: (1) the stock must be in a C corporation with gross assets not exceeding $50 million, (2) acquired at original issuance, and (3) held for more than 5 years.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The exclusion is 100% for QSBS acquired after September 27, 2010, not 50%. While a portion of the gain *could* be taxed at a maximum rate of 28% if the exclusion was less than 100%, that is not the correct exclusion percentage.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the $25 million asset test is correct, the exclusion percentage for stock acquired after September 27, 2010, is 100%, not 75%. The 75% exclusion applied to stock acquired before February 18, 2009.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §1202 allows a noncorporate taxpayer to exclude 100% of the gain from the sale of QSBS acquired after September 27, 2010, and held for more than 5 years, up to the greater of $10 million or 10 times the adjusted basis of the stock. This incentive encourages investment in small businesses.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The exclusion is 100% for QSBS acquired after September 27, 2010. The 90% exclusion applied to stock acquired after February 17, 2009, and before September 28, 2010.

questions.educational: The QSBS exclusion under IRC §1202 is designed to incentivize investment in small businesses by providing a significant tax break on the capital gains realized from the sale of qualified stock. Understanding the specific requirements for QSBS, including the asset size of the C corporation, the original issuance requirement, and the holding period, is crucial for advising clients on tax-efficient investment strategies. This also ties into understanding the differences in tax treatment between C corporations, S corporations, and partnerships.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates provided in QSBS questions, as the exclusion percentage hinges on the acquisition date of the stock. Also, remember the limitation on the exclusion: the greater of $10 million or 10 times the basis.
questions.memoryAid: Think '100/10' - 100% exclusion, up to $10 million (or 10x basis).
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates are unaware of the specific rules and limitations surrounding the exclusion of gain from the sale of Qualified Small Business Stock. A common trap is remembering the wrong percentage. Test-takers often incorrectly recall the 50% or 75% exclusion amounts that applied in earlier years, or they forget the dollar limitations on the exclusion.

questions.question: The Section 199A Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction is available to owners of which types of pass-through entities?
options: C corporations only, due to their direct taxation at the corporate level and the subsequent distribution of dividends to shareholders, which are then taxed again.
S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates with qualified business income
Only sole proprietorships, because the QBI flows directly to the individual owner's tax return, simplifying the calculation and application of the deduction.
Only partnerships, as the partnership agreement dictates the allocation of QBI among partners, ensuring a clear and auditable trail for the deduction.
questions.explanation: The Section 199A deduction is available to noncorporate taxpayers with qualified business income from pass-through entities, including S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates. The deduction is generally 20% of QBI, subject to limitations based on taxable income, W-2 wages paid, and the unadjusted basis of qualified property. C corporations are not eligible because they are taxed at the entity level at 21%.
questions.subtopic: Qualified Business Income Deduction for Entities

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - C corporations are not pass-through entities and their income is not considered qualified business income for the purposes of the Section 199A deduction. The deduction is specifically designed for pass-through entities.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Section 199A Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction is specifically designed for non-corporate taxpayers who receive income from pass-through entities. This includes S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates, allowing them to deduct up to 20% of their qualified business income, subject to certain limitations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While sole proprietorships are eligible, the QBI deduction is also available to owners of S corporations, partnerships, and certain trusts and estates. Limiting it to only sole proprietorships is incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While partnerships are eligible, the QBI deduction is also available to owners of S corporations, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates. Limiting it to only partnerships is incorrect.

questions.educational: The Section 199A deduction was created as part of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 to provide a tax benefit to owners of pass-through businesses, aiming to level the playing field between them and C corporations, which received a significant tax rate reduction. Understanding the nuances of QBI, qualified property, and applicable limitations is crucial for accurate tax planning and compliance for your clients.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the income thresholds and limitations associated with the Section 199A deduction, as these can significantly impact the amount of the deduction a taxpayer can claim. Remember to consider specified service trades or businesses (SSTBs) and their impact on the deduction.
questions.memoryAid: QBI = Qualified Business Income; think 'QBI for Pass-Through Pie' to remember it applies to pass-through entities.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly limit the availability of the QBI deduction to specific types of pass-through entities. A common trap is assuming it only applies to sole proprietorships or partnerships. Test-takers often incorrectly believe that C corporations are eligible or that the deduction is limited to only one type of pass-through entity, overlooking the broad applicability to S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates.

questions.question: Tom owns 30% of an S corporation, and the corporation pays $10,000 in health insurance premiums for him. Regarding the treatment of these premiums, they are:
options: Considered completely tax-free to the shareholder because they are a qualified fringe benefit, and neither the corporation nor the shareholder recognizes any income related to them.
Included in the shareholder's W-2 wages as income (subject to income tax but not FICA), then deductible by the shareholder on Schedule 1 of Form 1040
Not deductible by anyone, as health insurance premiums paid on behalf of a more than 2% S corporation shareholder are treated as a non-deductible distribution of corporate profits.
Deductible only by the corporation as a business expense on Form 1120-S, page 1, line 14, reducing the overall taxable income of the S corporation passed through to shareholders.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1372, a more-than-2% S corporation shareholder is treated like a partner for purposes of fringe benefits. Health insurance premiums paid by the S corporation must be included in the shareholder-employee's W-2 wages (Box 1 only — not subject to FICA). The shareholder may then claim the self-employed health insurance deduction on Line 17 of Schedule 1 (Form 1040), which is an above-the-line deduction. This effectively makes the premium deductible but through a different mechanism than for C corporation shareholder-employees.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Health insurance premiums paid for a more than 2% S corporation shareholder are not completely tax-free; they are included in the shareholder's income.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1372 dictates that a more-than-2% shareholder in an S corporation is treated as a partner for fringe benefit purposes. This means the health insurance premiums paid by the S corporation are included in the shareholder's W-2 wages (subject to income tax but not FICA taxes). The shareholder can then deduct the premiums as a self-employed health insurance deduction on Schedule 1 (Form 1040).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The shareholder can deduct the premiums on Schedule 1 of Form 1040 after including them in W-2 wages.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the corporation initially pays the premiums, the shareholder ultimately deducts them on their individual return, not as a reduction of corporate income.

questions.educational: The treatment of health insurance premiums for more-than-2% S corporation shareholders reflects the hybrid nature of S corporations. They are pass-through entities like partnerships, but also have some corporate characteristics. This rule aims to balance the tax benefits available to employees of C corporations with the pass-through nature of S corporations, ensuring that shareholder-employees are taxed appropriately on these benefits while still allowing a deduction.
questions.examTip: Remember that for the EA exam, the key is to understand the flow-through nature of S corporations. Health insurance premiums for more-than-2% shareholders are a common area where candidates make mistakes, so pay close attention to the W-2 inclusion and subsequent Schedule 1 deduction.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corp Health: W-2 IN, Schedule 1 OUT (Wages In, Deduction Out)
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates incorrectly assume that health insurance premiums paid by an S corporation for a more-than-2% shareholder are always tax-free. The exam exploits the misconception that S corporations are always pass-through entities for all purposes. While the income generally passes through, certain payments to shareholder-employees are treated as wages.

questions.question: Sarah is a partner in Green Acres LLC. Under the at-risk rules of IRC §465, Sarah's deductible losses from Green Acres are limited to:
options: Their capital contribution only, without regard to recourse debt for which they are personally liable or the adjusted basis of any property contributed to the LLC, even if that property is directly used in the Green Acres LLC activity.

The amount the partner has at risk — generally cash contributions, adjusted basis of property contributed, and amounts borrowed for which the partner is personally liable or has pledged property other than used in the activity

Their share of partnership income, calculated annually and without consideration for any capital losses, suspended losses from prior years, or the partner's direct investment of cash or property into the Green Acres LLC.

50% of their outside basis in the partnership, regardless of whether the partner has any personal liability for partnership debt or has pledged any personal assets as collateral for loans related to the Green Acres LLC activity.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §465, a partner may only deduct losses to the extent of the amount they have "at risk" in the activity. The at-risk amount includes: (1) cash contributed, (2) the adjusted basis of property contributed, (3) amounts borrowed for which the partner is personally liable, and (4) amounts borrowed secured by property not used in the activity. Nonrecourse loans generally do NOT increase the at-risk amount (except for qualified nonrecourse financing for real estate under IRC §465(b)(6)).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The at-risk rules consider more than just capital contributions. Recourse debt and the adjusted basis of contributed property also increase the amount at risk.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §465 limits a partner's deductible losses to the amount they have at risk in the partnership activity. This amount generally includes cash contributions, the adjusted basis of contributed property, and amounts borrowed for which the partner is personally liable or has pledged property (other than property used in the activity) as security. This option accurately reflects the components of the at-risk amount.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Partnership income does not determine the at-risk amount; it is the amount the partner could actually lose. The at-risk amount is based on contributions and certain liabilities.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The at-risk rules are not based on a percentage of the outside basis. The at-risk amount is determined by the partner's economic risk of loss, not a fixed percentage.

questions.educational: The at-risk rules, found in IRC §465, were enacted to prevent taxpayers from deducting losses exceeding their actual economic investment in an activity. This is a loss limitation rule, similar to the passive activity loss rules under IRC §469, but applies before the passive activity loss rules. Understanding both at-risk and passive activity loss rules is crucial for accurately determining a taxpayer's deductible losses from business activities.
questions.examTip: Remember that nonrecourse debt generally does *not* increase a partner's at-risk amount, with the exception of qualified nonrecourse financing secured by real property used in the activity. Pay close attention to the type of debt when analyzing at-risk scenarios on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'At Risk' as 'What you stand to lose'. It's the cash, property, and borrowed funds you're personally liable for in the business.
questions.commonMistake: Many candidates confuse the at-risk rules with the passive activity loss rules or the basis limitations. The exam exploits the misconception that a partner's deductible losses are simply limited to their capital contribution. The at-risk rules are more nuanced and consider recourse debt and pledged property.

questions.question: A C corporation that receives dividends from another domestic corporation is entitled to a dividends received deduction (DRD) of:
options: 100% regardless of ownership percentage, as long as the holding period requirement of at least 46 days is met.
50% for less than 20% ownership, 65% for 20-79% ownership, or 100% for 80% or more ownership
70% for all ownership levels, provided the recipient corporation includes the dividend income in its taxable income calculation.
No deduction is allowed unless the dividend paying corporation is a member of the same affiliated group as the recipient corporation.
questions.explanation: Under IRC §243, the DRD for dividends received from another domestic corporation is: 50% if the recipient owns less than 20% of the paying corporation (after TCJA, reduced from 70%); 65% if ownership is 20% or more but less than 80% (reduced from 80%); and 100% if ownership is 80% or more (affiliated group). The DRD may be limited to a percentage of taxable income. The DRD mitigates triple taxation of corporate earnings.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a holding period is required, the DRD is not 100% regardless of ownership. The percentage varies based on ownership levels.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §243 outlines the dividends received deduction (DRD) based on ownership percentage: 50% for less than 20% ownership, 65% for 20% to less than 80% ownership, and 100% for 80% or more ownership. This tiered approach mitigates triple taxation of corporate earnings at different levels of inter-corporate investment.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The DRD is not a flat 70% for all ownership levels. The percentage varies based on ownership levels, and 70% is not a valid DRD percentage.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A DRD is allowed even if the corporations are not part of the same affiliated group, although the percentage may be different.
